resources.string: The IP address, hostname, or URL that this connection will be sending HTTP requests to.
NOTE: This field should contain just the domain/path portion of the target URL, the resource name and any query parameters should be placed in the
field, which is appended to the path specified in the URL Path field. For example, if you wanted to send a HTTP request to the URL
in the URL Path field, and send
as the Message. The request path must be specified this way in order to ensure it is sent to a valid URL.
For HTTP (Send) connection type, the send method should be a GET or POST.
When GET is selected, the payload is appended to the URL. When POST is selected, the payload will be inserted it into the body of the request.
HTTP Connections can optionally use an authentication method to communicate with other systems' HTTP APIs that require valid user credentials in order to perform actions.
- No authentication information is added to HTTP requests sent by this connection.

- Basic authentication data is added to the Authorization header of HTTP requests sent by this connection. Basic authentication requires that a username and password are specified. This authentication option is the one that is most commonly used.
- Hashed authentication data is added to the Authorization header of HTTP requests sent by this connection. Digest authentication requires that a username, password and realm is specified.

When checked, the Inception will require the remote server to have a correctly authorized certificate when making this connection over HTTPS. Certificates are an additional layer of security to ensure that the connection is trusted and secure.
When this option is unchecked, all certificates will be accepted, including self signed and invalid certificates.
The username to use for HTTP authentication.
The password to use for HTTP authentication.
The realm to use for HTTP Digest authentication.

The key string that this connection will be identified by. When an incoming HTTP connection message is received by this Inception device, it decides which connection to pass the message to based on the key string in the URL in the
will send the message to a connection with a key string of
. The key string must be unique amongst all other HTTP receive connections, and can only contain upper/lower-case letters, numbers, the underscore character (_) and the hyphen character (-).
The HTTP Authentication method that this connection expects clients to use.
Sets the username required for HTTP Basic authentication, if enabled.
Sets the password required for HTTP Basic authentication, if enabled.
This checkbox enables and disables the Connection. This can be used to temporarily disable the Connection while the network is down or the connection target is unavailable.

When this checkbox is enabled, this Connection will attempt to resend messages that fail to send initially due to the network being down, configuration being incorrect, etc. Otherwise, messages are discarded when they fail to send. NOTE: This option only applies to
is enabled, sets the time limit for how long this Connection should keep trying to resend a single message before discarding it.
Optionally prepend a sequence of characters to the start of each sent message. These should match the start of message character sequence expected by the other system.
Special characters (e.g. carriage return, line feed) can be specified with special character sequences. Other characters can be specified with their Hex representation.
- Specifies any byte value as a 2-digit hex number between 0 (00) and 255 (FF)
Optionally append a sequence of characters to the end of each sent message. These should match the end of message character sequence expected by the other system.

Using suffixes for messages are recommended to ensure the end of a message is correctly identified. Otherwise, there is a chance that a message could be broken in half and processed separately, or multiple messages could joined together.
Specifies the expected prefix for received messages from the other system. If this field is non-empty, incoming messages will be discarded until the correct prefix is matched.
Specifies the expected suffix for received messages from the other system. If this field is non-empty, incoming messages will only be processed once the sequence of suffix characters are received.
If this checkbox is enabled, the Minimum Value and Maximum Value fields can be used to set limits for this Counter's value.

The minimum allowable value for this Counter. This value cannot be higher than the Maximum Value field. Attempting to set the Counter to a lower value will cause it to be set to this minimum value instead.

The maximum allowable value for this Counter. This value cannot be lower than the Minimum Value field. Attempting to set the Counter to a higher value will cause it to be set to this maximum value instead.
Select a Credential Template for the card that will be assigned to this user.

Informational Only: The credential template used for this mobile credential. This mobile credential template is automatically generated by the Inception system upon linking to a mobile access system, and is based off the site code that is configured during the initial linking process.
Informational Only: The card number assigned to this mobile credential. This number is generated automatically and cannot be changed.
Information Only: The user that this mobile credential is linked to.

string.name: 92D259A0F84A9F3B51B7D127ED3467DA
string.notes: Must be unique amongst all "MobileCredentialType" entries

string.name: 4695654D9A54210407E32E78A14D5CBB
string.notes: Must be unique amongst all "MobileCredentialType" entries

resources.string: Informational Only: The type of mobile access credential this is.
Informational Only: The email address used to send the last invitation code for this credential.
The duration of time that the door will be unlocked for if unlocked by a card access, PIN access, REX/REN inputs or the web interface's

The duration of time that the door will be unlocked for if the door is accessed by a user that requires a time longer than the default unlock time. The extended unlock time works on a per-user basis and requires the
option to be enabled in the
This option is used for users who require extra time to enter a door, like a delivery driver bringing in parcels or a user with a disability or impaired mobility.

The duration of time that the door's reed or tongue inputs can remain in alarm while the door is locked. The time starts counting once the door re-locks. Once this time expires, any outputs with DOTL / Held or beeper behaviour linked to this door will turn on, plus any SIFER/OSDP readers linked to the door will give feedback.
is configured, that warning timer will start, otherwise the door enters the

input for this door is set to active. This input can be monitored for reporting to a monitoring station, and with the warn time, allows the user a short period of time to close the door properly before the event is reported.
This option requires the door to be wired with a door reed or tongue sense to determine if the door is open or closed.
The Held state can be stopped by closing the door, or unlocking it.
The duration of time after the
expires that the door reed or tongue inputs can remain in alarm. Once this time expires, the door enters the
input goes into alarm. The same outputs (DOTL / held and beeper outputs) and SIFER/OSDP readers that were activated when the
Allows a time period to control the door. When the time period is valid the door will unlock and when invalid the door will lock. This feature can be used together with the

feature on this page. In this case, the door will unlock when the time period is valid and the area is disarmed. Otherwise, if the followed area is armed or the time period is invalid, the door will remain locked.

This can be used, for example, to allow free access through a door during working hours, but only once an employee has accessed the door first and hence the followed area on the other side of the door has been disarmed.

Note: If a user has overridden the state of a door to be locked or unlocked from the web interface, the override will take priority. However, when the time period changes state, the override (unless overridden to Lockout) is automatically cleared and the door defaults to normal behaviour.

Allows a user to unlock the door with a 2-badge or 3-badge action at an associated reader, and clear the override with a single or 2-badge action. The user must have override permission for the door in order to unlock or clear its override state.
This can be used to quickly unlock doors for extended periods of time without using the web interface.
- Default behaviour, multi-badge actions will not override door lock state.

string.name: FFD3B7903018D0B4A724AB683B3D9A1C
string.notes: Must be unique amongst all "FreeAccessControlType" entries

resources.string: - A valid 2-badge action will unlock the door, and a single badge action will clear the override.

string.name: EA3A35CCFCF18A212F020F6F05471CB7
string.notes: Must be unique amongst all "FreeAccessControlType" entries

resources.string: - A valid 3-badge action will unlock the door, and a 2-badge action will clear the override.
Allows the user to select when this door's override state is automatically cleared (this does not occur if the override is Lockout).

By default, doors will clear their override state when a free access time period changes or when an area arm state change occurs. This can be changed to only happen on one of these events occurring, or to never clear at all.
If enabled, causes the door to suppress the
state, which is normally triggered by the door's reed switch being opened while the door is still in the

state. This option is suited for situations where the Forced state may be displayed inappropriately during normal use, for example, when a door's locking mechanism always allows the door to be opened from the inside even while the door is in the Locked state (e.g with free egress).
Note that this does not affect the Door Forced input, which will still activate and can be used for alarm monitoring.

Pressing a REX/REN button will not unlock the door and will instead suppress the Door Forced state and will not activate the Forced input. Used for doors that can be opened from the inside without needing the lock itself to activate.

When this mode is enabled and the input activates, the lock output will not turn on and the door will not change to an unlock state. The DOTL Timer will still activate, and the door will not enter the 'Forced' state when opened.

string.name: CF8A1CFDCD58A101882DE35FE96EB693
string.notes: Must be unique amongst all "FollowAreaStateMode" entries

resources.string: Defines the area located on the outside of the door. This is used for area arming, area to follow door control and tracking the location of users.

string.name: 2183ADDA4D8D8D838A32E3FAE5C805F1
string.notes: Must be unique amongst all "FollowAreaStateMode" entries

resources.string: Defines the area located on the inside of the door. This is used for area arming, area to follow door control and tracking the location of users.
Determines how many different credential types are required to access the door from the outside. This field is used in conjuction with the
field to determine which credential types this door will accept or reject (with the default configuration, all credential types are accepted).

string.name: 4C089FD51CE964DF98467413D9683FF4
string.notes: Must be unique amongst all "AuthenticationFactor" entries

resources.string: - a single valid credential (of an accepted type) will unlock the door.

string.name: CBE40FEE0223D1A1CCA259CFFC7260BC
string.notes: Must be unique amongst all "AuthenticationFactor" entries

resources.string: - two credentials of different types (e.g. Card and PIN) are required to unlock the door. The second credential must be presented within 10 seconds of the first one.

string.name: 88E3EB28EC8847B987D5E49EC9939EAC
string.notes: Must be unique amongst all "AuthenticationFactor" entries

resources.string: - three credentials of different types (e.g. Card, PIN and Biometric - Fingerprint) are required to unlock the door. Each credential must be presented within 10 seconds of the previous one.

Determines which credential types are required to access the door from the outside. When the list is empty, all credential types are accepted; when the list contains items, only the credential types in the list are accepted, any other credentials are rejected. When a credential is presented at a reader, the received data may be processed differently based on which Credential Template it matches in the system. The
page determines what credential type the data will be processed as.
This field is used in conjuction with the
field to configure advanced access requirements for secure areas, for example, you could set up
on a door and have it accept only
type credentials. Any unique credential type in the list will count towards a valid access attempt, but repeated presentations of the same type will not. For example, when
is enabled for a Door, and a User with 2
type credentials badges both of them in succession at the reader, the second card badge will not open the door; a credential of another category is required to complete the access attempt.

string.name: CDBC895D08B5D92DB04174533A8548F7
string.notes: Must be unique amongst all "AcceptedCredentialType" entries

resources.string: Determines how many different credential types are required to access the door from the inside. This field is used in conjuction with the

Determines which credential types are required to access the door from the inside. When the list is empty, all credential types are accepted; when the list contains items, only the credential types in the list are accepted, any other credentials are rejected. When a credential is presented at a reader, the received data may be processed differently based on which Credential Template it matches in the system. The

If enabled, a user badging a card to enter a door will automatically disarm the area on the opposite side of the door from where the user is located. The user will need to have the appropriate permissions to disarm the area. This maybe convenient as it removes the need to disarm the area from an LCD Terminal using the users PIN.

This could however present a security risk. If the card was dropped outside the door and found by another person, they could enter the door and disarm the area, without requiring the additional knowledge of a PIN number.
Allows the inside or outside area to control the door. When the selected area is armed, the door will lock, however when disarmed the door will not unlock. This feature can be used together with the

option found on this page. In this case, the door will unlock when the time period is valid and the area is disarmed. Otherwise, if the followed area is armed or the time period is invalid, the door will remain locked.

Note: If a user has overridden the state of a door to be locked or unlocked from the web interface, the override will take priority. However, when the followed area changes it's arm state, the override (unless overridden to Lockout) is automatically cleared and the door defaults to normal behaviour.

If enabled, allows this door to use alternative door programming, if the assigned schedule is active. This allows some of the access options for a door to change based on the time of day. Note that if enabled, the

During the day, a user could use their Card or PIN to access a door for convenience, however after hours, the site could require that both Card and PIN are used for higher security. Similarly, access modes could differ based on weekday vs weekends and so on.

For alternate door programming to be used, a schedule must be configured. The schedule can consist of one or more trigger conditions that evaluate to True or False (e.g. when a Time Period is Active, or when an Area is Armed), which are processed in combination to calculate whether the schedule should be active or not. The schedule logic can process the conditions such that the alternate mode schedule is only active when ALL of the conditions are true, or active when ANY of the conditions are true. When the schedule's state is active, the alternate set of options below will be applied, overriding the default programming for the door.
is enabled and the state of the
condition is active, this option will be used instead of the door's
option, this option determines what credential types are required to access the door from the outside.
Alternate Outside Access Mode - Specific Credentials
Alternate Inside Access Mode - Specific Credentials

Enables anti-passback logic on this door. Anti-passback adds additional validation of user access requests that check whether the user is already in the area they are trying to access. This uses the last known location of the user and is intended to prevent tailgating, moving throughout a building without following access control proceedures or passing a card back to allow someone else access to the area. Note that the user still requires permission to access the door.

If a user always presents their access credential when accessing doors, even if a colleague is holding the door open for them, then the user's location will always be accurate and they will never be stopped by anti-passback. Violations only occur when the user ends up in a different area than what the system knows. This could be by changing areas without presenting their credential, or presenting their credential at a door but changing their mind and not going through the door for example.

Anti-passback is commonly used on sites whose policies rely on accurate user locations. This could be for time on site reports, accurate muster reports for OH&S compliance and safety, or secure locations in the building. It can also help protect resources such as a carpark, preventing access into the carpark, a card being passed back and another driver entering with the same credential.
It is recommended that doors with anti-passback logic enabled on them have a credential reader on both sides of the door to allow proper location updating.

The below configuration options can determine when the anti-passback rules apply, additional rules on what can cause a violation and how the system responds when a violation occurs. If access is denied due to the violation, forgiveness or

The anti-passback logic for this door can optionally be limited to specific times of the day. This can allow, for example, the anti-passback rules to be enforced during the day while others are present to forgive violations but to not be applied at night when a user could become locked in
If a time period is selected, the anti-passback rules will only apply when the time period is active. Otherwise, anti-passback rules will always apply.

By default, anti-passback logic only checks that the user is not currently in the area they are trying to access. If enabled, the system will also check that the user is located in the area they are exiting from. If this option is used, credential readers on both sides of the doors throughout the site are strongly recommended so user locations are correctly updated.
This allows for more advanced antipassback logic, ensuring that users always use their access credentials to move around the building.

Site security is improved with this option as a duplicated card will not work if the real owner is already on site. Similarly, access to the site through correct entry and exit points can be enforced as internal door access would not work if the users current location is outside.
This type of anti-passback does not need to be used on all doors in the system. It could be limited, for example, to a section of the site where strict control of access is required.
For users familiar with other Inner Range products, this option is similar to the
logic from Concept 4000 systems or the

When an anti-passback violation occurs a review event will be automatically logged, a system message (a blue message on the System Overview page requiring acknowledgement) will be created and optionally notifications to users can be sent.
This option determines whether the user will be denied access to the door or not due to the violation. This functionality is the difference between

If access is not denied, the user is allowed through the door even though a violation occured. However, an administrator could follow up this violation at a future time for example. This option may be used on a site to help introduce and train the users about anti-passback, generating violations without delaying the users or affecting productivity.

If enabled, the user will be denied access through the door when a violation occurs. In this case, the user will need to be granted forgiveness or amnesty by an administrator which gives them one free pass through an anti-passback door to correct their user location. Alternatively, a user could correct their location by using a non anti-passback door or with someone else opening the door for them. The
option below will also become available.

response is enabled above, the user will be denied access to the door if they violate anti-passback. To access the door, someone needs to open the door for them or they need to contact a system administrator to forgive the violation which could all take time.

This option allows a configurable amount of time to be set where forgiveness will automatically be given to the user after a violation occurs. This allows annoyance for the user who caused the violation, being stopped for 20 seconds for example, without being too inconvenient like having to report to reception or contact someone. The user may learn to always use the access control readers and other users don't need to continuously forgive violations as they occur.

Longer durations of minutes to hours could be chosen to ensure a violation is forgiven if it remains for too long. An example could be overnight, where the user violated anti-passback when leaving the building. A colleague may have let them out but the violation remains. With a long auto forgiveness, this violation will be cleared overnight and the user will be able to access the building the next morning.

resources.string: The remote hardware type should match the type of remote that this template will be linked with. The selected type will determine what options are available below. For Paradox RF system, the Inception controller supports the single button REM 101 and the four button REM1, 2 and 15 remote fobs. For Inovonics systems, all remotes are supported, however which actions are performed depend on the model of the remote.
Selecting the 1 Button Remote type will change the options below to a single response type. This single button can be used to indicate user duress, or execute a number of actions.

Selecting the Paradox 4 Button (REM1, REM2, REM15) type will change the options below to allow you to specify the main control item for the unlock and lock buttons and configure the response actions for the additional control buttons.

Selecting the Inovonics 1, 2 or 4 Button type will change the options below to allow custom actions to be assigned to up to 4 buttons. The model of the remote determine which actions are available. In addition to being just one, two or four button remotes, some two button remotes can generate a 'Button Three' press when both buttons are pushed, while others require both buttons to be pressed to transmit a single button. Please see the Inovonics documentation to determine the remote type.
Sets the area or door that will be controlled by the
If using a REM2, controlling this item or pressing the
button will cause the feedback about this item's state.
Red LED fast flash with fast beeps
Red LED On with a beep
Green LED On with a beep
- Unknown state, could not contact the RF Module
If using an IR Wireless RF remote fob, controlling this item will cause feedback about this item's state.
above is an area, then arm attempts from the remote will arm the area with

Typically, arming via a remote would be done outside of the area, causing the area to arm straight away and prevent the need to wait for exit to complete. However in some circumstances, the area state indicators are within the area, requiring the user to be in the area being armed.
Determines what action or actions to perform when Button One is pressed. This is the
button on the REM15 and REM2 remotes, or the
Determines what action or actions to perform when Button Two is pressed. This is the
Determines what action or actions to perform when both Button One and Button Two are pressed at the same time. These are the
buttons for REM15 and REM2 remotes, or the
Determines if pressing the single button on a REM101 remote fob is used for user duress or performing a custom action.
If set to user duress, the system will send a duress event to the monitoring station when the button is pressed.
If set to perform a custom action you can define the action or actions below.

resources.string: Determines what action or actions to perform when the remote button is pressed

Determines what action or actions to perform when 'Button One' is pressed. Depending on the remote type, a button combination may be needed to trigger 'Button One'. See the Inovonics remote documentation for more information

Determines what action or actions to perform when 'Button Two' is pressed. Depending on the remote type, a button combination may be needed to trigger 'Button Two'. See the Inovonics remote documentation for more information

Determines what action or actions to perform when 'Button Three' is pressed. Depending on the remote type, a button combination may be needed to trigger 'Button Three'. See the Inovonics remote documentation for more information

Determines what action or actions to perform when 'Button Four' is pressed. Depending on the remote type, a button combination may be needed to trigger 'Button Four'. See the Inovonics remote documentation for more information
Determines what action or actions to perform when the I button is pressed.
Determines what action or actions to perform when the II button is pressed.
Select a Remote Fob Template for the remote fob that will be assigned to this user. This will define what the buttons on the remote will do

is enabled, which has a message with the specific input change.

resources.string: state should be swapped with either the
- The input maintains its default behaviour according to its defined EOL configuration. This is the default value set for all newly created inputs.

- If selected, the input's alarm/active state is swapped with the sealed/inactive state. This is used when a normally-open detection device, such as a smoke detector, is wired to the system. A normally-open device reverses the End of Line resistance and will appear sealed when in alarm and in alarm when sealed.
Note: This can be used for detectors, switches and buttons.

- If selected, the input's alarm/active state is swapped with the tamper state. This option is intended for sites where the majority of inputs follow the standard EOL logic, however some sensors (fire sensors, etc.) behave differently and require that their alarm and tamper state are flipped.
Note: This option can only be used for standard EOL inputs, it cannot be used for two state inputs (switch/button) as they do not have a tamper state.

If enabled, if this input detects a Mask state based on End of Line resistance values, the system will automatically translate this to an Alarm state. This option is primarily used when two physical detectors are linked together into the one input, for example if two door reed switches on a double-door are wired into the one input. In this case, the addition of End of Line resistance from two alarm conditions could be incorrectly treated as Mask instead of Alarm.
Informational only: this is the physical location where the input is connected to in the Inception system.
Informational only: This displays the serial number of the RF input.
Informational only: This displays how the RF Input has been configured on the
page for the RF module the input is located on.

resources.string: Determines which menu is shown to the user after they log in to any LCD Terminal. This is only used for a normal login (PIN + OK), if the user logs in with an area control shortcut (PIN + ON to arm or PIN + OFF to disarm), they will be taken to the
menu automatically. Similarly, if there is an area associated with the terminal and it is in alarm, entry mode, exit mode or is about to automatically arm, then the
- The default main menu screen, showing all menu options available to the end user. This is also available by pressing on the MENU key on the LCD Terminal.
screen, allowing the areas in the system to be armed and disarmed.

string.notes: Must be unique amongst all "TerminalLoginPreference" entries

string.notes: Must be unique amongst all "TerminalLoginPreference" entries

resources.string: screen, allowing doors in the system to be unlocked and locked from the terminal.
Note: If setting to something other than the
, ensure they have permission to the menu in the
section below. Also, for the Control menus, the user is only able to control items that are assigned to the user's
Hides the greeting message that normally displays for a few seconds after user login.
Determines a user’s ability to view the

string.name: 3682D1665CF331373000C20680732D3A
string.notes: Must be unique amongst all "TerminalPermission" entries

resources.string: menu will not be accessible from any LCD Terminal.

resources.string: menu can be viewed, allowing the user to view review events.
menu can be viewed, allowing the user to change their own Security PINs.
Depending on how PINs are managed on site, allowing a user to change their own PIN may not be desirable as they could discover other user PINs
menus will not be accessible from any LCD Terminal.
menus can be viewed, allowing the user to arm and disarm areas.
Note: A user’s ability to control an area from an LCD Terminal requires that:
The area is assigned to the user’s
menu can be viewed, allowing the user to lock and unlock doors.
Note: A user’s ability to control a door from an LCD Terminal requires that:
The door is assigned to the user’s
menu can be viewed, allowing the user to control outputs.
Note: A user’s ability to control a output from an LCD Terminal requires that:
page and the output is assigned to the user’s
menu can be viewed, allowing the user to permanently isolate or de-isolate (re-enable) inputs.
menu can be viewed, allowing the user to manually send a test message by pressing the OK button.
Note: To test alarm communications you must first configure alarm reporting via the
menu can be viewed, allowing the user to identify the Inception controllers Serial Number, Firmware Version and PCB Revision.
menu can be viewed, allowing the user to enable or disable DHCP, identify the controller's IP address and manually configure network settings.
Note: This menu should only be made available to the Installer or the system administrator, as changes made in this menu may result in issues with connecting to the Inception web interface.
menu can be viewed, allowing the user to view any current system messages and remove them by acknowledging them.
If there are any system messages in the system and the area associated with the terminal is not in alarm, the user will be shown the

menu after logging into the terminal. System messages are informational, telling the user about events that happened in the past such as the reason for a recent failed area arm, a system reset or a health issue that has been restored, and just need to be acknowledged to remove them.
Limit User View to Controllable Items
If enabled, when a user visits the
menus, they will only be able to see items that they have permission to control. Often enabled for end user profiles, providing them with a simpler list of items to see.
menu can be viewed, allowing the user to enable or disable Service Mode. If service mode is enabled, it will automatically be disabled after eight hours.
Note: This menu should only be made available to the Installer as enabling service mode will disable area alarm sirens and alarm reporting.
menu can be viewed, allowing the user to change the EOL Settings for all inputs.
Note: This menu should only be made available to the Installer as changing the Input EOL Settings will risk the site's security.
menu can be viewed, allowing the user to backup and restore the system database from a USB drive.
Note: This menu should only be made available to the Installer as restoring the system database could result in lost data and requires the Inception controller to be restarted.
menu can be viewed, allowing the user to update the controller firmware from a USB drive.
To use this feature, a controller firmware file must be located on a USB drive connected to the Inception controller.
Note: This menu should only be made available to the Installer as updating the controller firmware should only be done with care.
menu can be viewed, allowing the user to secure the LAN from a terminal.
Note: This menu should only be made available to the Installer as securing the LAN will prevent module substitution.
Determines a user’s ability to bypass (isolate) hardware modules from a touchscreen terminal.
- An attempt to bypass with this user's PIN from the System Health menu will result in an Invalid PIN error. The user will be unable to force arm an area that is blocked by system health issues.
- The user will be allowed to bypass system health issues.
Determines a user’s ability to access the Settings submenu on a touchscreen terminal.
- The Settings submenu on touchscreen terminals will be disabled.
- The Settings submenu on touchscreen terminals will be enabled.
Determines a user’s ability to access the Technician submenu on a touchscreen terminal.
- The Technician submenu on touchscreen terminals will be disabled.
- The Technician submenu on touchscreen terminals will be enabled.

string.name: 5BE998D01BCC5AABAFCBCD33E5C25270
string.notes: Must be unique amongst all "EntityChangeNotifications" entries

resources.string: Specifies the readers that can control this lift car that allows a user to present either their PIN, card, or other credential. Multiple readers can be assigned to the same lift car if required, allowing readers to be mounted in different locations or be of different types.
To add a reader to the lift car, click the
button, this will open a window where you select the readers to add. If there are no readers in this window, new readers can be configured by navigating to the
Determines how many different credential types are required to access the lift car buttons. This field is used in conjuction with the
field to determine which credential types the lift car readers will accept or reject (with the default configuration, all credential types are accepted).
- a single valid credential (of an accepted type) will allow access.
- two credentials of different types (e.g. Card and PIN) are required to gain access. The second credential must be presented within 10 seconds of the first one.
- three credentials of different types (e.g. Card, PIN and Biometric - Fingerprint) are required to gain access. Each credential must be presented within 10 seconds of the previous one.

Determines which credential types are required to access the lift car's buttons. When the list is empty, all credential types are accepted; when the list contains items, only the credential types in the list are accepted, any other credentials are rejected. When a credential is presented at a reader, the received data may be processed differently based on which Credential Template it matches in the system. The
on a lift car and have it accept only
is enabled for a lift car, and a User with 2
type credentials badges both of them in succession at the reader, the second card badge will not allow access; a credential of another category is required to complete the access attempt.

Allows lift buttons to be configured for the lift car. Lift buttons represent the physical button within a lift car which when pressed will take the user to a floor. The configuration of a button that this lift car can control requires both a
for the button destination and an output that can enable and disable the button. Optionally, an input can also be specified that would indicate when the button has been pressed.
To add buttons to the lift car, click the

button, this will open a window showing all of the configured floors in the system. Once a floor has been selected, the output that can enable the button can be assigned as well as the input that provides button feedback for when the button is pressed. Additional floors can be configured by navigating to the
page and new outputs and inputs can be configured on the
Allows the configuration of a number of outputs that can provide feedback of a users interaction with the lift car.
To add feedback outputs to the lift car, click the
button, this will open a window where you select the outputs to add. New outputs can be created on the
Once added, the outputs must be assigned a
, which will determine the type of feedback that the output will show. Outputs can be configured with one of the following behaviours:
- Will turn on after a card swipe that enables lift floors and will remain on until the
expires, or a valid floor is selected via button feedback.

- Will turn on after a card swipe that does not enable any lift floors. Will also turn on when button feedback is detected for a button that isn't enabled. Once on, the output will remain on until the

The duration of time that the lift buttons will be enabled for when a user presents a valid card or PIN to a configured car reader. This duration should be set to allow the user enough time to make their floor selection after presenting their credential.
If button feedback is available, that is, inputs have been wired in to detect which lift button a user selects, then the button enable time will be cut short when they make a selection.
The duration of time that the lift buttons will be enabled for if the lift car is accessed by a user that requires a time longer than the
to make their floor selection. The extended button time works on a per-user basis and requires the
This option is used for users who require extra time to select a floor, like a delivery driver bringing in parcels or a user with a disability or impaired mobility.

If enabled, allows this lift car to use alternative access programming, if the assigned schedule is active. This allows some of the access options for a lift car to change based on the time of day. Note that if enabled, the

During the day, a user could use their Card or PIN to access a lift car for convenience, however after hours, the site could require that both Card and PIN are used for higher security. Similarly, access modes could differ based on weekday vs weekends and so on.

For alternate access mode to be used, a schedule must be configured. The schedule can consist of one or more trigger conditions that evaluate to True or False (e.g. when a Time Period is Active, or when an Area is Armed), which are processed in combination to calculate whether the schedule should be active or not. The schedule logic can process the conditions such that the alternate mode schedule is only active when ALL of the conditions are true, or active when ANY of the conditions are true. When the schedule's state is active, the alternate set of options below will be applied, overriding the default programming for the lift car.
condition is active, this option will be used instead of the lift car's normal
option, this option determines which credential types are required to access the lift car's buttons.
Alternate Reader Access Mode - Specific Credentials

string.name: 615794CB1487D307F4F4E9FB25F70081
string.notes: Must be unique amongst all "EntityChangeNotifications" entries

resources.string: Allows a time period to control the lift floor. When the time period is valid the floor will be set to free access and when invalid the floor will secure. This feature can be used together with the
feature on this page. In this case, the floor will be set to free access when the time period is valid and the
is disarmed. Otherwise, if the followed area is armed or the time period is invalid, the door will remain locked.
This can be used, for example, to allow free access to a floor during working hours, but only once an employee has accessed the floor first and hence the area on that floor has been disarmed.

Note: If a user has overridden the state of a floor to be secure or in free access from the web interface, the override will take priority. However, when the time period changes state, the override (unless overridden to Lockout) is automatically cleared and the floor defaults to normal behaviour.
Allows an area to be associated with the lift floor. This area is the one used by the
option below to ensure the floor is secure when the area is armed, possibly overriding the
If button feedback is available, that is, inputs have been wired in to detect which lift button a user selects, this is the area that will be disarmed when the user selects this floor if the
option below is enabled. Also, the user's location will be updated to this area.

Note: In a lift car, a user will be denied access to the floor if there is an associated area, the area is armed and the user does not have permission to disarm that area. This occurs even if they have access permissions to the lift floor, because it would mean they would potentially step off the lift into an armed area that they are not allowed to control. This is another way to limit access to a floor, where a user is normally allowed to access the floor when they present their credential, but only once an authorized user has accessed the area and disarmed it first.
If enabled, a user accessing this floor via a lift car will automatically disarm the

. The user will need to have the appropriate permissions to disarm the area. This maybe convenient as it removes the need to disarm the area from an LCD Terminal using the users PIN once they enter the area.

This could however present a security risk. If the card was dropped outside and found by another person, they could enter the lift car, select the floor and disarm the area, without requiring the additional knowledge of a PIN number.
Note: Button feedback within the lift car is required for this feature to work. That involves inputs being wired into the lift buttons so the Inception system knows which floor the user selected.
state to control the floor. When the area is armed, the floor will be secured, however when disarmed the floor will not be set to free access. This feature is designed to be used together with the

option found on this page. In this case, the floor will be set to free access when the time period is valid and the area is disarmed. Otherwise, if the followed area is armed or the time period is invalid, the door will remain secured.
This can be used, for example, to allow free access to a floor during working hours, but only once an employee has accessed the floor first and hence the area on that floor has been disarmed.

Note: If a user has overridden the state of a floor to be secure or in free access from the web interface, the override will take priority. However, when the associated area changes state to armed or disarmed, the override (unless overridden to Lockout) is automatically cleared and the floor defaults to normal behaviour.
This checkbox enables and disables the notifier from monitoring system events. This can be used to temporarily disable the processing without needing to delete or edit the programming.

Product Variant disables all notifiers in the system by default, in order to prevent cases such as restoring a customer's database backup on a test Inception for diagnosis and having undesired notifications being automatically sent out. This checkbox may be enabled in combination with the
checkbox in order to explicitly enable a Notifier on a IRSupport system.

Monitoring of events for this notifier can optionally be limited to specific times of the day. Limiting event monitoring can, for example, allow events to be sent at only certain times of the day, or can allow events to be sent to specific people at certain times of the day and to different people at other times.
If a time period is selected, monitoring of events for this notifier will only occur when the time period is active. Otherwise, this notifier will always be monitoring for events to send.

If enabled, this notifier will send messages to recipients for events via push notification. To receive push notifications, a recipient must have the IR Connect app installed on their smartphone and be signed in using their linked IR Connect account.
Linking an IR Connect account to an Inception user can be done on the
will show the currently linked account. The chain icon on the right of the field allows a user to be linked to an IR Connect account.
Note: To enable push notifications to the user’s smartphone, an App subscription is required, please contact your security installer for more information on setting this up.
The IR Connect app can be downloaded from the Apple App store and Google Play store.

option is enabled, this setting will determine how long Inception will attempt to resend failed push notifications generated by this Notifier (e.g. due to network failure). You can configure a longer retry time (up to 24 hours) for more important notifications to make sure they are sent through after network issues get resolved. If any notifications cannot be successfully sent within the retry time limit, they will expire and an error will be logged to the events page. Note that this retry time limit only applies to valid push notifications that fail due to network or SkyTunnel service issues; notifications that fail due to missing IR Connect user IDs or missing subscriptions will be discarded.
If enabled, this notifier will send messages to recipients for events via email. To use email as a notification path, the email server settings must be set up on the
Emails sent from this notifier will use the subject set in the

To prevent too many emails from being sent and potentially filling an inbox or having the configured SMTP server marked as a spam server, Inception will attempt to group messages together if they occur close together. Messages will be grouped only if they are being sent to the same recipient address and have the same subject. Any messages that are grouped will include the date and time they occurred within the message.

When an email is to be sent, Inception will wait 15 seconds in case any other events occur for the same recipient address and subject and will group them into a single email message. After sending, if any new messages occur for that same recipient address and with the same subject, Inception will wait for the configured

option is enabled, this will be the subject used for any emails sent via this notifier. The subject selected can be used, for example, to indicate to the recipient what type of notification is being received.
Email messages with the same subject may be grouped together (see the tooltip for the

option above for more information). To reduce excess messages from being sent, the subject can be set to the same subject used by other notifiers. Otherwise, to send messages from this notifier separately, a different subject can be entered.
Specifies which users should receive notifications from this notifier. Multiple users can be selected to be notified of events and the same user can be selected in multiple notifiers if necessary.
If this notifier is sending messages via email, the selected users must have an email address assigned to them. This is set via the
If enabled, events from all areas will be monitored to see if a notification message should be sent. Otherwise, specific areas to monitor can be selected with the
In order to notify about area events, either this option must be enabled or one or more areas must be selected in the
Filters which areas this notifier will monitor for events to specific areas. Only available if the
option above is disabled. This allows different notifiers to be created that monitor specific events for specific areas.
In order to notify about area events, one or more areas must be selected or the

resources.string: Allows users to be notified about area arm attempts. The areas that may be notified on are determined by the
- No area arm events will be sent.
- Any arm attempts that successfully arm the area will be notified. The message will include which area was armed and who armed the area (either a user, an automated action or the system)

- Any arm attempts that failed to arm the area will be notified. The message will include which user attempt the arm, which area was being armed and why the arm failed (either due to unsealed inputs or no permissions)
- Both successful arm and failed arm attempts will be notified

string.name: D0F2783DBB5CB9847A68D78919DC7C31
string.notes: Must be unique amongst all "AreaDisarmNotifications" entries

string.name: 65F11648B634A4E86350B559EFD0799F
string.notes: Must be unique amongst all "AreaDisarmNotifications" entries

string.name: 5C6D153BDEE8AE9429982FBDC39C0BE5
string.notes: Must be unique amongst all "AreaDisarmNotifications" entries

resources.string: Allows users to be notified about area disarm attempts. The areas that may be notified on are determined by the
- No area disarm events will be sent.

- Any area disarms which cancel an active alarm in the area will be notified. The message will include which area was disarmed and who disarmed the area (either a user, an automated action or the system). It will also include that the disarm was to cancel an active alarm.

- Any disarm attempts that failed to disarm the area will be notified. The message will include which user attempted the disarm and which area they were disarming. Failed disarms occur due to insufficient permissions.
- All disarm attempts will be notified, both successful and failed attempts

string.name: 054E1D071598A8EF9E798D693A2C707D
string.notes: Must be unique amongst all "AreaArmValidationNotifications" entries

string.name: A219EAD4ED4F5513FA33E27D3D0BFBD2
string.notes: Must be unique amongst all "AreaArmValidationNotifications" entries

resources.string: Didn't Arm by End of Schedule

string.name: 8DFC210BFFE796A63B87F389B83B78E4
string.notes: Must be unique amongst all "AreaArmValidationNotifications" entries

resources.string: Allows users to be notified if an area is not armed at the correct time. This covers when a user or the system arms an area at an invalid time, or when areas are not armed by a certain time. The areas that may be notified on are determined by the
To use arm validation, a time period must be set in the
- No area arm validation is performed
- A message will be sent if a successful arm attempt is made when the time period in the

option is inactive. This option allows a message to be sent if an area is armed outside of a normal schedule, for example, indicating that a shop closed early. The message will include which area was armed and who armed the area (either a user, an automated action or the system)
- A message will be sent if the area is not armed when the time period in the

option transitions from active to inactive. For example, if a store should always be armed by a certain time, a message can be sent indicating that the system has been left disarmed or that something is out of the ordinary.
- Both types of area arm validation will be processed and notified
Sets the time period for when arming of areas is allowed. See the
option above for more information about the types of validation that can be notified on.

string.name: 561F04BC9A88400577EC5E1B317D1671
string.notes: Must be unique amongst all "AreaDisarmValidationNotifications" entries

string.name: 5FA75E857387B4CEBAB15F011B35448B
string.notes: Must be unique amongst all "AreaDisarmValidationNotifications" entries

resources.string: Didn't Disarm by End of Schedule

Allows users to be notified if an area is not disarmed at the correct time. This covers when a user or the system disarms an area at an invalid time, or when areas are not disarmed by a certain time. The areas that may be notified on are determined by the
To use disarm validation, a time period must be set in the
- No area disarm validation is performed
- A message will be sent if a successful disarm attempt is made when the time period in the

option is inactive. This option allows a message to be sent if an area is disarmed outside of a normal schedule, for example, indicating that someone is potentially accessing the store when they shouldn't be. The message will include which area was disarmed and who disarmed the area (either a user or an automated action)
- A message will be sent if the area is not disarmed when the time period in the

option transitions from active to inactive. For example, if a store should always be disarmed by a certain time for the start of business, a message can be sent indicating that the system has not yet been disarmed and opened.
- Both types of area disarm validation will be processed and notified
Sets the time period for when disarming of areas is allowed. See the

Allows users to be notified when an input in an area has been isolated. This lets a user know when a section of the system is potentially not being monitored when the area was armed. The areas that may be notified on are determined by the

Temporary isolations can occur if an input was not secure when the area was armed, requiring that the input be isolated so the arm could complete. Permanent isolations are also notified, often due to an input being faulty to prevent needing to temporarily isolate the input each time the area is armed.

string.name: FA2B06E1BEEDAC10ECC3499E7A34C599
string.notes: Must be unique amongst all "InputsWhenToSend" entries

string.name: F62708131B13934950FA92671966ECC9
string.notes: Must be unique amongst all "InputsWhenToSend" entries

resources.string: Allows users to be notified when input alarm events occur in the area. The areas that may be notified on are determined by the
This option only determines how many alarm events should be sent, while the
option below determines which types of events should be monitored for.
- No area input events should be sent.
- To prevent too many messages from being generated, this option allows only the first input event that matches the types selected in the
option below to be sent. This will be reset once that area is disarmed. If multiple areas are being monitored, up to one event will be sent per area.
- Any input events that match one of the types selected in the
Allows the user to be notified when only certain types of input events occur in the area. The areas that may be notified on are determined by the
Multiple input event types can be selected for this notifier to monitor, while the
option above determines how many alarm events should be sent per area. Different inputs may report different event types, depending on the
linked to this input in the area.
- Reported when an input with input type
- Reported when an input with End of Line resistors triggers a tamper state, with any input type other than
- Reported if the input is an RF Input that triggers a low battery state, with any input type other than
- Reported if the input is an RF Input that fails to poll in to the RF receiver, with any input type other than
- European markets only. Reported when an input with End of Line resistors triggers a Mask state, indicating something is potentially covering it

Allows users to also be notified when an input event is restored. For environmental events, such as temperature or water sensors, knowing when the condition has been restored can be helpful. For standard intruder events this is less useful, as it doubles the amount of notifications received and it is more important to know when a door opened or movement was detected than when the door closed or movement stopped.
The areas that may be notified on are determined by the
Restore events will only be sent for the input types selected in the
option above. The quantity of restore events is also limited by the above
If enabled, events from all doors will be monitored to see if a notification message should be sent. Otherwise, specific doors to monitor can be selected with the
In order to notify about door events, either this option must be enabled or one or more doors must be selected in the
Filters which doors this notifier will monitor for events to specific doors. Only available if the
option above is disabled. This allows different notifiers to be created that monitor specific events for specific doors.
In order to notify about door events, one or more doors must be selected or the

string.name: 60B75AFF3AFF5CECD9E21D6E516EB0C6
string.notes: Must be unique amongst all "DoorHeldEvents" entries

string.name: C89C893C99BDFF12032C0AC465156255
string.notes: Must be unique amongst all "DoorHeldEvents" entries

string.name: 5E25DEB3421D40FF706F041A10C5B74E

resources.string: At Beginning and End of Warning
Allows users to be notified if a door has been held open too long. The doors that may be notified on are determined by the
A door being held open too long is determined by the

options of the door. After the Held time expires, a Warning phase begins to alert nearby users about the open door, often via buzzers. This warning phase allows users to close the door before the issue escalates, possibly alerting a monitoring station about the issue or sending notifications.
- No user access events will be sent.
- A notification will be sent when the Held Warning phase begins for a door. This could allow a user who should be nearby to close the door before the event gets escalated.

- A notification will be sent when the door has been held open too long and no one has closed it. This event is sent at the end of the Warning phase, or straight away if no Warning phase is configured.
- A notification will be sent when the Held Warning phase begins and ends.

string.name: 95D755E4BCDAC248FD320186E9BC8920
string.notes: Must be unique amongst all "AccessEvents" entries

resources.string: Allows users to be notified about user access attempts. The doors that may be notified on are determined by the
- Any user access attempts that are successfully granted access through the door will be notified. The message will include which door was accessed and who accessed it

- Any user access attempts that are denied access through the door will be notified. The message will include which door the attempt was made at, who made the request and why the request failed (either due to the door being locked down, the area on the other side of the door being armed or no permissions)
- Both successful and failed door access attempts will be notified
Allows users to be notified if a user has triggered an anti-passback violation at a door. The doors that may be notified on are determined by the
The event includes information about the user who caused the violation, the door it occured on, the area they were entering and the user's last known location.
Anti-passback logic must be enabled on the door for violations to occur. See the

string.name: F60A3C93D19B8B4545EA60B506129E02
string.notes: Must be unique amongst all "ScheduledDoorEvents" entries

string.name: D9279F707E79C36D09DB7AE90AABC6FF
string.notes: Must be unique amongst all "DoorOverrides" entries

string.name: 6C37CBDA8237A098B8B2A811927B1D3C
string.notes: Must be unique amongst all "DoorOverrides" entries

resources.string: Allows users to be notified when a door's state has been overridden by a user or the system. This involves, for example, overriding a door to be unlocked when it is normally locked, or locked when it is normally unlocked. It also includes locking out a door, preventing any user access attempts from working. The doors that may be notified on are determined by the
- No door override events will be sent.

- Users will be notified when the door is set into the Locked Out state. This state prevents any users from being allowed access through the door, allowing the door to be locked down if necessary. Any user access attempts that are successfully granted access through the door will be notified. The message will include which door was locked out and who locked it out

- Users will be notified of all door override events. This includes overriding the door state to Locked, Unlocked and Locked Out, as well as clearing of an override to return it to normal behaviour. The message will include which door was overridden, how it was overridden and who controlled it
If enabled, allows users to be notified if a user badges an inactive (i.e. lost/stolen) credential at a door reader.
The event includes information about the credential used, the user associated with the credential, and the door/reader at which the credential was badged.
Allows users to be notified if any module goes offline or comes back online.
A module is considered offline if it fails to contact the controller within its configured
Allows users to be notified about any power or battery issues related to any module in the system. Notification messages will be sent both when the issue occurs and when it is restored.
Module power and battery issues include any of the following:

string.name: B8EB979FA01DAE0651F5BACB17887168
string.notes: Must be unique amongst all "ModuleTroubleType" entries

resources.string: Allows users to be notified about any system tamper events on any module in the system. Notification messages will be sent both when the issue occurs and when it is restored.
System tampers are often indicators that the system could be compromised. System tamper events include any of the following:
Allows users to be notified if any module is detected as being potentially substituted.

During commissioning, when all hardware modules are configured and online, a LAN secure process is performed to identify all present modules. After that, if a module connects that was not online when the LAN secure process was performed, they are considered to be substituted. This could be part of maintenance such as replacing a damaged module, or could be compromising the site security by replacing an existing module with a fake version.
If a module substitution error occurs, a technician is needed to inspect the hardware and LAN to ensure the security of the system before performing a new LAN secure process.
Allows users to be notified if an LCD Terminal triggers its PIN Lockout mode. This could indicate that someone is attempting to guess a user's PIN to gain access to the system.
PIN Lockout is triggered when multiple failed PIN attempts occur at an LCD terminal in a row. The number of attempts required to trigger PIN lockout is configured on a per-terminal basis with the

Allows users to be notified when any health issues related to a module are overridden or isolated. Overriding of health issues causes the system to temporarily stop monitoring the module, often allowing areas to be armed.

Temporary isolation occurs if the health issue was present when an area was armed, allowing the arm to succeed. This isolation will last until the area is disarmed. Users can also permenantly isolate modules from reporting issues if there is an ongoing fault with the module, removing the need to temporarily isolate the health issue each time the area is armed.

Allows users to be notified if the Inception system resets. The notification will include the reason for the reset if it can be detected, such as power loss, firmware update, database restore and several others.
Allows users to be notified if a User Duress event occurs.
User duress events can be triggered using a number of options:
- A single button RF Fob can be configured for User Duress mode, triggering user duress when the button is pressed
page), a user duress event can be triggered by pressing the
button on the terminal three times in a row while the terminal is logged off
page), a user duress event is triggered if the duress user PIN is entered on an LCD Terminal or a keypad reader.
The message will include the name of the user if it is known or the module where the event was triggered.
Allows users to be notified when an RF Remote assigned to a user reports a low battery event. The notification will include the name of the user who owns the remote.
Low battery events are only available from Inovonics remotes.
Allows users to be notified when a user successfully logs into the Inception web interface. The notification will include the name of the user who logged on.

Allows users to be notified if Inception triggers lockout mode on the web interface. This could indicate that someone is attempting to guess a user's web login credentials to gain access to the system.

Web interface lockout is triggered when multiple failed web login attempts occur in a row. The number of attempts required to trigger web interface lockout is three, preventing any users from logging in for a short period of time. If the failed login attempts continue, the amount of time that the web interface is locked out for will increase, reducing the effectiveness of a brute-force attack.
Allows users to be notified whenever service mode is enabled or disabled in the system. The message will include which user enabled or disabled service mode.

Service mode allows features like sirens and alarm reporting to be temporarily disabled to allow maintenance of the system without having to modify programming, however if done without permission it could compromise the security of the system.
Allows users to be notified if an alarm reporting path to an alarm monitoring station has broken or has been restored.

Reporting path failures are typically also known by the monitoring station, as the reporting paths are polled. Any alarms that occur while the path is down will be queued up to be sent once the connection restores itself.

resources.string: Allows users to be notified whenever a programming change is made in the system. The message will include which user made the change, the item that was changed and whether the item was created, updated or deleted.
Multiple item types can be specified for the notifier to monitor, allowing users to be informed when settings that shouldn't be touched are modified.

If enabled, the output will become a permissible item. You can assign it directly to a user or add it to the user’s permission group. Once assigned to a user, the user will be able to control the output from the web interface via the
pages. The user can also control and output via the LCD terminal by logging in and navigating to
If enabled, the output can be controlled from the LCD terminal without needing a user to log on.
key on the terminal will display the outputs, and the
This option tries to strike a balance between security and convenience, it is not recommended to enable this option for outputs only certain people should be allowed to control.
You can restrict the terminals that provide logged off output control by disabling the
option, allowing only certain terminals to offer this functionality.
Informational only: this is the physical location where the output is connected to in the Inception system.
If enabled, the physical hardware output would be in the opposite state to the one shown in the system.
This is useful when using the
contacts of a relay output, allowing the output to be turned
in the system and show that state, but have the physical output be closed.

Permissions determine what areas, doors and outputs a user can access or control on the system. The permissions also determine what items the user can control via the web interface. Permissions are created using a
determines what the permission allows them to control and in what way, such as accessing a door or disarming an area. The
determines when this permission applies, such as
, or based on a time period, area state or door state for example.
button, this will open a window where you will select the permissible items. If this list is empty, the programming of doors, areas and outputs has not been completed.
For a user to control an output, it must be configured as a user controllable output in the
page, otherwise it will not appear in the add items list.
Each permission can be marked as
. A user must be assigned an
permission to be allowed to do something. If set to
condition is true, the user will be unable to perform the action listed in the

permissions, that is, even if allowed the same permission in other places the user would still be denied. This is used when combining permission groups together. For example, if you want this permission group to include the same permissions as one or more other permission groups, with an exception or two. Those exceptions could then be added as a

resources.string: Informational only: the door that this reader is linked to. It is possible to change the door via the
Informational only: the side of the door where this reader is located. It is possible to change the location via the
This determines the action that occurs when a user badges a card three times at the reader.
Arm Area on Other Side of Door
- this will arm the area that is located on the opposite side of the door to where this reader is located, this area is configured in the door settings on the
page. The arm request will fail if the user does not have permission to arm the area, or if there are unsealed inputs in that area.

string.name: 18200094894522E252E692B48F034C2E
string.notes: Must be unique amongst all "AreaToArm" entries

resources.string: - this will arm every area in the system that the user has permission to arm. The arm request will fail if the user does not have permission to arm any areas, or one of the areas they can control contains unsealed inputs.

If an arm request fails, the user can attempt to find which input was unsealed and fix it, or try to arm via an LCD Terminal or the web interface which will provide additional information for why the arm failed.
The SIFER/OSDP reader will provide a confirmation tone on successful arming and disarming of areas via the 3 badge action. Additional feedback for area arming can be programmed in the
Informational only: it is possible to change the reader type via the
If set, the reader lockout functionality for this reader will be disabled.

The reader lockout feature helps to protect the system from brute force attacks where multiple access cards or PIN numbers are attempted in order to impersonate a user. If multiple access cards or PIN credentials are presented at a reader that do not match a user, reader lockout is triggered. Only credentials that do not match a user are counted, attempts that are denied due to permissions do not count.

Three failed credential attempts in a row trigger a lockout on this reader which will last for 90 seconds. Once triggered, any credentials presented to the reader will be ignored until the lockout time expires. After the lockout time has expired, repeat invalid credentials will cause the 90 second lockout time to restart.

Editing and saving of a User within the system resets the invalid credential count for all readers and clears reader lockout if it has been triggered. This allows cards to be assigned to users by swiping an unknown card at a reader, then assigning that unknown card swipe to a user, without worrying about triggering reader lockout.
Informational only: this is the module that the reader is connected to.

resources.string: Allows the selection of the the type of Wiegand keypad. The type selected will determine how information about PIN key presses are interpreted.
key on the keypad to be used to control an area's state (often the # key or the ON/OFF key on an Inner Range Weatherproof keypad.

, the key will function as normal, requesting access to the door for the PIN that was entered. Otherwise, the key will use the PIN entered to try to control the area instead of requesting access to the door. How the area is controlled will depend on the current state of the area. If the area is armed (including when entry or exit delay is active), an alarm is currently active or the area is about to arm, the system will attempt to disarm the area. Otherwise, the system will attempt to arm the area. The user who entered the PIN must also have the correct permissions for these actions to succeed.

resources.string: If enabled, the SIFER/OSDP reader will provide feedback for the following states:
- If the door is held open for too long the reader will make a beeping sound until the door reed or tongue sense are sealed. DOTL functionality is configured in the
- When a user without permission to access the door badges a card, the SIFER reader will make a single beep and the Big LED will flash red.
- When the door is unlocked by any method the SIFER reader will make a single beep and the Big LED will flash green.
If enabled, the SIFER/OSDP reader will provide feedback for the area located on the same side of the door as the reader. Feedback is provided for the following area states:
- When the area is successfully armed, the SIFER reader will sound the Area Arm Success tone and the Big LED will flash green.
- When the area fails to arm, either due to permissions or unsealed inputs, the SIFER reader will sound the Area Arm Failure tone and the Big LED will flash red.
- When there is an active alarm in the area, the SIFER reader will sound the Area Alarm tone and the Small LED will fast flash red until the area is disarmed.
- When the area's entry delay mode is active, the SIFER reader will sound the Entry Delay tone and the Small LED will flash green for the duration of the entry delay.
- When the area's exit delay mode is active, the SIFER reader will sound the Exit Delay tone and the Small LED will flash green for the duration of the exit delay.

- When the area is about to automatically arm, the SIFER reader will sound the Area Auto Arm Warning tone and the Small LED will flash orange until the area is automatically armed or manually disarmed.

Note: The states above are prioritised by the system in the order they are listed. If the SIFER reader is configured to provide area state feedback for the areas on both sides of the door, both areas are tested and the area state with the higher priority will be provided.
If enabled, the SIFER/OSDP reader will provide feedback for the area located on the other side of the door to the reader. Feedback is provided for the following area states:

This determines the purpose of the reader's status LED. For SIFER readers, this is the small LED, for other OSDP readers, this is the only LED. This LED can be used to display a static colour or the door or area state.
- the status LED will be the colour selected in the LED colour field on this page.

- the status LED will turn red if the door is locked and will turn green when the door is unlocked. The status LED will also flash red if the user badges a card and is denied access to the door. Selecting this option will override the
field for SIFER readers, or the
field for OSDP readers on this page.
Area state, same side & other side

- the status LED will turn red if the area is armed and will turn green when the area is disarmed. This can be used to display the state of the area on either the same side as the reader or the opposite side. The status LED will flash green when a user is granted access to the door and will flash red if the user is denied access to the door. Selecting this option will override the
key ([#] key) on the SIFER/OSDP keypad to be used to control an area's state.
Informational only: it is possible to change hardware assigned via the
page. This is the identification number of the SIFER/IR reader, this number is printed on the rear of the reader.
Sets the volume of the tones generated by the SIFER reader. There are numerous actions that will generate a beeping tone such as card access, area control etc.
Sets the brightness of the SIFER readers big and small led.
Sets the colour of the big LED.
Sets the colour of the small LED. Only applies if the above
page. This is the OSDP Bus address of the OSDP reader, and is what the reader uses to identify itself with the module.
Sets the colour of the LED.
The calculated EAC number of the Aperio wireless lock.
This EAC can be found within the Aperio Programming Application, assigned by the lock's parent hub to the lock based off the hub's own EAC and the lock number.
