resources.string: Enables the periodic sync of the system time using NTP (network time protocol)
Enter the NTP server(s) to synchronise the system time with. If using more than one NTP server, use spaces to separate multiple servers.

If an internet connection is not available for the Inception controller, or the specified NTP servers can not be reached, specify a local NTP server. This ensures the Inception system time is accurate, events have the correct time stamp, and any scheduled tasks or permissions occur at the correct time.
Sets the system's timezone that the controller is located in. The time zone is used as the offset for GMT that is kept in sync by the NTP server.
In addition, Daylight Savings adjustments are automatically handled by the timezone configuration.
Note: changes to the time zone may take up to a minute to take effect.
Whether or not the connection to Mobile Access system is enabled. When disabled, warnings related to mobile access will not be generated, and new mobile credentials are unable to be allocated.
The URL to the Mobile Access server. This shouldn't ever need to change, and is only exposed on Beta variants.
The default URL for the Mobile Access server is: https://mobileaccess.innerrange.cloud/
Informational Only: The site code that all mobile credentials generated by this system will use. A corresponding card template is created using this site code, and used by the mobile credentials.
This is set during initial configuration of the Mobile Access connection, and cannot be changed afterwards without unlinking and relinking the Mobile Access connection.
Informational Only: The name of the Mobile Access system that has been linked to the Inception system. This can only be changed via the Mobile Access Portal.

This disables Dynamic Host Configuration Protocol (DHCP) which is a server protocol that automatically provides the controller with its IP address. This option should be selected if there is no DHCP server on the connected network or the Inception controller requires a specific IP address.
Selecting the use static IP option will make the IP address, subnet mask, default gateway and DNS server options below editable.
An IP address is a unique string of numbers separated by full stops that identifies each device using the internet protocol to communicate over a network.
disabled: an informational only field that displays the automatically assigned IP address.
enabled: this field is used to manually enter the static IP address.
The subnet mask is a binary pattern that is stored in the controller. It is matched with the IP address of a packet to determine which network segment the packet is destined for.
disabled: an informational only field that displays the subnet mask.
enabled: this field is used to manually enter the subnet mask.
The default gateway is the router used to forward all traffic that is not addressed to a device within the local network or local subnet.
disabled: an informational only field that displays the default gateway.
enabled: this field is used to manually enter the default gateway.
DNS server is short for domain name system, this is an internet service that translates domain names into IP addresses.
disabled: an informational only field that displays the DNS server.
enabled: this field is used to manually enter the DNS server.
Informational only: the physical address of the controllers network interface. Commonly required by on site it to allocate an IP to the device.
Enables the use of Wi-Fi as a method to connect to the Inception web interface via the controller. This option will only work with the Inception Wireless Adapter.
If you aren't connecting Inception to a local WiFi network, it is recommended to still leave this option enabled and set to

string.name: A0BEA2BC35C57BFF3FE0192451E50880
string.notes: Must be unique amongst all "WifiConnectionType" entries

resources.string: . Then, during maintenance, a wireless adapter can be connected temporarily to allow an to easy connection to the controller without touching the on-site network.
Displays the IP address assigned to the controller when connected to a Wi-Fi network. Wi-Fi allows DHCP only, so to fix the IP address of the controller the MAC address shown below should be used.
Displays the subnet mask assigned to the controller when connected to a Wi-Fi network.
Displays the default gateway assigned to the controller when connected to a Wi-Fi network.
DNS server is short for domain name system, this is an internet service that translates domain names into IP addresses.
Informational only: the physical address of the WiFi Adapter. Commonly required by the on site IT department to allocate an IP to the device.

Enables remote access to the web interface over the internet via Skytunnel. Skytunnel is a hassle free connection method that allows any device with internet access connect to an Inception Controller.
To connect to your Inception Controller, open a web browser and navigate to
Note: Since the web interface will be accessible over the internet it is recommended that you use secure usernames and passwords.
Sets the Domain Name or IP Address of the SkyTunnel server to connect to.
Sets the Port used to communicate with the SkyTunnel server.
Sets the Domain Name or IP Address of the KeyPoint server to connect to.

By default, Cross-Origin Resource Sharing (CORS) is disabled on Inception devices. If the Inception REST API is accessed via cross-origin requests, the origin address to allow through can be provided here.
When enabled, Inception will redirect all standard HTTP requests to HTTPS for increased security of web traffic.

string.name: 2985F4ADD68997617FABE96B924AFF68
string.notes: Must be unique amongst all "WifiConnectionType" entries

string.name: 6ADCC3A42308FD1B63F6B20AA7ECE900
string.notes: Must be unique amongst all "WifiConnectionType" entries

resources.string: This selects the mode of operation for the Inception Wi-Fi Adapter.

- Allows a pc or mobile device to connect via Wi-Fi directly to the Inception controller without connecting to the on-site network. Note: This is intended for easy connection during installation and maintenance of the system and is not recommended as a permanent configuration option.

- Allows the Inception controller to connect to a wireless network using WPA-personal authentication. Once connected, a pc or mobile device on the same network can be used to configure the Inception controller.

- Allows the Inception controller to connect to a wireless network using WPA-enterprise authentication. Once connected, a pc or mobile device on the same network can be used to configure the Inception controller.

The SSID (service set identifier) is the name assigned to a Wi-Fi network. This is used to configure the Inception controller's wireless hotspot name, this name will be broadcast to all wireless devices.
This defines the password that is used when a device attempts to connect to the wireless hotspot. This password must be atleast 8 characters long and can be a combination of letters and numbers.

The name of the wireless network the Inception controller should attempt to connect to. The name can be manually entered, or optionally the Detect SSID button can be used to detect nearby Wi-Fi networks and select one from a list.
Note: the Detect SSID feature only works when the
option is enabled and the connection mode is not Hotspot. Ensure these settings are correct and saved to the system before attempting to detect networks.
The shared password used to authenticate the connection to the wireless network.
The username to use when connecting to the wireless network.
The user-based password to authenticate the connection to the wireless network.

Allows the name of the system to be configured. This name will be shown on the login page and in the footer of every page. This can be helpful in identifying the system you are working with if interacting with multiple Inception interfaces at once.
Sets the region of the Inception system, enabling region specific functionality (such as Europe specific GSM devices and reporting settings).

Sets the end of line resistance scheme that is used by the system to determine the state of detector inputs. The default setting is 2k2 + 2k2 or 2k2 + 6k8 which supports the resistors supplied with the Inception controller and the Inner Range expansion modules. If you are using a different End of Line resistor configuration, select the one that you are using for the site.

The End of Line configuration such as 2k2 + 6k8 determines the resistance values for the seal and alarm states. The first number 2k2 is the resistance that should represent a sealed input. The sum of both numbers 2k2 + 6k8 = 9k is the resistance for an input in alarm.

If the resistance scheme required is not available, Inner Range may be able to supply an upgrade file for a fee to allow your Inception system to support the custom scheme. Pricing and availability is subject to Inner Range discretion

Determines the duration of time to delay reporting of module AC Fail events to monitoring stations, or to users via notifications. If the AC supply is restored before this delay expires then no message will be sent.

Backup batteries should be fitted to the system to ensure the system can run without an AC supply for an extended period of time. AC supply to the system could be removed for a short period of time, for example, due to a brief power outage or brownout to the site or building maintenance that requires power to be removed for a short period of time. In these cases, power is restored quickly with little drain on the batteries, so the delay prevents the unnecessary notification to the monitoring station of the event. This in turn removes the need for the monitoring station to action the event and follow their response plan of informing the client for the short outage.
, no delay will be used and an AC Fail event will be sent as soon as one is detected.

If enabled, modules and alarm reporting paths will also be tested for any issues when an area arm attempt is made. If a user requested the arm, the attempt would fail if any issues are present and the user would be shown the reason for the failure and given the option to temporarily isolate them and try again. Alternatively, if the system requested the arm, the issues will be temporarily isolated (in the same way as inputs are temporarily isolated) and the arm will succeed. Any health issues isolated by the user or the system in this way will be automatically deisolated when the area disarms.

This ensures that users are made aware of health issues that could compromise the security of the system when securing the premises, allowing them to either fix the issue or make the decision to temporarily override the issue.
The system only monitors module health items that have been configured via the
page. If a health issue will persist for several days, the module can be permanently isolated via the

page. Similar to permanent isolation of inputs, this isolation will persist until a user manually removes it. Alternatively, if the issue will persist for longer or the system should not be monitoring the module for that issue, the module should be edited via the

If a module has its health issues isolated, the system will ignore health issues raised by that module until it is deisolated. Any isolations of health issues are also reported to the monitoring station, ensuring that any potential compromise to the security of the system is known.
If testing of system health is desired on some areas but not others, each area can be configured to ignore health issues via the

If enabled, area arm requests from users located off-site will include an Arm Warning phase prior to arming. This allows some time for someone to leave or cancel the arm if they are still on site. With this option configured, a remote
request will ignore the Exit Delay part and only enter the Arm Warning phase, arming the area when the phase completes.
A user arm request is considered remote or off-site if it comes from the web interface (i.e the
page), the IR Connect App, or any REST API integrations that indicate the request is remote.
Arm requests from an LCD terminal, an RF fob, a Reader action, or API integration with a system like a home automation system are all considered on-site.
Determines the duration of time after a module goes offline before the Inception controller updates the state of the modules inputs and peripheral devices.

Rarely, a LAN module may drop offline and re-register itself, coming back online often within one second. This could be caused by LAN signal interference or other factors. In the event that this occurs, this module fail time allows a buffer where the module can restore itself. If the time expires without the module coming back online, the module will be treated as failed and will have the state of its inputs updated and any peripheral devices attached to it set to offline.
It is recommended that you choose a value equal to or greater than your longest module poll time (1 minute by default), as this minimises false detections where the module thinks it is still online.
Input states are updated to either an
Set Inputs to Tamper on Module Fail
If enabled, the detector inputs for the module will be set to the
state if the module is offline longer than the
option above. Otherwise, the detector inputs will be set to an

The above failure time is designed to filter out brief LAN comms issues, but if the module remains offline for longer than the configured time then this option determines how the system responds. In this case the detector inputs on the module can not be known to the controller. This could be due to power loss or a lan cabling issue, or it could be due to an intruder intentionally interfering with the LAN.

state of the inputs may cause area alarms depending on the input processing for those inputs, which will sound sirens and generate an alarm event that will be sent to a monitoring station if alarm reporting is configured.

state is a secure state that won't generate an alarm or prevent area arming. A module offline event will still be sent to the monitoring station if alarm reporting is enabled, as well as messages indicating the presence of an offline module will be displayed in the Inception web interface and on LCD terminals.

Note: Enabling this option creates a more secure system, where inputs on modules that have gone offline are treated as if they have been tampered with and are compromised. This could however cause undesirable behaviour (siren responses and alarms being sent to the monitoring station) if sections of the LAN are unreliable
Prevent Siren Retrigger from Same Source

If enabled, an input or module will only be able to trigger the sirens once for an area per arm cycle. Only the retriggering of sirens will be prevented, subsequent activations will still cause alarm events to be reported to a monitoring station if configured.

This option is used for a number of scenarios. If an input is faulty and repeatedly activates, or if it is externally accessible and an intruder can easily activate it, then repeated siren activations could annoy neighbours, cause people to eventually ignore the siren or potentially use up the siren activation limit of the area. With this option enabled however, subsequent activations of an input or module health issue would still be reported to the monitoring station, but the sirens won’t activate. Then, if a second input activates, the sirens will be able to sound and attract attention to the potential break in.
This functionality is also a requirement of AS2201.1 and should be enabled if meeting this standard is desired.

Allows the internal system functionality to be configured with a certain compliance standard in mind. Depending on the option chosen, internal processing logic may change or additional features may become available which are required by the selected standard.
By hiding away functionality that is specific to the compliance modes, it allows the system to remain simpler and easier to use when those standards are not required.

- No additional specific compliance features will be enabled. This is the default option and should be selected for almost all installations, unless compliance with one of the listed standards is required at the site.

string.name: 72DFD085201CA945172F7AF0F219BB4D
string.notes: Must be unique amongst all "ComplianceModes" entries

resources.string: - Adds additional processing logic to support the EN50131 standard used in many locations throughout Europe. This adds many things to the system, most of which are internal to the system, while some additional options and features are shown. For example, changes to area alarm processing logic are included, additional Review Events and rules to how they are saved, rules applying to

(installer technicians) are applied and many others. These features, along with additional options and validation, modify the Inception from the original system used in countries around the world to allow compliance with standards required by a number of European countries.

string.name: E0915E94955913AD1CFD2C6FD98852CD
string.notes: Must be unique amongst all "ComplianceModes" entries

resources.string: - BS8243 is in general an extension of the EN50131 standard. With this option, in addition to the features and processing logic added to support the EN50131 standard, the extra features and logic required by the BS8243 standard are enabled. On top of the existing EN50131 enhancements, the BS8243 standard primarily adds Confirmation logic to area alarm processing to differentiate between unconfirmed and confirmed intruder and hold up alarm events. Anti-code functionality is also included, allowing the ARC / monitoring station / alarm company to provide a level 2 user with a code to temporarily let them perform installer level actions.

Determines the amount of time that can pass after an unconfirmed intruder alarm occurs before the system is reinstated. To be compliant with the BS8243 standard, a time between 30 minutes and 1 hour must be selected. However, shorter times are available to aid with testing.

Determines the amount of time that can pass after an unconfirmed hold up/duress alarm occurs before the system is reinstated. To be compliant with the BS8243 standard, a time between 8 hours and 20 hours must be selected. However, shorter times are available to aid with testing.

Allows areas to have their confirmation logic grouped together, letting unconfirmed alarms from one area confirm alarms in other areas of the group. This affects both Intruder confirmation and Hold Up confirmation logic.

Inception allows multiple security areas to be defined, letting individual areas be armed and disarmed to provide more flexibility on site design and use. Certain sections of the site can be secured, such as server rooms and other critical areas, while others are freely accessible.

When the BS8243 standard is selected, each area will automatically enable alarm confirmation logic, allowing alarms and other triggers to generate unconfirmed alarms and confirmed alarms in those areas. However, each area is isolated, with alarms in one area not affecting the other areas which may be desirable in some circumstances and not desirable in others when it comes to confirmation logic.

By grouping areas together, it allows an unconfirmed alarm in one area to trigger, then if another unconfirmed alarm occurs in another area within the same group, both areas will confirm their alarms. This allows for a multi-floor building to be able to have individual areas for each floor that can be armed separately, but if an intruder triggers one detector on the first floor and another on the second floor, confirmed alarm events would be activated, instead of multiple unconfirmed alarms. The alternative would be to have one large area covering the whole building, with the only option to have the building either armed or not.

Disarming an area to reinstate it (which restores any unconfirmed or confirmed alarms and stops any confirmation timers), will remove those alarms from the group. For areas that confirmed their unconfirmed alarm based on this other area's alarms, if there are no other alarms in the group, then those areas may restore their confirmed alarm, reverting back to just an unconfirmed alarm and waiting for a second activation.
Multiple groups can be created by selecting which areas should be grouped with the
button, then selecting which confirm group they should belong to.
Sets the minimum number of digits that can be used for user PIN numbers. This number is also used when PIN numbers are automatically generated in the
Sets the maximum number of digits that can be used for user PIN numbers. This number is also used when PIN numbers are automatically generated in the
Note, if increasing PIN length limits, LCD Terminals can accept at most 8 digit PINs and the
Sets the minimum number of characters required when creating or changing user web profile passwords.

Specifies the minimum requirements for a user's created or changed password. By default, there are no minimum requirements. Selecting numeric characters as a requirement means users will need to put a number in their password. Alphabetic characters as a requirement means any letter. Symbols means users require a non-number non-alphabetical character in their password.
Sets the number of attempts any particular user may make before being locked out of login for a duration specified by the

setting, default 6 attempts. If the user is unknown or erroneous (their username is not found) and does not enter their username correctly after 3 attempts, instead a default lockout will be used for a minute and a half independent of settings.

Setting this value to 0 will disable lockouts, meaning that any individual user may attempt as many times to login, but the default lockout will still be present for any unknown or erroneous usernames.
Sets the duration of a lockout when a user exceeds the number of attempts specified by the
setting. A lockout time of None will set all lockouts to last indefinitely until intervention by another user with the ability to control users - to disable lockout, the
setting must be set to zero.
Entering an unrecognised username in 3 different attempts is distinct and will use the default lockout time.

Specifies the amount of time between mandatory password changes. When a user has not changed their password in the specified timespan, they will not be able to access other pages until they change their password. By default, the user is never required to change their password unless it does not meet the specifications in the
This option determines whether the system's local IP address is shown on the login page.

While it is useful for installers and system administrators to easily access this IP address from the login page, potential malicious third parties could use this knowledge to target other internal systems on the network.
Disallows users from using a PIN to login, removing the 'Switch to PIN Entry' option on the login page and preventing PINs from being used to authenticate with API requests.

If enabled, allows configuration of Two-Factor Authentication (2FA) for users, providing an added level of security when they log into the Inception web interface by requiring them to enter an external code generated by an app running on a smartphone or similar device. The
option below determines which 2FA system will be used for authenticating user login. Users can be enrolled for 2FA on the

The Two-Factor Authentication (2FA) system that the Inception web interface will utilise for user login. Users can be enrolled for 2FA on a per-user basis from the Manage Users page. For added security, 2FA can also be made compulsory for specific Web Page Profiles, requiring new logged-in users to enrol for 2FA before they are allowed to access the rest of the web interface.

string.name: E9DD1BFA336E4E7674653BA00C24BE5F
string.notes: Must be unique amongst all "TwoFactorAuthMode" entries

resources.string: - Configures the controller to request 6-digit Time-based One-time Password (TOTP) codes from users in addition to their username and password. These login codes can be generated by the Google Authenticator mobile app or another TOTP-compatible 2FA app. TOTP codes allow users to log in using 2FA even while the controller or user device cannot connect to the internet.
NOTE: Changing the 2FA method will delete all existing 2FA enrolment data for users and require them to be re-enrolled.

If enabled, the system will have the duress user functionality. This enables a user that is under duress to type in the duress PIN number instead of their own. If the duress user has permission (configured below) they will be granted access as usual, however the system will generate a silent duress alarm event that will be reported to the monitoring station. This event will not generate area sirens or LCD messages.
Sets the PIN number that is used to trigger a duress event from an LCD terminal or keypad reader. This PIN number uses the minimum and maximum PIN lengths values specified on this page in the
section and must be unique compared to all other users in the system.

Sets the permissions provided to a user when triggering a duress event from an LCD Terminal or keypad reader. It is recommended that some permissions are assigned to make it appear that an actual user has logged in, however, it is not recommended that high risk doors and areas are assigned as it would be a PIN number known to many users.

Sets which terminal profile will be applied if the duress user PIN is used to log in to an LCD Terminal. It is recommended that a profile is selected that allows the user access to menus that they would need when under duress and to appear that an actual user logged in. However, the profile shouldn't let them access items like system information, network settings or installer menus as it would be a PIN number known to many users.
Determines how often the Battery Test will run. The frequency is based on a weekly scale range, you can select which day of the week and the what time of the day below.
It is recommended to run the Battery Test regularly to ensure that the in the event of AC failure, each module can be powered from its attached battery.

Once the battery testing is triggered, all modules with a power supply connected, battery connected and battery testing enabled will be added to a queue for testing. Modules are tested one at a time so that if there is an AC fail mid test, only part of the system is vulnerable, not all of it.
For a module to be included in the battery test it will need to have a power supply connected, battery connected and battery testing enabled on the
page. The duration of the battery test is set on a per module basis, on the
page. The length of the battery test will depend on the average current consumption of the module and the capacity of the connected battery.

If a battery issue is detected during a battery test, the monitoring station will be notified about it and a warning will appear on the web interface. A module with a failed battery test state must pass a battery test to clear it. This can happen with the next scheduled test, or a test can be manually run on the

Battery tests can abort for a number of reasons, a user can cancel the test, AC was lost on the power supply, a wire was shorted, comms to the module was lost or the module needed to sound sirens. An aborted test is neither a pass or fail and nothing is reported to the monitoring station.
Determines what day of the week the Battery Test will run.
Determines what hour and minute of the day the Battery Test will run.

By default, SIFER readers will be able to read SIFER credentials and ECOLITE credentials. The Multi-Format version of the SIFER reader will also be capable of reading the CSN data of various third party 13.56Mhz credentials.

With this option enabled, the readers will be limited to reading SIFER credentials only. There may be a slight performance improvement with this option as the reader only needs to read one format instead of testing multiple formats. It also offers slightly higher reader security as only the high security SIFER credentials are accepted.

Inception requires a LAN Encryption Key in order to communicate with Ethernet LAN modules on the network. You can either use the default encryption key, or specify a custom passphrase or key to use. All Ethernet LAN modules in your system must be configured with the same encryption key as the controller in order for them to be able to communicate.

If the LAN Encryption Key is changed while Ethernet LAN modules are connected to Inception, Inception will attempt to migrate modules from the old key to the new one. If some modules are failing to connect after changing the LAN encryption key, use the
toolbox button to manually migrate modules from their old key to the new one.

string.name: 7A1920D61156ABC05A60135AEFE8BC67
string.notes: Must be unique amongst all "LANEncryptionTypes" entries

resources.string: - The default LAN encryption key will be used.

string.name: 7D9A6D091A1AC8739E205280F092DB99
string.notes: Must be unique amongst all "LANEncryptionTypes" entries

resources.string: - Allows a passphrase to specified for the LAN encryption key.

string.name: 897356954C2CD3D41B221E3F24F99BBA
string.notes: Must be unique amongst all "LANEncryptionTypes" entries

resources.string: - Allows a 32 character hexadecimal key (0-9, A-Z) to be specified for the LAN encryption key.
The passphrase that will be used to derive the LAN Encryption Key for communicating with Ethernet LAN modules.

The hexadecimal key that will be used as the LAN Encryption Key for communicating with Ethernet LAN modules. The key must be exactly 32 characters long and can only contain the characters 0-9, A-Z and a-z.

By default, any Intelligent LAN Access Module that is connected will have all entities that can be stored for offline functionality synchronised upon initial connection and any relevant entity changes.

Not all details can be synchronised with full compatibility to the ILAM offline database (such as non-Time Period permissions), so a warning is generated whenever something synchronised does not fully match the Inception system.
With this option enabled, all warnings generated related to ILAM offline database incompatibilities with anything in the Inception system will be suppressed, and will no longer appear.

Inception requires an email server to be configured in order to send email notifications. Currently, an SMTP server is the only option for sending emails, however in future there will be other server options.
Once a server is configured, test emails can be sent using the
option in the toolbox at the top of this page.
- No email server is configured for the system. Inception will not be able to send email messages.

string.name: 2E97C522FEA3FF212E662A50235BFDF5
string.notes: Must be unique amongst all "EmailSMTPType" entries

resources.string: - Allows an SMTP server to be configured for the system to use. This will require settings like the host address and port number to be configured, along with a username and password if required.

A custom logo image can be uploaded to the Inception system for branding purposes, for example: if an installer wishes to display a company logo in place of the Inception logo on the login screen and on the navigation bar. The custom logo also replaces the Inception logo in PDF reports generated from the
An image can be uploaded by dragging and dropping the image file directly onto the image upload area (if using a PC), or by clicking the upload area and selecting the image that you want to upload.
If the image field is left blank, the default Inception logo will appear on all web pages as normal.
The image's dimensions and aspect ratio should be equivalent to the default Inception logo image (500 x 100px, or 5:1) for optimal quality.

This field sets a custom text message that will be shown to users on the login screen when they log into Inception via the web interface. An installer can use this field to display information such as important phone numbers, email addresses,
Enables the Dynamic User Import Module (DUIM) service for this Inception controller.
The DUIM service periodically scans a network share folder and imports CSV files containing user data in the same format as the one specified by the

Successfully imported files are deleted from the network share folder after the user data is imported. Files that fail to import are moved into a user-specified subfolder, or deleted if no subfolder is given. If the DUIM service fails to import a file, a system message will be created with the details of the error to help identify why the import failed, so the file can be amended and reimported if required.
The full network share folder path that the DUIM service will monitor for new user data CSV files, e.g.
. NOTE: The network share location must already exist and allow full read/write access, otherwise the DUIM service may not function correctly.
Specifies the password to use for network/domain authentication.
Network Share Subfolder for Import Failures (optional)

Optionally specifies the subfolder where files that fail to import should be moved to. If no subfolder is specified, the files will be deleted instead. The folder must exist and be writable in order for the DUIM service to function correctly.
NOTE: This field specifies a subfolder of the Network Share Location, not the absolute path. For example, if the Network Share Location is
, failed imports will be moved to
Determines how often the DUIM service should check for new files to import from the network share path.
Optionally determines which files the DUIM service should process or ignore based on whether they match the mask.
The wildcard character (*) can be used to match patterns in filenames, e.g.
will only match .csv files starting with
If no filename mask is specified, the default mask
The Credential Template that will be used when importing any users from CSV files. A template must be selected if you are importing users with Credential data.
The Fob Template that will be used when importing any users from CSV files. A template must be selected if you are importing users with Fob credential data.
page, columns that do not correspond to a user property and are not mapped in the
in the DUIM settings will normally prevent the import of that .csv file.
Enabling this option will allow for .csv files to be imported regardless of whether unmapped columns are found or not, and no error or system message will occur.

Specifies the user properties that will have a mapped alternative column header name for imported user data CSV files, whether imported automatically through DUIM or manually through user configuration at

When a user property is selected, a custom column name can be entered which will be treated as the original user property when imported. For example, the 'Name' user property could be mapped to the alternate column name 'Full Name' so that any data in the CSV file under 'Full Name' will be imported into the user's 'Name' property.
Select the user properties that will have an alternate column name under CSV imports.

When a user property is selected, a custom column name can be entered which will be treated as the original user property. During the import process, user property data under that alternate column name will be imported as if it was for the original user property.

resources.string: As messaging is grouped on both recipient address and subject, a longer rate limit time can be selected to allow common messages to be sent as only a few emails, while higher priority messages can be sent to a different address or with a different subject.
Rate limiting can be disabled by setting the value to
, however if too many emails are sent too quickly then external systems may treat the configured SMTP server as suspicious and mark future emails as spam.
The email address that is used when sending email notifications from this system. However, depending on the SMTP server used, this address may be overridden by the server account.
The address must be in a valid format such as jsmith@example.com
The name that will appear as
the email came from when this system sends an email message.
The address of the mail server you wish to use for sending emails. This would be the outgoing address of the mail server, such as smtp.gmail.com if using Gmail.
You may need to contact your service provider to learn the server address.
The port used to connect to the mail server. This is often port 465 or 587 if SSL/TLS is required, or port 25 otherwise.
You may need to contact your service provider to learn the server port.
If enabled, Inception will connect to the mail server with an encrypted connection. This should be enabled if the mail server you are connecting to requires or supports a secure connection
If enabled, Inception will use the username and password specified in the
fields below to authenticate with the mail server.
The username that Inception will use to authenticate with the mail server if authentication is required. This may be in the form of an email address such as jsmith@example.com
The password that Inception will use to authenticate with the mail server if authentication is required.
Please refer to your Azure App Registration
The Email user name of the service account. This can be different from your From address
The client secret, please refer to the Certificate & Secret section of your Azure App Registration

resources.string: The following {0} modules have failed battery tests.

This could indicate that the batteries backuping up these modules are no longer capable of powering them when the main power supply is lost, either because the capacity has fallen over time or because the amount of power needed has increased. It could also be because the main supply was lost for a time prior to the test, causing the batteries to be tested when not fully charged.

Check the batteries to verify their capacity is still suitable for the modules they must support and replace if necessary. To clear the failed test state the test must pass. Battery tests can be run manually from the

resources.string: The following {0} inputs have failed a soak test.
Inputs in Soak Test are isolated from the system and will not report any alarms.

To pass a Soak Test, an input must not generate a reportable alarm for the duration of the test. If no alarm is generated, the input will automatically return to normal operation at the end of the test. Otherwise, if the test fails, the input will remain in the failed soak test state until someone de-isolates the input.
To stop a Soak Test early, or to remove the failed soak test state from an input, the input must be de-isolated via the

This page is used to view live and historical events that are created by the Inception Controller. These events can be displayed, filtered and exported.

Review Events are a live and historical log of every event that has occurred on the Inception System. Review events are created every time a scheduled event occurs, a user interacts with a system or even when a programming change is made.
When this page is opened the last 24 hours of events are shown (up to a maximum of 500), new events are dynamically added to the top of the list. If you want to load more events clicking the
button will load an additional 500 events. Clicking the
button will load every event stored on the Inception controller. This is not recommended as displaying this number of events may have an impact on the pages performance.
The displayed events can be filtered to make it easier to find particular events. The
fields are used to filter events to certain dates and times, which is useful if you know what time the event you are looking for occurred. The
field will filter events based on the text you enter. The
filter is used to show or hide events that belong to a particular category, for example, if you were looking for door access events it would be recommended to only show the
The displayed events can be exported as a PDF or CSV file to be printed or used in a different application.
If an incident occurred 1 week ago and you wanted to review the events, click the
button until the events from a week ago are displayed. If there are too many events displayed to find the incident, reduce the number by using one or more filters.
This page is used to configure the users that interact with the Inception Controller and the Inception web interface. This includes their credentials and user permissions.

A user is any person that interacts with the Inception System by either badging a credential, entering a pin number or logging into the web interface. Every user will have one or more credentials and some permissions that determine how and what they can interact with in the system. This page is used to create or manage users, assign credentials and RF Fobs and assign permissions for the Inception Controller and web interface.

Create or select an existing user and assign the credentials required for the user to interact with the system. Credentials are what identify a user and must be unique between all users. Credentials include the PIN number used to request at access keypad readers or log in to the LCD terminal, cards or biometric data for access control and RF remotes for remote control. Also included are the credentials required to log on to the web interface, both via the local network and over the internet via SkyTunnel (if enabled). Web access requires a username, password and a profile assigned, which determines which pages the user can view and how they can interact with those pages.
Permissions determine what areas, doors and outputs a user can access or control on the system and when they are allowed to control them. Permissions are assigned in one of two ways:
Individual items are assigned to each user, this offers a lot of flexibility, however, does not scale very well.

Permission Groups containing many permissions can be created to suit particular roles in a business. Many users who share the same role, for example, all the warehouse staff, could share the same permission group. This option scales well for larger sites.
Individual permissions can also be marked as Deny, allowing
. Even if a permission group gives them permission to something, the denied permission would override that.
If there are many users that need to be created, you can import multiple users from a CSV file by clicking the

button. The CSV import can create users from data supplied in the CSV file. CSV import can map user data from columns representing any of the data fields visible on the Manage Users page, for example, a CSV file could contain a list of column names like
Name, Notes, Security PIN, Credentials, Email Address, User Expiry Time
Users can also be exported to CSV which includes some basic information about them. You can select the columns you wish to be included by clicking the

button to bring up the export dialog. As user updates can also be performed with the Import option, this CSV uses the same column names as the Import CSV format. This allows the users to be exported, some fields changed and then the file re-imported to perform updates to many users at once. Note that the Password and PIN columns will never be populated in the export and are only used to match with import format. Additionally, if the
columns are exported, only the first credential or fob number assigned to a user will be exported due to CSV format limitations. Similarly, if the
column is exported, only the user's first assigned Permission Group will be exported.
Depending on your web page profile you may only be able to view users and not modify them.
It is important to identify how a user should be allowed to interact with the system and only give them permission to do the things they need to do, but nothing more.
This page is used to configure Permission Groups which are a collection of permissions that can be assigned to one or more users that have similar roles.

Permissions can be assigned to users in one of two ways, the first method is to add individual items such as a doors, areas or outputs directly to a user. This method does offer a lot of flexibility however does not scale very well once the site has more than a few users. The preferred method for assigning user’s permission is a permission group. A permission group is a collection of permission rules that can be assigned to multiple users.

Alternatively, you can use a combination of these two methods by assigning the user one or more permission groups based on their role and then allowing or denying additional items on a per user basis. The benefit of this is that it reduces the overall number of permission groups required, with the core permissions that a number of users share forming a permission group and any exceptions to that rule being applied to the individual users. Permission groups can also contain additional permission groups, which may be used when a role in the company requires the same permissions as another, as well as additional permissions. For example, warehouse managers could have a permission group created including the warehouse staff permission group, as well as the other permissions required as a manager. Multiple permission groups could also be combined together in this way.

Permission groups are generally created to suit particular roles on a site such as warehouse staff. The Warehouse Staff permission group would contain all the permissions required for a warehouse worker to access the doors and control the areas and outputs for them to do their job. If a new person was hired in the warehouse, you would simply assign them the Warehouse Staff permission group. The other benefit of using a permission group such as Warehouse Staff, is that if a new door was installed in the warehouse, this door would only need to be added to the permission group once rather than adding it individually to ten warehouse staff members.
Depending on your web page profile you may only be able to view permission groups and not modify them.

When creating permission groups it is recommended to first identify what is common between a number of users, this will generally be role based such as IT Staff, Admin Staff etc. However on some sites it may simply be different level of access such as Basic Access or Management Access. The Basic Access permission group would contain all the doors, areas and outputs needed for every user, you would then provide additional permissions or permission groups to users that have other access requirements.
It is recommended that you clearly name the permission groups based on the role to allow them to be assign to users without needing to look at their contents.
This page is used to create and edit credential templates.

Credential templates are used when assigning credentials (e.g. cards, biometric data) to users. The credential template contains information such as the credential type, the site code and the card format of the cards, if applicable.

field is used by the Inception system to distinguish between different categories of credential data when it is received from a card reader, a fingerprint/iris scanner, or another source. The type of the credential template is taken into account when users attempt to access a door with Dual or Triple Credential Authentication enabled, for example, Dual Credential Authentication requires that at least 2 unique types of credentials are presented at a door reader (e.g. Card type and Fingerprint type) before access is granted.

The site code is a unique facility code that is contained within the data on card type credentials, the site code acts as a second authentication for the credential number. Cards for a site are generally purchased with a single site code, however, multiple purchases of cards can lead to one site using multiple site codes. The card format determines how the data from the card that is badged at a reader is interpreted. This will determine what part of the card data is the credential number and what part of the data is the site code.

When assigning a credential to a user, the first step is to select the credential template. This instructs the system how to interpret that data and what site code to expect. Once a credential template has been added to the user, the credential number is typed in.

If the cards on site use more than one card format or more than one site code a credential template will need to be created for each. Alternatively if the site is only using the raw data from the cards the card format would be set to raw (direct entry).
Without a correctly programmed credential template, no user will be able to access a door.
This page is used by the installer during the initial installation and ongoing maintenance of the Inception system.
This page is used to create and edit remote fob templates.

The Inception Controller supports the use of RF remotes with either the Concept Paradox RF Expander or the Inovonics RF Expander. This will provide users the ability to remotely control a door, area or output from the press of a button.

Remote fob templates are used when assigning remote fobs to users. The remote fob template contains the type of remote and describes what each button on the remote will do. When assigning a remote fob to a user, the remote fob template is selected and the serial number of the remote fob is typed in. This allows multiple users to be given remotes that have the same button behaviour and if any behaviour changes are required it can be edited in one place. Multiple remote fob templates can also be created if different users require different button behaviours.
The Inception System supports the following remotes:
The REM101 is a Paradox single button remote that can be used as a duress pendant or to perform automation.

The REM1, REM2 and REM15 are Paradox four button remotes that can be configured with the main control function using the open and closed padlock buttons. These buttons are used to change the state of a door or area. The unlock button could be used to disarm an area and the lock button to arm. The 2 remaining buttons can be configured to perform one of 3 different actions.
The Inovonics remotes allow custom actions to be configured for each of the buttons on the remote.
Without a correctly programmed remote fob template, the buttons on the remote fob will not do anything.
This page is used to create, edit and control areas.

An area is a collection of inputs that are grouped by their location or purpose. Inputs are assigned to areas and linked with an input type (process group) that determines how the system treats and reacts to the input state changes. Depending on the input type, especially Intruder related ones, certain input state changes may only be processed when the area is armed. The process group determines if sirens or strobes are activated and which alarm reporting message to send, if any.

A user will often arm and disarm an area from an LCD Terminal within that area. This means that the user needs an entry/exit path between the main point of entrance and the LCD Terminal. This is where the detectors along that path won't report alarms or sound sirens during a short entry or exit delay time.

The area can be configured to activate sirens and strobes. The 8 Input Expander can be configured as a Siren Device to drive an internal and external 8-ohm sirens. The Siren Device generates different siren tones based on the process group assigned to the input. In addition to alarm tones, the sirens can also be used to indicate when the area successfully arms, fails to arm, or is about to automatically arm. You can also configure standard outputs as
You can configure an area to automatically arm based on an
. This ensures that rarely used areas are armed more often without needing user interaction, or that areas aren't accidentally left disarmed overnight.

When this page is first opened the list of areas will display the state of each area. As the area changes state the area list dynamically updates. The area state can easily be identified by the text description and colour. The areas can be controlled by clicking the
button in the toolbar, this will open a window that contains control options and a progress display.
Your ability to view and edit areas from this page is based on your Web Page Profile. Your ability to control areas is based on your User Permissions.
This page is used by the installer during the initial installation and ongoing maintenance of the Inception System. Most users will control areas from the
This page is used to easily create multiple areas.
This page allows you to quickly create every area needed for your site. The area creation is done separately from the area configuration to aid the
process, allowing the areas to be created early. This allows the areas to be used on the
page to assign areas to doors and LCD Terminals.
This page is used to easily map inputs to areas.

This page simplifies the process of adding inputs to areas. This is done by adding the inputs to one of four categories. Three of these categories are related to Intruder Detection and will automatically assign the correct Process Group. This ensures that the inputs are processed correctly and the area functions as expected.
You can select the area you wish to map inputs to on the left-hand side, once selected you can define inputs in one of two categories, the

Entry/exit path inputs are specialised inputs that are located on the primary entry/exit path into the building. These create safe paths from the primary point of entry/exit to an LCD Terminal inside the area that allows area control. This allows for a brief period of time where the area can be entered while armed with no sirens sounding to give a chance to disarm, or armed without sirens sounding to give a chance to exit. Identify any inputs that are on the entry/exit path. For the input(s) that will be triggered first when entering the area, add them to the
section. All other inputs that may trigger after the first inputs have triggered along the entry/exit path, assign them to the
section. The system will automatically assign in the appropriate process group.
All other intrusion related inputs that are monitored by this area are to be added to the
Finally, if this area monitors any inputs not related to intrusion such as smoke detectors or duress buttons, these inputs are to be added to the
section where you can specify a process group your choice.
Once all the inputs that are monitored by this area have been mapped click the
Your ability to view and edit input mapping for an area from this page is based on your webpage profile.
This page is commonly used by the installer during the initial installation and ongoing maintenance of the Inception System.
This page is used to test area strobes and sirens.

This page allows quick and easy testing of the strobe and sirens linked to each area to verify they are connected and configured correctly. The manual control options can aid in diagnosing complicated issues as it can confirm whether the siren and strobe configuration within each area is correct.
Select the area you wish to test from the area list and click the

button based on the item to test. It is important to remember that there are local regulations that determine the duration of time that a siren can be turned on for, this control will not automatically turn the siren off after a certain period of time.
If the strobe or siren is not functioning correctly navigate to the
This page is used to control when doors should automatically lock and unlock, when areas should automatically arm and when lift floors should be set to free access.
The Scheduler is a simple interface for updating door free access times, auto-arming of areas based on a time period and lift floor free access times.
To configure door free access times, select a time period from the
drop-down selection, when the time period is valid the door will unlock and when invalid the door will lock. Alternatively, you can select an area from the
drop-down selection, when the area is armed the door will lock. This feature can be used together with the

option. In this case, the door will unlock when the time period is valid and the followed area is disarmed. Otherwise, if the followed area is armed or the time period is invalid, the door will remain locked.

This can be used, for example, to allow free access through a door during working hours, but only once an employee has entered accessed the door first and hence the followed area on the other side of the door has been disarmed.
To configure area auto-arm select a time period from the

drop-down selection. The area will automatically arm when the selected time period becomes active. This can be used, for example, to automatically arm the area late at night. This ensures that the area is armed overnight in case a user forgets to arm it. By default, the area will not automatically disarm when the time period goes invalid, however this can be changed with the

Permissions for this page can be provided to a user without them requiring the ability to edit areas or doors. This allows a user to be able to update these properties for doors and areas as required while minimising the chance of important door or area configuration settings being changed accidentally.
To configure lift floor free access times, select a time from the
drop-down selection, when the time period is valid the floor will be set to free access and when invalid the floor will secure. You can also set the
option for the floor. If set, the floor will be set to free access when the time period is valid and the
is disarmed. Otherwise, if the followed area is armed or the time period is invalid, the floor will remain secured.
This page is used to easily set outputs as user controllable outputs so that they can be controlled directly by users, either via the Inception web interface or LCD Terminals.
For a user to be able to control an output, the output must be flagged as user controllable. This option is located on the

pages and can be enabled a single output at a time. This page provides a single location where multiple outputs can be set as user controllable outputs at once. In addition to configuring output as user controllable this page can also configure the output as logged off controllable, allowing the output to be controlled from LCD Terminals without logging in. The logged off control menu is accessed by pressing the
arrow key on the LCD Terminal.

button will launch a window that displays all of the currently configured outputs, from this window you can select one or many outputs. Once configured as a user controllable output the output will be displayed when assigning permissions to the user. Provided the user is assigned the output in their permissions they will be able to control the output via the LCD terminal or the web interface.
Your ability to view and edit outputs from this page is based on your webpage profile.
This page is used by the Installer during the initial installation and ongoing maintenance of the Inception system.
This page is used to configure web page profiles. Web page profiles determine the level of functionality a user has on the Inception web interface.

The web page profile determines which pages a user is able to view on the web interface and what options are available on those pages. Their control of items will be based on the permissions assigned to them.
This page is used to create and edit web page profiles which are then assigned to the user on the
page. Once the user is provided with a username, password and web page profile they will be able to login to the web interface.

can be configured on a per item type basis to allow the user to view the page and related pages and edit the items on it, view the page but not modify items, or not see the page at all. All edit-type pages in the Inception system will appear in this section.
can be configured on a per item type basis to allow the user to view the state of and in some cases control items. Every monitor-type page in the Inception system will appear in this section.

are configured on a per item basis to allow the user to perform administration related tasks such as backing up the database, restore the database, updating firmware and enabling service mode. These tasks are not dependent on the user permissions and only require the execute option to be selected to enable this functionality.

will determine what is displayed for the user on the homepage of the Inception web interface. The dashboard content provides options to show shortcuts to common pages grouped by the type of action. For a shortcut to appear on the dashboard, the web profile also needs to include the correct permission to the page the shortcut relates to.

The web profile can also include a list of areas, doors and outputs that will be shown in the favourite items section of the users dashboard, located on the top of the page. The favourites section allows the user to view the state of and control any items added to without having to navigate to the state/control page.

It is recommended that when creating or modifying profiles, the profile should only be able to view and interact with the pages that are actually required, with the rest being hidden away. This results in an uncluttered and easy to use interface with unimportant items for that user hidden away.
This page is used to configure terminal profiles. Terminal profiles determine the level of functionality a user has on the LCD Terminal.

The terminal profiles determine which menus a user can view on the an LCD Terminal. The user can only control the areas, outputs and doors that their permissions allow them to, even if the profile lets them see the control menus.
This page is used to create and edit terminal profiles which are then assigned to the user on the
page. Once the user is provided a
and a terminal profile they will be able to login to an LCD Terminal.
section can be configured to allow or deny the user access to commonly used menus on the LCD Terminal.
section can be configured to allow or deny the user access to menus that are usually restricted to the Installer.
Note: The options found in the Installer menu can compromise the sites security.
It is recommended that when creating or modifying terminal profiles, the profile should only provide the user with menus that are required, with the rest being hidden.
This page is used to control, edit and view the state of inputs. Inputs are created on the
This page is not used for the creation of inputs, inputs are created and named when a hardware module is configured on the
page. This page is used for the renaming and configuration of the input and also provides you state and control of the inputs.
When naming an input, it is important to use a common naming convention across all inputs to ensure the easy identification of the input purpose and location. Inputs can be configured with the
option if the input is a normally open detection device such as a smoke detector, as opposed to normally closed detection device such as a PIR.

To edit an input click the input from the list and make the necessary changes and click the save button. The input list will also provide you the state of the input this is indicated by a text field that appears in a particular colour. Once the input is selected and can be controlled by clicking the control button this allows you to isolate or de-isolate the input. If an input is isolated, the area that monitors the input will not process the inputs state changes until the input is de-isolated.
Your ability to view and edit inputs from this page is based on your webpage profile.

This page is used to edit calculated inputs. Calculated inputs are virtual/logical inputs that can be used as real hardware inputs throughout the system, but their active/inactive state is controlled by the system itself.

A calculated input is an input that has no physical location or mechanism and exists only within the Inception Controller's logic. The calculated input and the state that it represents can be used in the system as a normal input, being added to areas and assigned an input type, triggering reportable alarm events and activating area sirens. With no physical hardware, the state of the input is instead controlled by the system.

Calculated inputs are created automatically under certain circumstances. For example, when a door is created in the system and is assigned a reed or tongue sense input, the Inception controller knows whether that door is open or closed. Two calculated inputs are automatically generated, one that represents when that door is forced (the door opened while locked) and one that represents when that door is open too long (held open past the held time limit and held warning time). These inputs can then be added to areas for monitoring and reporting those events.

The Inception system controls the state of calculated inputs automatically, based on the state of other things in the system. This allows scenarios that would normally require one or more automated actions or complex workarounds to be easily monitored and reported on. For example, the door forced calculated input will only activate if a door opens while in the locked state. This replaces techniques in some other systems where they monitor the reed or tongue input directly and regularly isolate and de-isolate the input on every door unlock.
This page is used to configure calculated inputs such as changing their name and also provides you with the state of the input and the ability to isolate it.
Your ability to view and edit inputs from this page is based on your web page profile.
This page is used by the installer during the initial installation and ongoing maintenance of the Inception System.

This page is used to control and edit custom inputs. Custom inputs are virtual/logical inputs that can be used as real hardware inputs throughout the system, but their active/inactive state is instead controlled by automated actions.

A custom input is an input that has no physical location or mechanism and exists only within the Inception Controller's logic. The custom input and the state that it represents can be used in the system as a normal input, being added to areas and assigned an input type, triggering reportable alarm events and activating area sirens. With no physical hardware, the state of the input is instead controlled by automated actions. These actions could be triggered by any state changes within the system, or by a 3rd party system via the 3rd Party Automation Interface, allowing reportable and actionable alarm events to be generated from other systems.

While this feature allows 3rd party system to create reportable alarm events, it should not be used as the method for reporting critical alarms such as fire alarms. It is recommended that a low-level connection is used between the systems involving physical inputs, outputs and end of line resistors for reporting any critical alarms.
This page is used to create and configure custom inputs and also provides you with the state of the input and the ability to isolate it.
To add a new custom input click the
button. These inputs can be assigned a name, but beyond that are just simple inputs that can be either active or inactive.
Your ability to view and edit outputs from this page is based on your web page profile.
Your ability to control inputs from this page is based on your user permissions.
This page is used to control, edit and view the state of outputs. Outputs are created on the
This page is not used for the creation of outputs, outputs are created and named when a hardware module is configured on the
page. This page is used for the renaming and configuration of the outputs and also provides you state and control of the outputs.
When naming an output, it is important to use a common naming convention across all outputs to ensure the easy identification of the output purpose and location. The output can be configured as

, this will allow a user to control the output via the web interface or by logging into the LCD Terminals provided the output is assigned to their user permissions. An output can also be configured as
to allow the output to be controlled from LCD Terminals without logging in. The logged off control menu is accessed by pressing the

To edit an output, click the output from the list and make the necessary changes and click the save button. The output list will also provide you the state of the output, this is indicated by a text field that appears in a particular colour. Once the output is selected it can be controlled by clicking the control button this allows you to turn on or turn off the output.
Your ability to control outputs from this page is based on your user permissions.

This page is used to control and edit custom outputs. Custom outputs are virtual/logical outputs that are primarily used in automation as a state holder that is controlled without a physical output activating.

A custom output is an output that has no physical location or mechanism and exists only within the Inception Controller's logic. The custom output and the state that it represents can then be used to trigger Automated Actions. When programming automation, you may need to use a custom item to trigger some automation or to hold the state of some automation. This could be an output turning on when an input goes into alarm or needing something that is user controllable. It is not recommended to use a physical output as something could eventually be connected to the output or the relay may activate thousands of times a day.
This page is used to create and configure custom outputs and also provides you with the state of the output and the ability to control it.
To add a new custom output click the
button. Much like a normal output, a custom output can have a name and be configured as
. A user controlled custom output could be used to manually control a number of automation responses, or reset the current state of the automation.
This page is used to create and edit automated actions to allow additional automation functionality on the Inception system.

The Inception system contains options for many of the commonly used automatic responses of intruder and access control systems. For any custom functionality outside of the core features, automated actions can be used. These would most often be simple automation cases such as turning on the lights when the area is disarmed, but have the flexibility to handle very complicated scenarios.

The automated action is configured in two parts; the trigger condition and the action responses. The trigger condition determines when this automated action is true or false. When the trigger condition changes from false to true, a set of actions can be automatically executed. Similarly, when the trigger condition changes from true to false, a different set of actions can be automatically executed.

You may want the Automated Actions to do the opposite of each other, for example, when the trigger condition is true an output turns on and when false the output turns off. Alternatively, you may only want to setup a single action.
There are no default automated actions, you can add an additional automated action by clicking
This page should only be used by the installer during the setup or ongoing maintenance of the system. Your web page profile will determine if you can view or view and edit the automated actions.
This page is used to control and edit doors. Use the Hardware page to add new Doors to your system

An access control door is any electronically controlled obstruction, such as a hinged door, auto door, roller door or boom gate, that restricts a user accessing an area. The door and door related hardware are connected to the Inception Controller or Standard LAN Access Modules (SLAM).
The door is made up of a number of components, including:

- This is the output that controls the electronic lock, when a door is unlocked the output will turn on. The door can be unlocked and locked by a user requesting access with a card or PIN, the web interface, or by pressing a REX (Request to Exit) button. The door can also be configured to automatically unlock and lock based on schedules or the state of the area on one side of the door.

- SIFER/OSDP or Wiegand readers are installed on one side or both sides of a door. The readers provide the controller with card or PIN information to identify a user who is requesting access. If they have permission to access the door, the door can unlock.

inputs are usually wired to buttons or detectors that, if pressed, will cause the door to briefly unlock. Often placed on the side of the door where access control is not restricted, allowing the door to unlock to exit the door without presenting a credential or creating a door forced alarm.
- These inputs allow the system to know if the door is open/unsecured or closed securely. This can be used with the door's lock state to identify DOTL (Door Open Too Long) or Door Forced states.

- DOTL (Door Open Too Long) is a logical state for a door which occurs when the door has been unlocked and opened and the system has tried to lock the door, however the door has not been closed within the
configured on this page. The DOTL input goes into the alarm state once the configured
expire without the door closing securely. Once triggered, the DOTL input seals when the door is closed.

- Door Forced is a logical state for a door which occurs when a door is opened without the door being unlocked first. When this state occurs the door forced input will go into alarm. Once triggered, the Door Forced input will seal when the door closes or is unlocked.

If doors are configured with inside and outside areas, numerous features can be used. User locations can update with access requests, the door can follow the area state to prevent unlocking when the area is armed, readers linked to the door can provide audible and visual feedback for area state changes and the readers can also be used to arm the area.
The door is created and configured in the
page. This page provides the same options as the

page, as well as some additional advanced options to configure the operation of the door. In addition to naming and configuring the doors, this page provides the state and control of doors, allowing you to see live state changes to any configuration changes you make. If your web page profile and user permissions allow, you can click on the control button to unlock, lock, override and grant access to the selected door.
This page is used by the Installer during the initial installation and ongoing maintenance of the Inception system.
This page is used to edit readers. Readers must be configured from the
page in order for them to appear on this page.

The reader is the access point that a user interacts with to unlock a door. Depending on the door there may be a reader on one side or on both sides of the door. The reader is usually configured and associated with a door in the configuration hardware menu. This page provides additional reader specific options to configure the operation of the reader.

A large number of options on this page are not editable as they are determined by the configuration hardware menu. The options displayed on this page will vary based on the type of reader connected, the common option across all readers is a three badge action. The three badge action is the action that will occur when a user presents a card three times in quick succession to the reader. This action can be set to arm the area on the other side of the door to where this reader is located, or arm all of the areas in the system. Please note that if the
Arm the Area on the Other Side of the Door
option is selected the door programming requires an area to be specified.

When using SIFER or OSDP readers, they can be configured to provide door or area feedback, including showing state information via the status LED. For SIFER readers this is the small LED, for OSDP readers it is the main LED. SIFER readers also include options to change the volume of the beeps produced by the SIFER reader and the brightness of the LEDs or the colour to display to customize the appearance if not showing state feedback.
This page is used by the Installer during the initial installation and ongoing maintenance of the Inception system.
This page is used to enrol and configure hardware modules.

The hardware page displays all the unconfigured and configured modules on Inception System. A hardware module is any expansion module that connects to the Inception controller by the RS-485 LAN to provide additional inputs, outputs, doors or readers. For the devices to work correctly they must first be configured.

When configuring a module, you will be stepped through multiple sections of programming. These steps will depend on the type of module that is being configured, for example, a reader module will have reader and door programming options as well as inputs and outputs, however, an input expander will only have options for inputs and outputs.
A module can be configured by selecting it and clicking the
button or by double-clicking the module. A module is considered to be configured once all of the steps are completed and the

In addition to configuring modules that are currently attached to the Inception Controller, this page can be used to create and configure new hardware that will be attached in the future. Clicking the
button will open a window where you are able to select the module type you wish to add.
This page does not dynamically update as modules are attached. If you have added new hardware to the Inception Controller while the page is open click the
What you able to do on this page will depend on your web page profile, in some cases, you may only be able to view the hardware modules and not edit them.
This page is used to create and edit lift floors.

A lift floor is a destination that a button in a lift car could take a user to. A lift floor can either span multiple lift cars or be limited to a single lift car. A floor that spans multiple lift cars usually contains an area or facilities that are required by many users such as a basement carpark. When a lift floor is only accessed by a single lift car it is usally beacause the lift car serves a particular purpose, such as a service lift or one that provides access to restricted levels.

Users are granted permission to access lift floors, so that when they present a card or PIN at a reader within a lift car, the buttons that would take them to the floors they can access would be enabled by Inception. Lift floors can also be in one of three states,

. A floor in free access will cause all of the lift cars that can access that floor to enable the button for that floor, allowing any user to select that floor without needing to present a card. A secured floor requires a card to be presented in a lift car before a button linked to the floor would be enabled. A locked out floor will prevent a button linked to the floor from enabling, even if a user with permission to the floor presents their card.
Lift floors are secured by default, however a time period and an area can be configured to schedule when free access to a floor is allowed. When the time period assigned to the
