is valid, the floor will be in free access and if invalid, the floor will be secure. This can be used with the
option, causing the floor to be in free access only if the time period is valid and the
is disarmed, otherwise the floor is secure. This can allow, for example, free access to a floor during working hours but only once an employee has entered the area and disarmed the area.

There doesn’t need to be one lift floor created per building floor though, in some cases there may be multiple lift floors created for the same physical floor in a building, for example if the lift car has two doors and there are different buttons for 7A and 7B. Two lift floors can then be created with different areas and permissions assigned to each side of the floor. Alternatively, if a particular lift car needs its access to be restricted to only certain users, for example a service elevator, there could be normal floors created for the other cars, along with another set of service floors for the service lift car, with only maintenance workers having permission to those floors.

You need only create lift floors for the levels in a building where you wish to restrict users access. If there are lift car buttons and floors that Inception does not need to control access to, then they do not require a lift floor.

If using the lift integration functionality for a reason other than lift car control, such as lockers, garages etc, then lift floors could be created to represent the inside of the locker or parking spot, or whatever destination that is being protected by an output. Users could then be given permission to access that lift floor.
This page is used to create and edit lift cars.

A lift car represents a physical lift car or button panel that the user interacts with. Lift cars require hardware outputs in the Inception system to be wired to the lift button panel so they can enable and disable whether the lift button works. Inputs can also be wired into the button to indicate when the button has been pressed. Using this information and ability in addition to a credential reader, allows Inception to control which floor buttons a user is allowed to press when they present their card, biometric, or PIN credential.
Lift cars are made up of a number of components, including:

- Multiple readers can be configured for a single lift car, along with the number of credential types that are required for a user to be identified (Single, Dual or Triple Credential Authentication). This allows, for example, readers to be mounted at different heights for accessibility, multiple readers to be used for different credential types or a separate reader and keypad for card and PIN entry.
- Represents each physical lift button that Inception is able to control. Requires both a
for the button's destination and the output that has been wired into the lift button. Optionally, an input can be specified that would indicate when the button has been pressed.
- These are the outputs that can be used to give visual or audible feedback to the user in the lift. A number of outputs can be specified along with how they should behave.
- Configures how long to enable the buttons for once a user presents their credential. Includes an optional extended enable time if the user presenting their credential has the
The inputs, outputs and readers required by the lift car must be created separately using the

A lift car should be created for the physical button panel that it controls. This could mean, for example, that a destination panel in the lobby would also be treated as a lift car. Also, if there are two readers and two button panels within the same lift car, then two separate lift car items should be created so the button panels can be controlled individually.

If the lift integration functionality is being used for a reason other than lift car control, such as lockers, garages etc, then lift cars would link the reader of the system to the outputs that are being controlled. The readers would still be where the user presents their credential, however a button would instead represent a locker door or a garage roller door that can be controlled by an output. The lift floor would be the inside of the locker or the parking spot and the users could be given permission to access that lift floor.
This page is used to create and edit storage units.

To protect the storage units, they can contain motion or door position detectors to monitor unauthorized access to the space. If unauthorized access is detected, the unit enters an alarm state which can sound sirens and be reported to a monitoring station.
To monitor a storage unit, when a unit is created, a
is also created to represent if that unit is in alarm. Instead of monitoring the detector inputs directly by the
, this calculated input can be monitored instead. When assigned to an area, it is recommended that this special input is assigned the

input type, which will cause local sirens if that area is disarmed and sirens and a reported alarm event if that area is armed. This allows staff to control whether alarm events should be reported for the unit alarms, for example, leaving the area disarmed while staff are on site during the day, and arming at night for after-hours customer access.
state will monitor its inputs and enter an alarm state when they activate. If
, then the unit can be accessed and the alarm state will not occur. Any outputs selected in the
option will also turn on when the unit unlocks, which could be used to control unit lighting, a door lock, etc. If a unit is not being rented, it can be marked as
allowing free access to the unit.
The unit will enter a Tamper state if any of the inputs within the unit are tampered with.
[Configuration > Access Control > Storage Unit > Storage Blocks]
page) can be used to group units together and provide additional functionality for them. Typically this represents a set of units, or the entire facility. When a User presents a credential at the
for a block, their unit can be automatically unlocked. Similary, when they leave the block via an
, their unit is automatically secured. Blocks provide a common programming location for their units, allowing an
time to be configured which alerts when a unit has been unlocked for too long, and an
time that lets a unit re-secure automatically after its door has been closed.
Users are granted permission to control storage units, letting them unlock and secure the unit and control the vacancy state. In addition, if a user has

permission to a unit, it will automatically unlock as they enter the Block and automatically secure when they leave. The granular permissions allow customers to be able to conveniently access the unit or units they are renting, while allowing staff users full control over all the units.

User permissions to units and storage unit state can both be managed via the REST API, allowing integrations to automatically create users and give them permission to units, as well as controlling those units and their vacancy.
This page is used to create and edit storage blocks.

A storage block represents a group of storage units. This could represent an entire site with 100s of units, a single building of units in a multi-building site, a single section of a building, and so on. There can be as many blocks as required in a system.
Storage blocks are made up of a number of components, including:

- Multiple readers can be configured for a block's entry. These could be dedicated readers or the readers that control existing access control points like an entry gate, the entry door of a building, or so on. A user presenting a credential at one of these readers will cause any Units that they have
permission to control to unlock, if that unit is in this block.

- Multiple readers can be configured for a block's exit. Similar to Entry Readers, these could be dedicated readers or the readers that control existing access control points like an exit gates or building exit door. A user presenting a credential at one of these readers will cause any Units that they have
permission to control to be secured, if that unit is in this block.
- Any outputs selected will turn on if any unit in the block is unlocked. This can allow building or section lighting to turn on only if any units in that building/section are unlocked.
- These are the units that belong in this block. If required, units can belong to more than one block.
- Configures how long to units in this block will wait before automatically re-securing after their door was opened and then closed.
- Configures how long to units in this block will wait before entering the Unlocked Too Long state.
The outputs and readers required by the storage block must be created separately using the

A storage block should represent a logical set of storage units with clear entry and exit points controlled with access control. For simple sites, there may be one block for the entire site with entry and exit gate readers used for automatic unit control in the block. Blocks could also represent a building full of units, and the entry and exit readers for that building would be used by the block.

Blocks serve a secondary feature though, providing summary states of their units for use with automation. Many smaller blocks could be created that all use the same entry and exit readers, but only control a smaller set of blocks. This would allow more granular section lighting or other automation to be easily managed.

Alternatively, to better manage common settings, a whole site block could exist with entry and exit readers to control all of the units, set common resecure and unlocked too long times, and so on. Additional blocks can then be created just for local building automation. Those additional blocks wouldn't have readers configured, and would just contain the units in a section of the building and the outputs to control lighting in that section. If using this approach, be sure to set the Unit Unlocked Too Long and Unit Auto-Resecure times to None to not override the values in the main Block.

This page is used to create and edit connections that allow the Inception system to handle text-based 2-way communications with other systems. These connections can be used to trigger certain actions when specific messages are received from the other system, or send messages to the other system when events occur in the Inception system.

Inception's Connections are intended to act as low-level, generic text based communication handlers which enable integration with other systems through sending and receiving short text messages over a network, serial or HTTP connection.

Serial Connections can be configured to connect to any serial device that has been previously connected to the Inception system. You can choose to either connect to the first plugged in and available serial device, or pick one of the devices from a list. The baud rate, parity bit, number of data bits and number of stop bits are all configurable for serial connections.

TCP Connections can be configured to act as a server and listen for incoming connections on a specified port on the Inception device, or to act as a client and establish a connection to a specified server given an IP address and port number.

Connections work by opening a connection to an IP address/port number, and holding that connection open indefinitely while sending text messages through to the other system. They are also capable of bidirectional communication through receiving text messages back from the other system's server, if the server supports that functionality. If the TCP connection fails due to network problems or configuration issues, the
connection will continually attempt to reconnect to the server until it is successful.
Connections work by continuously listening for incoming connections on a specific port and accepting clients, and accepting all incoming text messages from any of those connected clients. Only one

connection can be listening on a single port at a given time; attempting to have more than one connection listening on the same port will result in the first connection successfully listening on that port and the others failing to initialise. The
connection is also capable of sending text messages to the connected clients if an action uses the server connection as a target. Sending a text message through the

connection will forward that message to all TCP clients currently connected to the server (if no clients are connected, no message will be sent), which allows for bidirectional communication with clients if they support this functionality.
If bi-directional communication over TCP is desired, both the

connection types are capable of both sending and receiving messages over the one connection; however, it is generally more consistent and reliable to use a dedicated connection for each responsibility, i.e. using a dedicated client connection solely for sending messages and a dedicated server connection solely for receiving messages, if the other system supports this configuration.

Connections work by sending a HTTP GET request to a URL on a web server, with optional authentication. This type of connection is best suited for communicating with systems that expose a HTTP API, for example, a camera that can be made to perform pan & zoom actions when it receives requests for certain URLs. When the HTTP connection sends a
, it works by appending the message text to the URL Path specified in the connection details, and performing a GET request aimed at that URL. For example, if the URL Path is
and the message being sent is
, the connection would perform a GET request on the URL
. HTTP connections are only capable of sending messages, they do not support receiving messages. If a connection sends a message and receives a
response from the server, or no response at all, the connection status will reflect this by changing to
. The data framing prefix/suffix fields are ignored by HTTP connections.
authentication for sending requests to systems that require user authorisation.
authentication is the simplest and most common type, and it only requires a username & password to be set.

authentication is less commonly used but can be more secure, as it only sends hashed information. Digest authentication requires that a username, password, and domain is specified. Check the requirements of the target system's HTTP API to find out what type of authentication should be used for the Connection, if any.
Connections work by listening for incoming HTTP requests on the
endpoint on the Inception web server, with a

URL parameter to identify the intended receiving Connection in the system by its Key String field, and the message text supplied in either another URL parameter (for GET requests) or in the request body (for POST requests). To send a request to a Connection with
as its Key String, the request would have to contain
. The Key String field must be unique amongst all HTTP Receive Connections. HTTP Basic authentication can be enabled for added security with a required username and password.
Connections support receiving messages via both GET and POST requests. When sending messages to HTTP Receive Connections via GET requests, clients should supply the message text in the
URL parameter, for example: to send a message of
to a Connection with the Key String
, a client would make a request like
. When sending messages via POST requests, the message text should be placed in the request body instead, e.g.
If a Connection is repeatedly failing or messages are not being sent/received properly, enabling
page can help to diagnose faults by showing reasons for failure and individual message information in the
page. For example, if the review messages show that only partial messages are being received, there may be a problem with the Connection's framing strings.

Connections can be optionally configured to have framing characters to denote the start or end of a message being sent or received. These framing characters also support escape sequences to aid in specifying special characters like newline or null characters. For example, setting the suffix for received messages to '\ \ ' would require the other system to end all messages with a carriage return + line feed character sequence to signify that the message ends before those characters.
Incoming messages received through Connections can be used to trigger actions in the Inception system by configuring an Automated Action (
) and using the Connection item as part of a trigger condition. They can also be used to send messages when an Automated Action is triggered, by adding a
response to the Automated Action. Text messages in trigger conditions and action responses can also contain any of the escape sequences that the framing prefixes/suffixes support.
Your web page profile will determine if you can create or modify the items on this page.
This page is used to create and edit counters to allow advanced automation events when used in conjunction with automated actions.

Counters are items that contain an integer number value for their internal state, which can be modified manually or by events in the system. They have no additional properties besides an optional minimum and maximum limit value; the counter's functionality consists entirely of its state value, which can be viewed and controlled on the

Counters are primarily intended for usage in combination with automated actions, to allow advanced automation functionality to be configured, such as triggering an action once a certain number of events have occurred, e.g. an input activation counter or reader badge counter. Counter state values are persisted across system power cycles.
The counter's value can be restricted to a certain range by using the

fields. Attempting to set a counter's value outside this range will cause it to be clamped to the nearest min or max limit value. When setting the minimum and maximum allowed values for a counter, the minimum value cannot be larger than the maximum value, and vice versa.
You can control counters on this page by clicking the
button in the toolbox in the top-right corner of the page. The control dialog allows you to increment, decrement and manually set values for counters.

Counters can be used in conjunction with automated actions in 2 ways: as a trigger condition, and as an action response. As a trigger condition, counters can be used to trigger actions when their value becomes less than, greater than, or equal to a certain value. As an action response, the automated action can increment or decrement the counter's value by a specific number, or set the counter's value to a specific number.
This page should only be used by the installer during the setup or ongoing maintenance of the system. Your web page profile will determine if you can view or view and edit the counters.
This page is used to view and control the state of areas. To control an area you must have the appropriate user permissions and web page profile.
This page will dynamically display the state of areas on the Inception Controller. This page also allows you to arm and disarm areas remotely via web interface.

When this page is first opened it will display the current state of all the areas and the page will dynamically update as the areas change state. The area state can easily be identified by the colour of the area and the text description in the
The areas can be controlled in three different ways:
button for each area will arm or disarm the area based on its current state.
button will open a window that contains control options and a progress display.
button in the toolbar will also open a window that contains control options and a progress display, however, these controls will affect every area that you have permission to control.
. This is the time that, without any user interaction, this area is scheduled to automatically arm.

The areas that you are able to control will be based on your user permissions which applies any time or control restrictions. Your web page profile will also determine if you are restricted to only view or view and control the areas.
page would commonly be used if a user had left the site and had forgotten to arm an area or were unsure if the area arm was successful. In most cases this could be avoided by using an
for the area. However, you would be able to remotely arm the area ensuring the site is secure.

This page is used to view and control the state of doors. You can lock, unlock, lockout or grant access to doors from this page. To control a door, you must have the appropriate user permissions and web page profile.

This page will dynamically display the state of doors. The doors state is determined by combining the state of the door lock, door reed and tongue sense. The door reed and tongue sense determine if the door is closed securely or open/unsecured. This page lets you control a single door or all doors based on your user permissions.

When this page is first opened it will display the current state of all the doors, the page will dynamically update as the doors change state. The door state can easily be identified by the colour of the door and the text description in the
The doors can be controlled in 3 different ways:
button for a locked door will unlock the door for its
, allowing a user to access the door. If the door is unlocked the
button will open a window that contains control options to override the doors state and a progress display.
button in the toolbar will also open a window that contains control options and a progress display, however, these controls will affect every door that you have permission to control.

The override options are used to temporarily change the normal behaviour of a door, locking a door that should be unlocked or unlocking a door that is normally locked. A door can also be placed in the
state in an emergency, preventing any users from being granted access to the door until the lockout is cleared. Once an override is cleared, the door will revert back to its normal behaviour.

The doors that you are able to control will be based on your user permissions including any time or access restrictions. Your web page profile will also determine if you are restricted to only view or view and control the doors.
In most cases, a door is controlled by a user presenting a card at a reader, schedules or automation. You would use the
page to temporarily override the default behaviour of a door if the user has forgotten their card and is unable to unlock a door and enter the building.
This page is used to control and view the state of inputs. You can isolate and de-isolate inputs from this page.
This page is to identify any abnormal status for inputs and, if found, isolate the input to avoid unnecessary alarms being generated or remote arming of areas failing.

When this page is first opened all the current states for the inputs are displayed and the page will dynamically update as inputs change state. The input state can easily be identified by the colour of the input and the text description in the
The inputs can be controlled by clicking the

button, this will open a window that contains control options and a progress display. If an input is isolated from this page, the system will ignore all of the inputs state changes until it is de-isolated. An input that is isolated on this page can only be de-isolate from this page. This allows faulty inputs or those undergoing maintenance to remain isolated until they are fixed.

Since inputs can not be assigned to user permissions the control of inputs is based only on your web page profile. Your web page profile will determine if you are restricted to only view or view and control inputs.
This page is used to view and control the state of outputs. In order for you to control an output, you must have the appropriate user permissions and web profile. The output itself requires the
This page will dynamically display the state of all the configured outputs on the Inception Controller. This page also allows you to turn outputs on or off remotely via web interface.

When this page is first opened, all the current states for the outputs are displayed and the page will dynamically update as the outputs change state. The state of the output can easily be identified by the colour of the output and the text description in the
column. The outputs can be controlled in two different ways:
button for each output will turn the output on or off based on its current state.
button will open a window that contains control options and a progress display. When an output is controlled from this window the control is timed using the
option, this allows you to turn an output on or off for a period of time.
Only outputs that are configured as
pages will appear on this page.

The Outputs that can be controlled are based on your user permissions, which applies any time or control restrictions. Your web page profile will also determine if you are restricted to only view or view and control the outputs.

Since outputs can be used for many non-security functions such as controlling lighting or air-conditioning this page is commonly used to interact with building automation. You could easily turn lighting on or off using a smart phone, this could be used to ensure that all lighting is turned off at the end of the day.

Please note: It is not recommended to provide a user control of an output associated to the security system such as a piezo screamer. They may accidentally turn on an extremely loud screamer remotely that nobody onsite can turn off.
This page is used to view the state of hardware modules. This is used to identify modules that are online, offline or have an abnormal status.

This page will dynamically display the state of the Inception Controller and all Inception hardware modules. Inception hardware modules are devices that are added to the Inception Controller to provide additional inputs, outputs, doors and readers. These hardware modules require an RS-485 LAN connection to the controller to function. If this connection is severed, the module does not function and is considered to be offline and all detector inputs on that module are considered tampered with.

When this page is first opened, all the current states of the hardware modules are displayed and as the modules states change the page will update. The module state can easily be identified by the colour of the module and the text description in the
column. This page is informational only and does not provide any control options for the user.
This page will only display the modules that have been configured in the

Depending on their configuration, the system will monitor the modules for a number of health issues such as tampering or power issues. If these issues will persist for a period of time, the module can be

, causing the system to ignore health issues from that module. This prevents health issues from that module from being reported to monitoring stations and also prevents users from being prompted about the health issues from that module when they attempt to arm areas. Once the health issue is fixed, the module can be de-isolated to return it to normal behaviour.

page is commonly used by the installer during the initial installation of the Inception System during maintenance. This page allows the installer to identify any issues with the modules that may impact site security. It also allows modules to be isolated if issues are present that may take some time to repair.

This page is used to view the state of peripheral devices. Peripherals are the advanced devices that can be connected to hardware modules, like Unibus expanders on 8 Input Expanders, or SIFER or OSDP readers on SLAMs or the Controller.

This page will dynamically display the state of all the attached peripheral devices. Inception peripherals are devices that users interact with such as SIFER Readers that attach to hardware modules. These peripherals are monitored, for example, SIFER or OSDP readers are monitored to determine if they are still connected, unlike a Wiegand reader. This page allows an installer to identify and troubleshoot issues with peripheral devices.

When this page is first opened all the current states for the Peripherals are displayed, as the peripherals change state the page will update. The peripherals state can easily be identified by the colour of the peripheral and the text description in the
This page will only display the peripherals that have been connected to the Inception system.

page would commonly be used by the installer during the initial installation of the Inception System or during maintenance. This page allows the installer to identify any issues with the peripherals that may impact site security.
This page is used to view the state of time periods. Time periods are specific days/times that can be used with permissions or automated actions.

This page will dynamically display the state of all the time periods. Time periods are generally used for scheduling system responses or user permissions. This page displays the time period state and shows when the next time period change will occur, allowing you to identify why a door is not yet unlocked or why a user does not have access to a particular item.

When this page is first opened, all the current states for the Time Periods are displayed and as the Time Periods change state the page will update. The time period state can easily be identified by the colour of the time period and the text description in the

page would commonly be used to identify whether a time period is active or inactive and when the next time period change will occur without needing to open time period programming. If time periods states appear to be incorrect, ensure the current date and time of the controller is correct (seen at the bottom of the web page or on the
This page is used to view the location of users. Each time a user enters a door that is configured with areas on the other side, the user's location will update.

This page is used to identify where a user is located, this may be required in an emergency situation or simply when you want to easily find a person. User location can also be used to identify who is on site or off site if users need to badge in when they arrive and badge out to leave.

This location is updated based on the door access requests. If access is granted, the user will have their location updated to the area on the other side of the door. Note, if the door has a reed or tongue configured, the user's location will update when the door opens. This of course cannot account for doors in free access or with REX buttons, or a user tailgating another user in or out of a door.
User location is more accurate when used on a site that has multiple areas, with all areas physically separated by

When this page is first opened, all the current locations for the Users are displayed and as the user location changes the page will update the location column. This page is informational only and does not provide any control options for the user. If the user does not have a location they have not entered a door programmed with inside or outside areas.

If all your doors are configured with inside and outside areas this page can give you an accurate indication of their last known location. In the case of an emergency such as a fire alarm, this page can be used to provide emergency services the user's last known location to assist in finding the user.

If anti passback is enabled on any doors, anti passback violations or exemptions will also be shown on this page. Forgiveness for violations gives the user an anti passback exemption and can be given on a per-user basis, to all users with a violation or to every user. If a user has an exemption, their next access request that their permissions allow will be granted, even if it would have caused an anti passback violation. The purpose of an exemption is to help the user correct their location in the system, so a successful location change will automatically use up the exemption.

If there are a certain number of login attempts allowed for each user, they will be locked out after exceeding those login attempts and it will be displayed on this page. Quick control will allow you to end the lockout early for this user. This page can also forgive all users with a login lockout at the same time as long as your priority ranking is higher than each user being forgiven.
Your ability to grant anti passback forgiveness or lift login lockouts for users will depend on your web page profile and whether it lets you control users.

This page is used to view and control the state of lift floors. You can set to free access, secure or lockout floors from this page. To control a floor, you must have the appropriate user permissions and web page profile.
This page will dynamically display the state of floors. Floors can be in one of three states,

. A floor in free access will cause the lift cars that can access that floor to enable the button for that floor, allowing any user to select that floor without needing to present a card. A secured floor requires a card to be presented in a lift car before a button linked to the floor would be enabled. A locked out floor will prevent a button linked to the floor from enabling, even if a user with permission to the floor presents their card.

When this page is first opened it will display the current state of all the floors, the page will dynamically update as the floors change state. The floor state can easily be identified by the colour of the floor and the text description in the
The floors can be controlled in 3 different ways:
button for a secured floor will override its state to free access or if the floor is in free access it will secure the floor.
button will open a window that contains control options to override the floors state and a progress display.
button in the toolbar will also open a window that contains control options and a progress display, however, these controls will affect every floor that you have permission to control.

The override options are used to temporarily change the normal behaviour of a floor, securing a floor that should be in free access or freeing a floor that is normally secured. A floor can also be placed in the
state in an emergency, preventing any users from being granted access to the floor until the lockout is cleared. Once an override is cleared, the floor will revert back to its normal behaviour.

The floors that you are able to control will be based on your user permissions, including any time or access restrictions. Your web page profile will also determine if you are restricted to only view or view and control the floors.
In most cases, a floor is controlled by schedules or automation. You would use the
page to temporarily override the default behaviour of a floor for an emergency or a special scenario.

This page is used to view and control the state of storage units. You can unlock, secure, or control the vacancy of units from this page. To control a unit, you must have the appropriate user permissions and web page profile.

This page will dynamically display the state of storage units. Unlocked or Vacant units can be opened and accessed without triggering alarm states. If a unit has a Door Position Sensor input specified, the unit will also show if its door is open or not.

When this page is first opened it will display the current state of all the units, the page will dynamically update as the units change state. The unit state can easily be identified by the colour of the floor and the text description in the
column. It will also include information on who last accessed or controlled the unit.
The units can be controlled in 3 different ways:
button for a unit will unlock or secure it based on its current state. If vacant, the quick control button will clear that vacancy.
button will open a window that contains control options to override the unit state and a progress display.
button in the toolbar will also open a window that contains control options and a progress display, however, these controls will affect every unit that you have permission to control.

A secure unit will enter the Alarm state if any of its sensors activate. Unlocked units can be opened without risk of alarm, but may enter an Unlocked Too Long state based on configuration. For units that will remain empty, they can be marked as vacant, allowing free access and no automation responses.
Units that have their inputs tampered with will enter a Tamper state.

The units that you are able to control will be based on your user permissions, including any time or access restrictions. Your web page profile will also determine if you are restricted to only view or view and control the units.
In most cases, a unit is controlled by a users block access. You would use the
page to manually unlock a unit, or control that unit's vacancy.
This page is used to view and control scheduled tasks. To control a scheduled task you must have the appropriate user permissions and web page profile.

This page will dynamically display the state of scheduled tasks in the Inception system. This page displays the scheduled task's state, the last time the task was run (if known), and the next time a task is due to run.
The scheduled task can be controlled by clicking the
button from the advanced control dialog. This will queue the task to run immediately, and will show the progress and any errors encountered if the run was unsuccessful.
The scheduled tasks that you are able to view and/or control will be based on your assigned web page profile.
In most cases, a scheduled task automatically executes itself in accordance with a pre-defined schedule, or when triggered by an automated action. You would use the

page either to view the current state, last run time, and next scheduled run time of scheduled tasks; or to manually execute the scheduled tasks if you wish to test that the task is properly configured and handles the sending of results, emails, etc. correctly.

This page is used to view and control the state of counters. You can increment, decrement and manually set counter values from this page. To control a counter, you must have the appropriate user permissions and web page profile.
This page will dynamically display the state of counters. Counters' states are represented by a number value, which can be incremented, decremented or set to a specific value by events in the system.
When this page is first opened it will display the current state of all the counters and the page will dynamically update as the counters change state.
button will open a window that contains control options to change the counter's value and a progress display.
buttons will add or subtract 1 from the counter's current value, restricted by the counter's configured minimum and maximum value if applicable. The
button will set the counter value to the specified number, restricted to the range of the configured minimum and maximum value if applicable.
In most cases, counters are controlled by
programming when events occur in the system, e.g. a card being presented when a person enters a room may increase a counter by 1, and a person leaving a room may decrease it by 1. You would use the
page to manually adjust or reset a counter value if the counter's value has been changed inadvertently or is causing automation to function incorrectly.

This page is used to allow testing of inputs within areas, verifying that they are operating correctly. When active, input alarms will not be processed, allowing lesser used inputs like user duress or emergency buttons to be tested without sirens and alarms being reported.

Area walk tests are intended for routine maintenance of the system, allowing for periodic testing of the detectors and buttons within an area to confirm they are functioning correctly. Most importantly, this includes important but infrequently used inputs like medical, smoke or emergency buttons, as well as duress buttons.
To test an input, it must move from an

state. This typically involves pressing a button or activating a detector and then returning it to a normal state. This also ensures that user duress or emergency buttons which require resetting are returned to a normal position before the test is completed.
While the test is active, normal

states are ignored by the system, allowing buttons that would generate an immediate alarm to be tested without a siren and alarm response. Tamper detection and other fault states will still be processed however, preventing the system from being modified while the test is active. It is recommended that walk tests be performed as part of regular system maintenance and that tests do not run for too long, as some emergency response components may not be available during the test.

The current state of a test can be seen from multiple browsers with state dynamically updated. This allows tests to be run on multiple areas at once and allows progress be seen on portable devices like a smartphone. As each input is successfully tested, they will automatically move from the
list. The walk test completes successfully when all inputs have been tested and have moved to the

Tests can only be started when the area is disarmed and there is no active alarms in that area. Once started, a test will run indefinitely until it either successfully completes or is cancelled. A test can be manually cancelled at any time to return input processing back to normal and will automatically cancel if the area is armed while a walk test is running.
This page is used to backup/restore the Inception controllers database and default the Inception controller to factory settings.

The Inception Controller gives you the flexibility to configure the entire system from any device with a web browser, however, all the programming is held on the panel and not on the device you connect with.

The backup process provides you with a way to extract all the programming contained within the Inception Controller. It is recommended to perform backups regularly to prevent programming loss in case of an issue during firmware update, future configuration mistakes or for the rare chance of hardware failure. This may happen due to environmental factors such as power surges.

The restore process provides a way to roll back to a previous version of programming to undo unwanted changes. It also allows the Inception Controller to be replaced with another controller while keeping all the previously programmed functionality.

You can also perform a factory reset from this page, this will delete all programming currently on the Inception Controller and return it to factory default. This may be used if you were repurposing an Inception Controller.
Your web page profile will determine if you can perform each of the tasks on this page.
This page is used to display system status and system warnings.

The system overview page shows summary information about the Inception System. Any issues that could keep the system from running optimally are also listed here and will cause a warning or error to display in the notification bar at the top of the page. Any warnings should be rectified as soon as possible. These could be indicating issues like modules or peripherals are offline or have health issues like power problems, or that an alarm reporting path is offline. Other warnings like inputs that are permanently isolated or doors which can not lock due to a broken emergency breakglass are also shown here.

option in the toolbar allows verbose review messages and system log messages to be created. These additional events can be used to aid in diagnosing issues and identify unusual behaviour. It is recommended that this option is only enabled while diagnosing issues and is otherwise disabled, as the verbose review events are unsuitable for day to day usage.

option allows a commissioning report to be downloaded, which includes details about all configured options in the system. This report is useful as it gives a human readable hardcopy of all programming in the system, allowing easy reference in future about the programming at the end of commissioning. Clicking
will open a window where you can select the items the report will contain. Clicking the
button on this window will save a text file to your device.
If requested by Inner Range technical support you may need to use the
button to download the system log. The system log is a password protected event log that is designed to be read by Inner Range technical support staff, for use in advanced troubleshooting.
This page is used to view and upgrade the Inception controllers firmware.

As bug fixes and additional features are added to the Inception Controller new firmware versions will be released. These firmware files will be available on the Inner Range website (www.innerrange.com). The firmware file is then copied to the Inception Controller and once uploaded, the upgrade process can begin. This can take some time, do not power down the Inception Controller during this upgrade.

It is recommended that a database backup is taken before firmware updates are applied as the database is automatically updated during the upgrade and won't be compatible in case there is a need to go back.
This page is used to view and upgrade the Inception modules firmware.

As bug fixes and additional features are added to the modules connected to the Inception Controller new firmware versions will be released. These firmware files will be available on the Inner Range website. (www.innerrange.com). The module firmware file is then copied to the Inception Controller and once uploaded, the upgrade process can begin. One or more modules can be selected to be upgraded simultaneously, allowing the upgrade to be done in batches if on a live site. This can take some time, do not power down the Inception Controller or the module during this upgrade.

Peripherals such as Unibus modules and SIFER readers can also be updated in this way. When updating peripherals, the module or modules they are connected to is selected, which means peripheral updating can not be done individually and instead must be done on a per-module basis.

This page is used to enable, update or disable Service Mode. Service mode is used to disable area alarm processing, sirens, alarm reporting and door DOTL feedback during the initial setup and ongoing maintenance of the Inception system.
Service Mode is enabled by default during the initial setup of an Inception Controller, the commissioning checklist provides a step to disable service mode.

When enabling service mode for maintenance of the Inception System, certain features like the processing of area alarms, ability to sound sirens or report alarms and door DOTL feedback can be optionally disabled. This allows the system to be safely interacted with, without sirens sounding and alarms being reported due to cabinet tampers and other states.

The disabled features can be optionally re-enabled too if they require testing. This could, for example, allow area alarm processing to be enabled without sirens or alarm reporting to perform dry-runs of the security area.

The service mode can only be enabled for a limited amount of time, ensuring that the system will revert back to its normal operation and can't be accidentally left in service mode. Disabling service mode can also be done with a delay allowing the completion of tasks such as closing cabinets after disconnecting the ethernet or wifi adapter without generating tamper alarms.

This page is used to view entities and their details that normally would be acquired through REST API requests. This page can be used as a quick reference to acquire details required to make other REST API requests without having to do other ones first to acquire IDs and other required details.
This page is used to configure the date and time settings of the Inception Controller

It is important that the Inception controller is set to the correct date and time to ensure that time periods and calendar events occur at the correct time. Since time periods can be used for user permissions and automation there could be problems if the controller time was out even by the slightest amount. You may have programmed a door to unlock at 8am every day, if the controller time is incorrect the door will not behave as expected. If the date is set incorrectly then time periods that should be active during the week may incorrectly become active on the weekends instead and unlock doors when no one is onsite.

The system can be configured to sync with an NTP server, either from the internet or one locally on site. Using this with the time zone configured ensures the date and time is kept correct. Daylight savings changes are automatically handled by the selected timezone.
If no NTP server exists, either due to no internet access or none existing on site, the date and time can be updated manually.
Your web page profile will determine if you can view or view and edit Date and Time settings.
This page is used to configure the network settings of the Inception Controller

The only method for connection to the web interface is via a wired or wireless network. The default network settings are configured for wired Ethernet using DHCP, this means that if the Inception Controller is connected to an existing network it will automatically be assigned an IP address. This page allows you to configure the additional setting for the Ethernet connection and even enable Wi-Fi or allow access to the web interface via Skytunnel.

If the Inception Controller is connected to a network that does not support DHCP addressing, you may need to manually configure network settings such as the controller's IP address and the subnet mask.

You can also connect to the Inception Controller using a wireless network if you have purchased the Inception Wireless Adapter. You can configure the Wi-Fi connection in two different ways, wireless hotspot or wireless network. The wireless hotspot allows you to connect your laptop, tablet or phone directly to the Inception Controller on a private network using only the wireless adapter. This allows connection to the Inception web interface without connecting your device to the site's network.

The Inception Wireless Adapter can also be used to wirelessly connect the Inception Controller to the site's wireless network. This will allow a network connected laptop, tablet or phone to connect to the Inception Controller. The Wi-Fi connection supports WPA2 personal and enterprise authentication.

The Inception controller can also utilize the Inner Range SkyTunnel architecture. SkyTunnel is a hassle free connection method that requires an internet connection for the Inception Controller and the connecting device. SkyTunnel is commonly used when access to the Inception Controller is desired via the Internet, for example, a user accessing the Inception web interface from a mobile phone in a different location. Use of the SkyTunnel architecture for internet access requires a paid subscription or an active alarm monitoring account.
Your web page profile will determine if you are restricted to only view or view and edit network settings.

This page would commonly be used by an installer during the installation or ongoing maintenance of the Inception System. If incorrectly configured, you will not be able to connect to the web interface to change the programming. You will need correct the programming from an LCD Terminal via
or by factory defaulting the Inception Controller.
This page is used to create and edit notifiers that allow users to receive messages when various system events occur.

Notifiers are configured with a list of recipient users to send messages to, along with an optional schedule for when messages are allowed to be sent. Multiple notifiers can be created, each with different schedules and user recipients and different items to notify on. Notifiers can be configured, for example, to send notifications whenever an alarm occurs in a particular area or access to a particular door is granted or denied. Notifications can also be sent when repeated failed login attempts are made on the web interface or LCD Terminal, or if programming changes are made to particular entities. Maintenance events, such as power and battery issues of modules or RF devices can also be communicated.
Notifications can be sent to users via email with clear, easy to read language. To use the email notification path, an email server must be configured on the
page. The users specified as recipients also require an email address assigned to them via the

page. When multiple events need to be sent to the same user, they are consolidated into a single message to help prevent issues with the configured mail server or filling the user's mailbox. See the tooltip on the

Area notifications allow messages to be sent whenever arm or disarm attempts are made, or when alarm events occur. In addition, area arm and disarm validation can also be configured, with messages being sent if someone arms or disarms an area at the wrong time, or the area is not armed or disarmed when it should be. Specific input event types like alarm or duress events can also be chosen for monitoring, or events like low battery for RF devices can be sent to indicate potential maintenance is required. All areas in the system can be monitored, or specific areas can be manually chosen.

Door events allow monitoring of failed or successful user access attempts through doors. Messages can also be sent when doors are left open too long or if the door is overridden from it's default behaviour. Similar to areas, all doors in the system can be monitored or specific doors can be manually chosen.

Live state of the system can be monitored, with messages being sent if hardware modules fall offline, experience power issues or are tampered with. Messages can be sent if the system resets with the reason why, as users login to the web interface, make configuration changes within the system or many others.
This page is used to configure various system settings such as the user PIN length, duress user, end of line config and battery testing

The user PIN length can be configured with a minimum and a maximum length, this length requirement is used when manually entering a PIN number for a user or when a pin number is automatically generated for a user. Both of the methods for assigning PINs to a user are done from the

If users will be controlling areas or outputs from an LCD terminal, it is important to make sure the user PIN length is long enough that it is unlikely the user PIN's could be guessed. A longer PIN number combined with the
setting for the LCD terminal will make it very unlikely that a person could guess a PIN number. The
page for each of the LCD terminals.

feature is used when a person is placed in a duress situation and is forced to login to an LCD terminal to control an area or output. If the Duress User has been enabled and a security PIN has been assigned, the user can login with this PIN instead of their own. Once logged in as the Duress User, the Duress input for the LCD Terminal will go into the alarm state, which will be reported to the monitoring station if alarm monitoring is configured without sounding any alarms. Alarm monitoring is configured from the

Most security systems use End of Line resistors to determine if an input is in the alarm, tamper or sealed state. Inner Range products have historically used 2k2 and 6k8 or 2k2 and 2k2 ohm resistor pairs, however the Inception Controller and expansion modules can be configured to use other End of Line resistance values. This allows a security system from a different manufacturer to be easily replaced by an Inception Controller without changing the End of Line resistors found on every detection device.

Battery testing is required to ensure that the in the event of AC failure, each module can be powered from its attached battery. Testing should be done regularly to ensure that if a battery fails over time it is detected. For a module to be included in the battery test it will need to have a power supply connected, battery connected and battery testing enabled on the
Your web page profile will determine if you can view or view and edit System Settings.
This page is used to view the licensed feature summary and license keys for this Inception system.
This page can be used to apply license keys and fetch the latest license key information about the system. The latest licensing information is gathered from
. The latest information is fetched daily, but can be done early by pressing the
button in the toolbar. If there is a missing license due to a lack of a connection to Inner Range's licensing service, pressing the
button in the toolbar will allow you to apply a key manually.
The feature summary will display all possible licensed features and their current availability based on the associated license keys for the system.

The license keys section will show an independent breakdown of associated license keys and which feature the license key corresponds to. If the license has an expiration date, it will be displayed as well.
This page is used to configure alarm reporting.

The primary purpose of an intrusion detection system is to identify that an intruder has entered a building. The use of sirens, siren outputs and strobes are to ensure attention is drawn to the site and the intruder leaves the site quickly. In most cases, the intrusion detection is paired with alarm monitoring to report events to a monitoring station. The monitoring station will process the events according to their policies.
With analogue phone lines becoming obsolete the Inception controller provides 2 alternatives for alarm reporting.

- This uses the Inception Controllers network connection with internet access to communicate with the SkyTunnel server. SkyTunnel is a cloud-based service that links two outbound connections, which means that you will not require port forwarding. Once the event is received by the SkyTunnel server it is delivered to a monitoring station. This works out of the box and requires no other specialized hardware.

- The T4000 is an Inner Range device that connects to the Inception Controller via USB. It provides multiple redundant reporting paths using multiple 3G networks in addition to ethernet internet reporting. With all paths polled frequently, this ensures reliable alarm monitoring.
Both reporting paths can be configured for Periodic Test Reports. These test reports ensure that communication to the monitoring station occurs successfully in the absence of alarm events.

The Inception Controller supports both of these reporting paths at the same time to provide redundancy. You will need to select a primary and backup path based on your preferred method of communication.
Your web page profile will determine if you can view or view and edit settings on this page.
This page is used to create and edit time periods.

Time periods are items that change between active and inactive based on the current date and time. Time periods can be used to represent certain business rules and allow for things within the system to be scheduled, such as restricting when a user is allowed to do certain actions or automatically unlocking a door or arming an area.

Time periods are broken up into two sections. First are the weekly recurring times, where multiple time blocks across multiple days can be configured. These time blocks apply every single week to the time period, causing it to become active whenever the current day and time matches a time block. This could allow Monday to Friday, 9am to 5pm to be configured, and every week the time period would be active between those times. The second section contains the exceptions to the rule, which can override the weekly recurring times.

The first exception type is calendar exceptions. Calendars are focused on dates rather than days of the week and provide alternative start and end times to the regular recurring times of time periods for the dates defined within the calendar. Calendar exceptions are used when the same time change for a day should be repeated on multiple days throughout the year. The calendar can be updated, which could affect multiple time periods in a consistent way. A common use case example for calendar exceptions are extended trading hour days, where trading hours change consistently on many days throughout the year. The calendar in that case could apply to multiple time periods, like working hours, automatic door times and automatic arm times, each reacting to the same calendar. A user can then update the calendar throughout the year and the system reacts correctly. Another example is public holidays, a calendar can include holidays that that always fall on the same day each year, such as Christmas and New Year’s Eve and have them repeat, as well as being manually updated each year with holidays that change like Easter. The calendar exception could prevent the time period from being valid on a particular day and as such the normal operations would not apply for such things as user permissions or door free access times.

Time periods also offer another way to apply conditional rules, which is to add exceptions directly to the time period. These exceptions are similar to calendar exceptions, and ultimately achieve the same outcome, but are intended to be used in instances where a single exception may apply such as shutting for maintenance for a period of time or a brief period of extended trading hours.

In most circumstances exceptions and calendar exceptions will offer similar functionality, the major differences are that calendars can be programmed once and applied to many time periods, which saves programming time. Calendar exceptions also provided calculated dates, which offers much greater flexibility when trying to achieve otherwise difficult exception rules.

Note that you are not restricted in using one or the other of these exception settings, it's perfectly fine to have the time period use both exceptions and calendar exceptions. Overall, both exception types allow temporary modifications to be made to the time period, potentially well in advance, without needing to alter the standard weekly recurring times.

This page also provides a preview of the time period, this preview can be used to view any date in the future and will display how the time period would have state on that day, taking into account any calendar exceptions or date & time exceptions. This is useful to confirm whether exceptions planned for the future have been applied correctly.

This page is used by the installer during the initial installation and ongoing maintenance of the Inception system. Due to the fact that time periods are heavily used in user permissions and automation it is recommended that permissions for this page be limited to the installer or the system administrator.
Your ability to view or view and edit time periods from this page is based on your webpage profile.
This page is used to create and edit recurring calendar dates which are used as exceptions to the normal operation of time periods.

Calendars can be applied to time periods as calendar exceptions. Calendars are focused on dates rather than days of the week and provide alternative start and end times to the regular recurring times of time periods for the dates defined within the calendar. Calendar exceptions are used when the same time change for a day should be repeated on multiple days throughout the year. The calendar can be updated, which could affect multiple Time Periods in a consistent way.

A common use case example for calendar exceptions are extended trading hour days, where trading hours change consistently on many days throughout the year. The calendar, in that case, could apply to multiple time periods, like working hours, automatic door times and automatic area arm times, each reacting to the same calendar. A user can then update the calendar throughout the year and the system reacts correctly. Another example is public holidays, a calendar can include holidays that always fall on the same day each year, such as Christmas and New Year’s Eve and have them repeat, as well as being manually updated each year with holidays that change like Easter. The calendar exception could prevent the time period from being valid on a particular day and as such the normal operations would not apply to such things as user permissions or door free access times.
Calendars can be programmed once and applied to many time periods, which saves programming time.
Your ability to view or view and edit calendar dates from this page is based on your web page profile.
This page is used to test the inputs and outputs of each module individually.

This page will dynamically display the state of all the configured inputs and outputs allowing the installer to do a physical inspection or walk test. This page provides control of outputs without needing the output set as
or the having the output assigned to the user permissions.

When this page is first opened, all the current states for the inputs and outputs on the selected module are displayed and as they change state the page will update. The input or output state can easily be identified by the colour and the text description in the
column. The outputs can be controlled by clicking the

To test a module, select it from the module list, the page will update to display the current state of all the configured inputs and outputs for the module. If there are inputs or outputs missing from this list they have not been configured in the

page. The output test will allow you to control an output to confirm it has been wired correctly and is working as expected. The input test displays all the states that the inputs change to since the module was selected. This can be used to walk test all the inputs for a particular module.

Please note: This page is only intended to be used by an installer to test the installation of inputs and outputs. Controlling outputs directly could override behaviour, like a door that is considered locked could have it's lock output turned on. It is important to return any controlled outputs to their original state, or interact with the item that should control the outputs state, like unlocking the door.
This page is used to generate various types of reports which contain information about the system.

Each report can be customized to show specific information, for example, when running the Access History report, you may wish to only include certain Doors or Users, or set the included timeframe of the results to be between 1 month ago and now.
Multiple file formats are available for report generation:

outputs a single pdf which should be sufficient for most cases, however, extremely large reports (i.e. 1000+ pages) are not able to be generated in a single PDF file due to system limitations. If this is the case, you can either try to reduce the size of the report data by filtering it to only include specific users or items, or choose a different file format option. The
format option is ideal for viewing the report data directly in a web browser, and is not subject to the size limitations of the
format option outputs a series of individual PDF documents packed together for download in a single ZIP file, and is also not subject to the size limitations of the
have been specified for the desired
button to download a report file from the Inception System containing the desired information in the selected file format.
This page is used to create and edit scheduled tasks to perform regular system operations automatically.

Scheduled tasks are items that automatically execute system tasks on a specific schedule, and then send the results to the user(s) who require them. For example, you could use a scheduled task to schedule a database backup every 2 weeks on a Sunday night at 11:00pm, and have the backup file emailed to the system administrator.
Scheduled tasks consist of 3 main sections:
- When and how often the task should be executed.
- What task to carry out in order to produce a result.
- What to do with the task's results once it runs successfully.
Scheduled tasks can have their schedules set to

schedule allows you to specify a frequency (daily, weekly, etc.), a day of the week, and a time of day at which to run the task. This method is best suited for when you just want to run a task at a set interval and don't need precise control over which weeks or months it runs during. An
schedule allows you to specify a list of entries that consist of a

and a time of day to run. With this method, the task will run on every day that one of the referenced calendars are active on, at the corresponding time of day. This method is suited for when more precise control over the task's execution time is required, for example: when you want to run a task at 9:00am every first Sunday of the month, and also at 10am on every public holiday. For more information on how calendar items work and how to configure them, see the

means the task will not automatically execute itself at regular intervals, but doesn't necessarily mean that it is disabled entirely, as it can still be executed manually by users and triggered by automated actions. For example, a scheduled task could be configured to send a user location report as an email attachment, that is triggered by automated action when an emergency alarm occurs.

schedules will remember their next scheduled run time even if the Inception device loses power, so that when power is restored, tasks that missed their scheduled run time will be immediately executed as soon as possible.
There are currently two types of
backs up the current contents of the Inception database and produces a backup file that can be used to restore the database to that point in time (same as the file produced by the
allows you to configure a report to be automatically generated by the system at regular intervals. Any type of report that can be requested on the
page can also be produced by a scheduled task.

After the task has completed successfully, it needs a way of handling the results so that they can be viewed by users who are interested in them. The results can be sent via email attachment, or copied to a USB drive attached to the Inception controller.
To send results via email attachment, select the

control. This option allows you to select a list of Users in the Inception System to email the results to. Sending results via email requires that the email server configuration is correctly set up on the
page. Users who will be receiving emails must also have their email address configured on the
To copy results to an attached USB drive, select the
control. With this method selected, the task will copy the resulting file to the root folder of the USB drive after it completes.
To copy results to a shared network folder, select the
control. This option allows you to specify a network share location, a domain name, and the domain user credentials to use when copying the output file to the network share location.
Scheduled tasks can be immediately executed at any time by pressing the
button available in the toolbox. Pressing the

button on the dialog will manually run the task which can be helpful for testing the task. This requires the currently logged in user to have permission to control scheduled tasks via Web Page Permissions.

Scheduled tasks can fail in certain cases, either due to not being able to complete the task, or not being able to successfully handle the results. If a task fails, it will generate a system message notifying you about what task failed, when it failed, and the reason for failure.
This page is used to configure the Inner Range Mobile Access connection settings, connecting to a corresponding Mobile Access system using a pairing code generated from the Mobile Access portal.
To link these two systems, first navigate to
, create a new system, navigate to the
page, then generate a pairing code. On this page of the Inception system, click the
button in the toolbox and enter the pairing code that appears. Once the systems are linked, no further configuration is required, and mobile credentials can be enrolled for each user on the
The Inception system can be unlinked from the Mobile Access servers by clicking the

button in the toolbox. While unlinked, new mobile credentials cannot be allocated, however existing mobile credentials will continue to function. Upon relinking of the system back to the same mobile access system, new mobile credentials can then be allocated.
The current number of Mobile Credential licenses used and total available can be viewed by clicking the
button in the toolbox. For further licensing information, refer to the
This page is used to search for mobile credentials in the Inception system based on details like their card number, status, linked user, etc.

The Mobile Credential search table displays all the mobile credentials in the system in a format that enables you to sort and filter the data to see specific mobile credentials that you are interested in. The search boxes above the table header for each column allow you to search for matches in individual fields. You can also combine multiple search filters to get more specific results.

button allows you to selectively show and hide mobile credential fields. Not all fields are visible by default, so you may find it useful to enable visibility for other fields that you want to be included in search results, as hidden fields are not searched by the Search All Fields text box.
The Mobile Credential search table can be sorted by a specific column by clicking on that column's header to toggle sorting order between ascending and descending.

button on a mobile credential row will open a Manage Credential dialog for that specific mobile credential, allowing you to quickly perform actions such as resending the invitation code to the linked user, revoking the credential, or deleting the credential.
Column visibility options set through the Column visibility button are saved system-wide, and can be reset to the default visibility options with the
Any changes made to mobile credentials after the page has loaded will not be reflected in the search results until the page is reloaded.
This page is used to monitor configured wireless devices. To control a device you must have the appropriate user permissions and web page profile.
This page lists wireless inputs and remote fobs. This page will dynamically display the state of wireless devices on the Inception Controller.

When this page is first opened it will display the current state of all the devices and the page will dynamically update as the devices change state. The device state can easily be identified by the colour of the device and the text description in the
button for an offline or unpaired device that can be paired will initiate a re-pairing process with the associated hardware module.
This page is used to configure and control IR Video connections to third party systems, utilising supported Inception integrations.

allow the Inception's functionality to be extended beyond the typical intruder and access control functionality already provided, providing rich integration into external systems. One such integration that Inception supports is Inner Range's own 'IR Video' range, allowing the Inception to connect to, monitor, and control IR Video NVRs and cameras.
Integrations are represented in Inception as two different components:
. The Inception's connection to a third-party device, be it an NVR, camera, or some other device, is stored as an
can then be performed against this connection to populate all devices linked to this connection. For example, if connecting to an IR Video NVR, an
is configured with the NVR's details, and then a
will pull in all cameras connected to the NVR and populate them in the Inception system as
. The NVR itself would also be confingured as an
, allowing for its state to also be monitored.
This page is used to configure cameras that have been fetched from an IR Video connection.
can be managed and deleted as necessary, however they cannot be added. Each
has a reference to its Parent Connection, as well as a button that directly links to that
supports PTZ, then two additional properties will be displayed in the editor:
. These values are provided by the
and reflect what's been configured on the third-party system. If these presets are changed on the third-party system, a
will need to be performed on the parent
Serial ports have been cleaned up.

Allows the current user to be sent an email containing their Security PIN in case it has been forgotten. Requires the user to have an email address configured and for this system to be configured to send emails.
Revealing the Security PIN will show the user's PIN to the current operator, and a Review event will be created to indicate both the user revealing the PIN along with the user whose PIN was revealed.

Enter the user's email address to link this user with an IR Connect account. If the user does not have an IR Connect account already, they will be automatically emailed an invitation to the address entered. The invitation email includes an account activation link and information to assist the user on how to install the IR Connect App on their smartphone.

This dialog allows the generation and copying of an API Token which will be associated with this user. This API Token can be used instead of logging in with this user's details for REST API requests. This can be done by putting 'APIToken' at the start of the request authorization header, then a space, followed by the copied API Token value.
Pressing the Chain button will generate an API Token for this user, replacing the previous one if present.
Creating an API Token reveals it only once, and it can be copied to the clipboard via the Clipboard button.
Warning - Any generated API Tokens will only be visible and copyable right after creation. Closing this dialog will hide the API Token forever, so make sure to copy it.
