For example, 9:00AM - 5:00PM on Monday, Tuesday, Wednesday, Thursday and Friday would be recurring business hours
Add permissions to this user. Permissions can be added to allow access or control of doors, areas or outputs or entire permission groups.
Add permissions to this permission group. Permissions can be added to allow access or control of doors, areas or outputs or entire permission groups.
Use this option to limit which permission groups a user with this Web Page Profile can assign to another User. If none are selected, the user will be able to assign all permission groups
Use this menu to add permissions. Permissions can be added to allow access or control of doors, areas or outputs or entire permission groups..

Pulse state conditions are for moment-in-time events and are instantly set to 'true' then 'false' when the event occurs. Normal state conditions in contrast will transition to be 'true' upon meeting the condition and will stay 'true' until the condition stops being met.

For example, 'Detecting Input Activity' for an area stays true while any input is active, preventing further triggers if one input remains active. 'Detecting New Input Activity (pulsed)' will trigger briefly for each new input activation, allowing actions like timed lighting control based on user movement. Generally, pulse conditions are used to trigger a set of actions when the event occurs.
Select an item type to base this trigger on
Select When the user will have these permissions
What action would you like performed?
The connection to Inception was lost, automatically attempting to reconnect in
Permission can be granted by the ARC or the alarm company that allows a level 2 user to temporarily be allowed to perform certain tasks normally limited to level 3 users.

If permission is granted, the below date and time must be quoted to the ARC or alarm company and they will provide a 6 digit code known as an Anti-Code in response which should be entered below. If the code is correct, you will be briefly allowed to perform the action you were trying to do.
There are one or more system messages that must be acknowledged before the area can be armed. Acknowledge the messages before trying the area arm again.

If the issue preventing arming can not be resolved, the issue can be temporarily overridden with permission from the ARC or alarm company. If permission is granted, an Anti-Code is provided to allow temporary overriding of the issue.
Change the state of areas using the buttons below. This will only work for areas that you have permission to control.
Change the state of areas to Armed.
Change the state of areas to Armed while utilising the designated Exit Delay to allow people time to vacate the area before it is armed.
Change the state of areas to Disarmed.

- For use on areas with multi-mode arming enabled, ignored otherwise. Full Area arms the whole area. Perimeter Mode arms the perimeter, with entry delay when someone opens a door. Night Mode also arms the perimeter, with instant alarm if any perimeter input activates.
Change the state of counters using the buttons below.
Sets the counter's value to the given number.
Change the state of doors using the buttons below. This will only work for doors that you have permission to control, and that are able to perform the designated action.
Change the state of doors to Unlocked. The doors will remain unlocked until the overrides are cleared.
Change the state of doors to Locked. The doors will remain locked until the overrides are cleared.
Change the state of doors to Locked Out - this locks the doors and prevents cards and PINs from unlocking them.
Revert the state of doors to their default state by clearing any overrides.
Unlock doors for the designated Door Unlock Time.
This door does not support Timed Unlock or Grant Access functionality.
Change the state of floors using the buttons below. This will only work for floors that you have permission to control.

Change the state of floors to Free Access, allowing buttons in lift cars that go to the floor to be selected without needing a credential. The floors will remain in free access until the overrides are cleared.
Change the state of floors to Secure, where credentials are required to access the floors. The floors will remain secured until the overrides are cleared.
Change the state of floors to Locked Out. This secures the floors and prevents cards and PINs from enabling buttons that go to the floor.
Revert the state of floors to their default state by clearing any overrides.
Change the state of the selected input using the buttons below.
Isolate the input. This will allow users to arm any areas that this input is assigned to regardless of whether the input is in the alarm state.
De-Isolate the input. This will return the input to it's usual behaviour and prevent the arming of any areas that this input is assigned to if the input is in the alarm state.
This dialog allows for manual commands to be sent to
, allowing direct control or intervention where required.
If supported, the Integrated Device will move the specified preset.
If supported, the Integrated Device will start/stop the specified tour, based on whether
Turns the specified on or off, depending on the value of the
setting for the time specified in White Light Duration filed.
setting. Audio file spcified in Audio File ID will be played. Number of times this Alarm will be played based on the Cycle field value.
Change the state of the selected module using the buttons below.

Isolates the module. This causes the system to ignore any health issues that this module could report, which could possibly prevent area arming, cause sirens to sound and be reported to monitoring stations. Used primarily when issues have been identified with the module that will take some time to fix. If an issue is expected to last for an extended period of time, it may be better to edit the module on the
page and stop the system from monitoring that item.
De-Isolates the module. This causes the system to resume monitoring the module's health, processing and reporting issues if any exist.
Change the state of the selected output using the buttons below. This will only work for outputs that you have permission to control.
Select a time to apply to the on/off controls.
Control Scheduled Tasks using the buttons below. This will only work for scheduled tasks that you have permission to control.
Change the state of storage units using the buttons below. This will only work for storage units that you have permission to control.
Change the state of units to Unlocked, allowing them to be accessed.
Change the state of units to Secure, enabling input alarm monitoring.
Change the state of units to Vacant. Vacancy allows free access to the unit and will remain until it is cleared.
Removes the Vacancy state for the unit if it is active.
Note: Vacancy control requires permissions to be granted via Permission Groups.
Forgive User Anti-Passback violations or user login lockouts using the buttons below.

Users forgiven from an anti-passback violation will be able to move to another area if their permissions allow, even if it will violate Anti-Passback. Allows the user to correct themself if Anti-Passback rules are preventing them from moving.
Forgives all users who have an unresolved Anti-Passback violation.
Grants all users an Anti-Passback exemption, regardless of whether they have an unresolved Anti-Passback violation.
Forgiving user lockouts will lift all current lockouts for all users, giving them another chance to login after having exceeded the threshold of failed login attempts.
Lifts all lockouts (that occur on too many failed login attempts) for all users with a same or lower priority ranking.
The table below is used to configure inputs for the Inception Controller.

- The hardware point refers to the physical connectors on the Inception Controller. These connectors are labelled on the module, therefore each hardware point in the list has a matching connector on the controller.

- Each input hardware point on the controller can have its functionality configured. Although traditionally these have only been able to be used for the purpose labelled on the module, Inception gives you the flexibility to define the purpose per input. EOL resistors are required for all input behaviours except REX/REN and Generic Switch/Button.
- This is the default behaviour for an input, and if not changed the input will not appear in other configuration or status pages.

- Configures the input as a reed switch and once set you will need to link this input to a door. This input behaviour allows the system to know if a door is currently open or closed. The system can use this state to determine if a user went through a door after requesting access and, along with the state of the door lock output, determine if the door has been held open too long (DOTL) or has been forced open.

- Configures the input as a tongue/bond sense and once set you will need to link this input to a door. The tongue/bond sense provides similar functionality as the reed above, allowing knowledge of whether the door is closed securely.

- Configures the input as a request to exit or request to enter button and once set you will need to link this input to a door. This input would normally have a button wired that when shorted will cause the linked door to unlock temporarily. This input should not have EOL resistors.

- Configures the input as a breakglass and once set you will need to link this input to a door. Emergency breakglass is often wired directly into the door's power circuit, allowing the door to be unlocked in case of emergency. This however bypasses the lock output state so even if the controller says the door is locked, the door can actually be opened. By wiring the breakglass detector into the system and linking it to a door, the door will be marked as unlocked and identify the cause as the breakglass. A warning is also created, prompting that the breakglass should be fixed and that the door is not secure.

- Configures the input as a standard security detector input such as PIR. Once this behaviour is selected you will not be able to link this input to a door. Instead, this is just a normal EOL input that can be programmed into an area to monitor its state changes.

- Configures the input as a standard input for buttons or switches such as a duress point. Once this behaviour is selected you will not be able to link this input to a door. Instead, this is just a normal two state switch or button input that can be used for security or as a trigger for an automated action.

- Configures the input as a reader tamper input and once set you will need to link this input to a door. This input would normally be connected to the readers tamper wire to indicate when the reader has been tampered with. By using this behaviour, the module will monitor the reader for tampers and if detected, the module will report the alarm if alarm reporting is configured. This input should not be wired with EOL resistors and assumes that the tamper type is normally open. If the tamper type is actually normally closed, the
option can be set for this input on the
page. If EOL resistors are desired, the
option above can be selected and the input can be manually monitored.

- If the behaviour of the input is set to an access control function such as door reed, tongue/bond sense, REX, REN or break glass, then the linked door option must be set. The linked door is used to determine which door should be affected by the information from the input. The linked door option is not available if the input behaviour is set to generic detectors or generic switch/buttons as these behaviours do not relate to access control.

- The input will automatically be named based on the name of the controller and the input's behaviour. If entering a name it is recommended to use a naming convention that identifies the input behaviour and the linked door.
The table below is used to configure inputs for the Standard LAN Access Module.

- The hardware point refers to the physical connectors on the Standard LAN Access Module. These connectors are labelled on the module, therefore each hardware point in the list has a matching connector on the SLAM.

- Each input hardware point on the SLAM can have its functionality configured. Although traditionally these have only been able to be used for the purpose labelled on the module, Inception gives you the flexibility to define the purpose per input. EOL resistors are required for all input behaviours except REX/REN and Generic Switch/Button.

- Configures the input as a breakglass and once set you will need to link this input to a door. Emergency breakglass is often wired directly into the door's power circuit, allowing the door to be unlocked in case of emergency. This however bypasses the lock output state so even if the controller says the door is locked, the door can actually be opened. By wiring the breakglass detector into the system and linking it to a door, the door will be marked as unlocked and identify the cause as the breakglass. A warning will also be created, prompting that the breakglass should be fixed and that the door is not secure.

- Configures the input as a standard security detector input such as a PIR. Once this behaviour is selected you will not be able to link this input to a door. Instead, this is just a normal EOL input that can be programmed into an area to monitor its state changes.

- The input will automatically be named based on the name of the hardware module and the input's behaviour. If entering a name it is recommended to use a naming convention that identifies the input behaviour and the linked door.
The table below is used to configure inputs for the Intelligent LAN Access Module.

- The hardware point refers to the physical connectors on the Intelligent LAN Access Module. These connectors are labelled on the module, therefore each hardware point in the list has a matching connector on the ILAM.
The table below is used to configure inputs for this module.
- The hardware point refers to the physical connectors on the module. These connectors are labelled on the module, therefore each hardware point in the list has a matching connector on the module.
The table below is used to configure the outputs that are not controlling door locks for the Inception Controller.

- Each output hardware point on the controller can have its function configured. Although these have been traditionally only been able to be used for the purpose labelled on the module, Inception gives you the flexibility to define the purpose of each output.
- This is the default behaviour for an output, and if not changed the output will not appear in other configuration or status pages.
- Configures the output as a DOTL output, if the door is held open for longer than the

the output will turn on. This can be used to sound a buzzer or turn on a light to indicate that the door should be closed. Once set, you will need to link this output to a door. Note: For the door to generate a Held (DOTL) event the door must be linked to an input that is configured as a door reed or tongue sense.

- The output will turn on for several seconds when the door unlocks or a valid card or PIN is presented at a reader linked to the door. The output will also turn on briefly if an area on either side of the door arms successfully. Once set you will need to link this output to a door. The valid output can be wired to the valid wire on a Wiegand reader or to an indicator of your choice.

- The output will turn on for several seconds when an invalid card or pin is presented at a reader linked to the door. Once set you will need to link this output to a door. The invalid output can be wired to the invalid wire on Wiegand readers or to an indicator of your choice.
- The beeper is generally connected to an output that provides an audible tone such as the beeper in a reader. The output will be pulsed on and off for one of the following reasons:
Additional Credentials Required - two long beeps.
Door Access Denied - on for three seconds.
Arm Success (One of the areas on a side of the door armed) - 3 quick beeps.
Arm Failure (One of the areas on a side of the door failed to arm) - On for 3 seconds.
Area State Notification (Area in Arm Warning, Entry or Exit) - Continuous beeping

- Configures the output on the controller as a general purpose output that is not related to access control. This output behaviour allows the output to be used for intruder detection purposes such as a strobe or siren output, or for automation such as lighting control.
- The output will match the state of the door lock output. Can be wired into a light to indicate whether the door is locked or unlocked. The output will be on if the door is unlocked.
- The output will match the state of the area on the inside of the door. Can be wired into a light to indicate whether the area is locked or unlocked. The output will be on if the area is armed.
- The output will match the state of the area on the outside of the door. Can be wired into a light to indicate whether the area is locked or unlocked. The output will be on if the area is armed.

- If the behaviour of the output is set to an access control function such as DOTL, valid, invalid or beeper, then the linked door option must be set. The linked door option is used to determine which door the outputs are associated with and controlled by. The linked door option is not available if the output behaviour is set to generic output as this does not relate to access control.

- The output will automatically be named based on the name of the controller and the output's behaviour. If entering a name it is recommended to use a naming convention that identifies the module, output behaviour and linked door.
The table below is used to configure the outputs that are not controlling door locks for the Standard LAN Access Module.

- Each output hardware point on the SLAM can have its function configured. Although these have been traditionally only been able to be used for the purpose labelled on the module, Inception gives you the flexibility to define the purpose of each output.

- Configures the output on the SLAM as a general purpose output that is not related to access control. This output behaviour allows the output to be used for intruder detection purposes such as a strobe or siren output, or for automation such as lighting control. Note: The SLAM outputs are limited in the amount of current they can switch, please refer to the SLAM installation manual for the contact ratings.

- The output will automatically be named based on the name of the Standard LAN Access Module and the output's behaviour. If entering a name it is recommended to use a naming convention that identifies the module, output behaviour and linked door.

- If selected the input will function as a standard input for buttons or switches such as a duress point. This setting associates a single area to this LCD terminal for the purpose of status indication and convenience.
When navigating to the Control Area menu, this area will be focused by default if multiple areas are configured in the system

When logged off, this terminal will beep with feedback messages to indicate that the area has an active alarm, that entry or exit delay is active or that the area is about to automatically arm. If a user logs on when one of these feedback messages are active, the terminal will automatically take them to the Control Area menu to allow quick control.

In addition, associating an area to the terminal allows that area to be armed and disarmed without needing to navigate to the Control Area menu. When logging on to the terminal, instead of pressing the
button after entering the user PIN, the
button can be pressed instead to arm or disarm the area directly. For example, to disarm the associated area a user could press
as a shortcut, minimising the key presses required.
This setting determines which areas will be displayed in the Control Area menu of the LCD terminal.
If there are areas assigned to the list, the Control Area menu will be filtered to display only those ones; if the list is empty, there will be no filter applied to the list of areas.

This feature can be useful in systems with many areas, for example, by filtering to only the nearby areas for a given LCD terminal. When a user accesses the terminal to control an area, they will only see the most relevant areas for that terminal, rather than having to scroll through every area in the system.
Note: This setting also affects the
setting. If there are any areas included in this list, the
must also be included in the list.
This setting associates a door to the LCD Terminal. When a user logs into this LCD Terminal and presses the
) they will be granted access to the associated door, if they have the appropriate user permissions.
Once associated with a door, the
option below must be set before this LCD terminal can be used for access control.
This LCD terminal will also beep if the door is Held Open (reed or tongue switch is held open when the door is locked for a duration that exceeds the door's
Determines the location of the LCD Terminal in relation to the
. This is required if you intend to use this terminal for PIN access, or the
button as a logged off access button. This also allows a user's location to be updated when used for PIN access with an area on the other side of the door.
Note, to allow PIN access, ensure the door is configured to allow PIN access from the side of the door that the terminal is located on
button to be used as a Request Access button for the

when the terminal is logged off. This option is useful in situations where terminals may already be in proximity to a door, and an exit button / enter button is required. This feature avoids the need to install a separate access button.
If enabled, the LCD terminal will be restricted to access control functions only, such as PIN access for entry/exit or logged off
This option prevents all users from logging into the LCD Terminal to perform any other tasks, for example, make programming changes, arm areas or control outputs.
Note: This option should only be enabled if the LCD terminal is used solely for access control or there is potentially a security issue due to the LCD terminal being located in a public place.

If enabled, the LCD Terminal will be able to alert users about the presence of System Messages and System Warnings. This allows Inception to attract the users attention when it has new information to display, or as a reminder that there are still issues to resolve.

Alert feedback will be provided via the LCD Terminal's beeper and LEDs. If Inception needs to alert users, the internal beeper of the LCD Terminal will sound and any LEDs that are not showing an items state (configured in the

Alert feedback will only be allowed if the system is disarmed. If there is an area associated with the terminal (implying the terminals location), then alert feedback will be allowed if that area is disarmed. Otherwise, all areas in the system must be disarmed.

Two different types of alert feedback can occur. When a new Warning or Message is created, the terminal will beep continuously with the LEDs flashing fast. Once a user has viewed the Messages and Warnings (i.e by viewing the System Event menu of the LCD terminal, or by viewing the

web page), the terminal will beep for 1 second every two minutes to remind the user that there are still warnings or messages to process. Note that a user can only view the System Event menu of the LCD terminal if their

In addition, if a Message or Warning will persist for some time, alert feedback for any currently present events can be muted by pressing OFF in the System Event menu of the LCD Terminal (and ON to unmute), or via the
page. If a new Message or Warning is generated while others are muted, then standard alert feedback would still occur.

Not all Warnings or Messages trigger alert feedback. In general, Warnings and Messages relating to hardware health issues, reporting path issues and input fault issues trigger alert feedback. Also, temporarily isolated inputs and unauthorized level 3 user logins will trigger alert feedback.
These options allow the state of areas, doors or outputs to be mapped to the LCD terminal LEDs.
- LED on for area armed, off for disarmed. LED flashing fast for area in alarm, flashing slowly for area entry, exit and arming warning states
- LED on for door locked, off for unlocked. LED flashing fast for door forced, flashing slowly for door held (DOTL)
- LED on for output on, off for output off.
option is used to reverse the states that turn the LEDs on and off.

If configured, the Cabinet Tamper input on the terminal will be monitored by the system and will be included in the overall system health monitoring. The system will communicate this hardware event to a monitoring station if alarm reporting is enabled and correctly configured. In addition, all areas will go into alarm when a tamper is detected, regardless of whether the area is armed or not and will need to be disarmed to stop strobes and sirens sounding.

If selected, a normally closed detection device or switch / button can be wired to the LCD Terminal. The LCD terminal input does not support end of line resistors and therefore can not identify the tamper state of a detector, it is therefore not recommended to use this input for security purposes. This input is commonly used for automation such as a button that performs an automated action.
The input is wired to the GND and X3/Z01 connector on the rear of the LCD Terminal.

Once configured, this input needs to be programmed into an area to monitor its state changes or used as a trigger for automation.
The input is wired to the GND and X3/Z02 connector on the rear of the LCD Terminal.

If selected, an open collector output can be wired to the LCD Terminal. This output is a general purpose output and can be used for automation and security functionality. The output can switch a maximum of 100mA, if you need to switch a larger current it is recommended to use this output to switch a relay.
The output is wired to the +VE and X1 connector on the rear of the LCD Terminal.
The name of this output that will be used throughout the system
The output is wired to the +VE and AX2 connector on the rear of the LCD Terminal.

Each floor hardware point on the Lift Interface card has a relay contact to control a lift button, as well as a sense input to detect when the button has been pressed. See the installation manual for the Unibus 16-Floor Lift Interface Expander for more information about wiring these components.

The tables below allow configuring of the floor inputs and outputs for the Lift Interface cards that have been connected. Up to 6 Lift Interface cards can be connected via the unibus interface, allowing up to 96 floor points to be configured.

- The hardware point refers to the physical connectors on the 16-Floor Lift Interface Expander. These connectors are labelled on the module, therefore each hardware point in the list has a matching connector on the Lift Interface cards.
- Each hardware point can be configured as either
. If wired, an input and an output will be automatically created for that hardware point. The floors that aren't are not wired will not have anything generated.

- The input and output will automatically be named based on the name of the hardware module and the hardware point. If entering a name, it is recommended to use a naming convention that identifies the hardware point and the device that is connected. The input that is generated will have
appended to the name and the output will have

If enabled, SIFER or OSDP readers connected to the module will be monitored by the system for tampers. The SIFER reader, for example, detects a physical tamper using an optical beam to surface it is mounted on.

If a reader tamper occurs, the system will communicate this hardware event to a monitoring station is alarm reporting if enabled and correctly configured. When reporting the event, the system will include the door's name that the reader is connected to, to help in identifying where the event occurred. In addition, all areas will go into alarm, regardless of whether the area is armed or not and will need to be disarmed to stop strobes and sirens sounding.

If configured, the Cabinet Tamper on the module will be monitored by the system and will be included in the overall system health monitoring. The system will communicate this hardware event to a monitoring station if alarm reporting is enabled and correctly configured. In addition, all areas will go into alarm when a tamper is detected, regardless of whether the area is armed or not and will need to be disarmed to stop strobes and sirens sounding.

If configured, the Cabinet Tamper input on the module will be monitored by the system and will be included in the overall system health monitoring. This option determines the type of Cabinet Tamper switch that is connected to the hardware module.
- The tamper switch is open circuit when sealed/closed.
- The tamper switch is closed circuit when sealed/closed.
Note: Inner Range enclosures are supplied with

If configured, the system will communicate this hardware event to a monitoring station if alarm reporting is enabled and correctly configured. In addition, all areas will go into alarm when a tamper is detected, regardless of whether the area is armed or not and will need to be disarmed to stop strobes and sirens sounding.

If enabled, the module will be monitored by the system for RF Interference events and this event will be included in the overall health monitoring of the system. The system will communicate this hardware event to a monitoring station if alarm reporting is enabled and correctly configured.

Enabling Card Cache will ensure stored user card credentials on the Access Control Module will be valid after the Module goes offline. Disabling the Card Cache will block valid user credentials while the Access Control Module stays offline. By default the Card Cache is enabled.

If enabled, the Internal Siren driver (S1INT) on the module will be monitored for tampers and will be included in the overall system health monitor. To detect siren tampers, the siren devices need to be wired with a normally closed tamper switch.

Note, if enabled, the system will communicate this hardware event to a monitoring station if alarm reporting is enabled and correctly configured. In addition, all areas will go into alarm when a tamper is detected, regardless of whether the area is armed or not and will need to be disarmed to stop strobes and sirens sounding.

If enabled, the Internal Siren driver will operate in DC driven mode, rather than driving the siren to make specific tones. This option allows a piezo screamer to be plugged directly into the siren driver point, rather than wired into an actual relay.

If enabled, the External Siren driver (S2EXT) on the module will be monitored for tampers and will be included in the overall system health monitor. To detect siren tampers, the siren devices need to be wired with a normally closed tamper switch.

If enabled, the External Siren driver will operate in DC driven mode, rather than driving the siren to make specific tones. This option allows a piezo screamer to be plugged directly into the siren driver point, rather than wired into an actual relay.
Power Supply Connected to DC IN

When enabled, the supplied Inception 24V power supply will be monitored for AC Fail in the overall system health monitor. Only enable this option if the Inception controller is being powered via the DC IN supply.
The Inception Controller will report AC Fail events to a monitoring station if alarm reporting is enabled and correctly configured if this option is enabled and the DC power supply fails.

When enabled, the connected battery will be monitored for Low Battery and Missing Battery in the overall system health monitor. This option also allows the configuration of battery testing options below. The Inception Controller will report these hardware events to a monitoring station if alarm reporting is enabled and correctly configured, should the battery run low or a battery test is failed.

This option should only be used if there is an actual battery connected to the BATT connector of the controller. If the controller is being powered from an external 12-14V power supply into the BATT connector, this option should not be enabled.
If enabled, the external power supply's AC Fail, LAN Fuse Blown and Power Supply Failure states will be included in the overall system health monitor.
Note: Only enable this option if this module is connected to an Inner Range 3/8Amp Smart Power Supply via the 10pin PSU patch lead.
The Inception Controller will report these hardware events to a monitoring station if alarm reporting is enabled and correctly configured, should any alarm conditions occur with this power supply.
Battery connected to Integriti SMART Power Supply

If enabled, the battery connected to the external power supply will be monitored for the Low Battery condition by the overall system health monitor. Regular battery tests can also be performed to verify the battery's condition.
Note: Only enable this option if this module is connected to an Inner Range 3/8Amp Smart Power Supply via the 10pin PSU patch lead and there is a battery attached to that power supply.
The Inception Controller will report these hardware events to a monitoring station if alarm reporting is enabled and correctly configured, should the battery run low or a battery test is failed.
If enabled, along with the power supply and the battery options above, the module will be included in the systems scheduled battery testing.

Battery testing is required to ensure that in the event of AC failure, each module can be powered from its attached battery. The regular testing of the battery ensures that if a battery fails over time it is detected and can be replaced.
below, only allow this module to be added to the list of modules to test. When the tests occur and how often testing is performed is configured on the

page. When the scheduled time occurs, all modules configured for battery testing will be added to a queue. Modules are tested one at a time so that if there is an AC fail mid test, only part of the system is vulnerable, not all of it. Testing will only start if the module is online, AC power is present, the battery state is not low or missing and no short is detected.

A battery test passes if no issues are detected for the duration of the test. If a battery issue is detected during a battery test, the monitoring station will be notified about it and a warning will appear on the web interface. A module with a failed battery test state must pass a battery test to clear it. This can happen with the next scheduled test, or a test can be manually run on the

Battery tests can abort for a number of reasons, a user can cancel the test, AC power was lost on the power supply, a wire was shorted, comms to the module was lost or the module needed to sound sirens. An aborted test is neither a pass or fail and nothing is reported to the monitoring station.
Determines the duration of time that the module's battery will be tested.

The duration of the battery test should be determined by the average current consumption of the module and the capacity of the connected battery, as well as the minimum amount of time that you want the system to remain powered in the event of AC power failure.

The table below is used to configure outputs for the 8 Input Expander
- Each output hardware point on the 8 Input Expander can have its functionality set one of the options below.
Sets the type of reader that is connected to the module. The other options on this page are based on the Reader Type chosen.
Note, for Wiegand readers, the reader configured in each slot (e.g
) should match the readers on the Wiegand reader ports on the module. SIFER/OSDP readers, on the other hand, can be in any order.

This is the unique identification number that is hard coded to the SIFER/IR Reader. In most cases the serial number will automatically appear once a SIFER/IR reader is detected and there is a free reader location. Otherwise, the serial can be found on the back of the reader itself, the SIFER packaging or on
page when connecting the SIFER Reader.

This is the OSDP Address that the Reader currently uses to communicate with the host module. It must be unique between the readers connected to the same module. In most cases the OSDP Address will automatically appear once an OSDP reader is detected and there is a free reader location. Otherwise, it can be manually entered if known, or on
page when connecting the OSDP Reader.

Readers can be configured to arm one or more areas when a user presents their card at the reader three times in a row in short succession. A user can only arm areas that they have permission to arm and the arm may fail if the area contains unsealed inputs. If this occurs, the user should use a more advanced interface to try and arm, such as the LCD Terminal or the web interface.
Arm Area on the Other Side of the Door
- The area configured on the other side of the door from this reader will arm. Exit delay will not be triggered.

- This will arm all areas currently configured in the system, that the user has permission to arm. Any areas that a user does not have permission to arm will not be armed. Exit delay will not be triggered.
Arm Area on the Same Side of the Door
- The area configured on the same side of the door as this reader will arm. This will also begin Exit delay for the area.

The SIFER/OSDP reader can provide visual and audible feedback for the state of an area or door. This option determines which item, if any, to provide feedback for. In addition, an LED on the reader can be used to show that item's state, instead of a static colour. For SIFER readers, this is the small LED. For OSDP readers, this is the only LED. If configured, the colour of the status LED is determined by the item it is following and can't be manually set, which overrides the status LED field below (
- The status LED will default to the color selected in the field below (
for OSDP readers). When a user badges a card, the status LED will turn green if access is granted and red if access is denied.
- The status LED will turn red if the door that this reader is linked to is locked and turn green when unlocked.

- The SIFER will provide feedback on the state on the area located on the same side of the door as the reader. The reader will beep with different tones to indicate an alarm in the area, entry delay, exit delay and auto-arm warning. The small LED will turn red if the area is armed and will turn green when the area is disarmed.
option above, but for the area located on the other side of the door to the reader.
Note, both LED brightness and feedback volume for SIFER readers can be adjusted on the

Note, the LED brightness can be adjusted on the
Sets the colour of the small LED on the SIFER reader. This only applies if the above

Wiegand keypad readers allow the entering of a user's PIN number in order to request access through a door. Different keypads send through PIN numbers in different ways, this option specifies how to process the key presses. If the type of keypad connected is not listed, try each of the available options anyway, as it may match a known type.
A user can only request access through a door if that door's
is set to a mode that uses PIN.
The table below is used to configure inputs for the RF Expander Module
The IR-Paradox RF Module supports up to 32 RF input detector devices.
- The hardware point refers to the 32 RF inputs that can be used on the IR-Paradox RF Module.
- Each RF Input hardware point on the IR-Paradox RF Module can have its functionality set to one of the four options below.
- This is the default behaviour for an RF input and left unchanged the RF input will not appear in other configuration or status pages.
- If selected the input will function as a motion detector, this is used if the device you are connecting is a Wireless PIR.

- If selected the input will function as a door/window contact, glassbreak detector or asset protector. This is used if the device you are connecting is a wireless door reed, a wireless glassbreak detector or a wireless asset protector
- If selected the input will function as a smoke detector, this is used if the device you are connecting is a wireless smoke detector.

- This is the six-digit serial number of the RF input, often located on the back or inside of the detector. If there is no number on the detector, you can identify the serial number by opening up the detector, waiting for its transmit LED to stop flashing and then pressing and releasing the tamper detection switch. You can then navigate to the
page and find the most recent
event, this event will contain the serial number. See the detector's instructions for more information about learn mode.

- The input will automatically be named based on the name of the RF module and the hardware point. If entering a name, it is recommended to use a naming convention that identifies the hardware point and the device that is connected.
The Inovonics RF Module supports up to 32 RF input detector devices.
- The hardware point refers to the 32 RF inputs that can be used on the Inovonics RF Module.

- If selected the input will function as a standard alarm detector. Device alarm and tampering will be monitored. This can be used with most transmitter devices like motion, smoke and glassbreak detectors. In addition, RF remotes could also be used if they are mounted as push-button inputs for wireless button inputs or user duress.

- Certain Inovonics detectors are capable of reporting multiple alarms, this option is used to access the secondary alarm of the detector. This would be used, for example, with dual-input detectors that allow a separate device to be wired into the transmitter.

- If selected, the input will represent the state of a repeater. The input will not go into the alarm state, however it will enter the tamper state if the repeater is tampered with, as well as monitoring for failed polls and low battery information.

- This is the eight-digit serial number of the RF input, often located on the back or inside of the detector. If there is no number on the detector, you can identify the serial number by opening up the detector and pressing the learn button. You can then navigate to the
The following options will enable calculated inputs that can be manually triggered from the touchscreen using an Emergency button.

Enabling any of the emergency buttons will cause the Emergency button to be shown on the Touchscreen's main screen, which will open a menu with the enabled options when tapped. It will also automatically create a calculated input for this touchscreen associated with that emergency button. The calculated input will be pulsed when the button on the touchscreen's Emergency menu is activated by a user.
To set up monitoring and reporting of these events, the calculated inputs should be added to an area using the Edit Areas or Area Input Map pages, using an appropriate input process group.
If configured, a button will be enabled on the touchscreen interface to allow users to trigger a fire alert. When activated, it will trigger a calculated input in Inception.
If configured, a button will be enabled on the touchscreen interface to allow users to trigger an ambulance alert. When activated, it will trigger a calculated input in Inception.
If configured, a button will be enabled on the touchscreen interface to allow users to trigger a police alert. When activated, it will trigger a calculated input in Inception.
This setting determines which areas will be displayed on the Touchscreen terminal.
If there are areas assigned to the list, the Touchscreen will be filtered to display only those ones; if the list is empty, there will be no filter applied to the list of areas.

This feature can be useful in systems with many areas, for example, by filtering to only the nearby areas for a given Touchscreen terminal. When a user accesses the terminal to control an area, they will only see the most relevant areas for that terminal, rather than having to scroll through every area in the system.
This setting determines which doors will be displayed on the Touchscreen terminal.
If there are doors assigned to the list, the Touchscreen will be filtered to display only those ones; if the list is empty, there will be no filter applied to the list of doors.

This feature can be useful in systems with many doors, for example, by filtering to only the nearby doors for a given Touchscreen terminal. When a user accesses the terminal to control an doors, they will only see the most relevant doors for that terminal, rather than having to scroll through every door in the system.
This setting determines which inputs will be displayed on the Touchscreen terminal.
If there are inputs assigned to the list, the Touchscreen will be filtered to display only those ones; if the list is empty, there will be no filter applied to the list of inputs.

This feature can be useful in systems with many inputs, for example, by filtering to only the nearby inputs for a given Touchscreen terminal. When a user accesses the terminal to control an input, they will only see the most relevant inputs for that terminal, rather than having to scroll through every input in the system.
This setting determines which outputs will be displayed on the Touchscreen terminal.
If there are outputs assigned to the list, the Touchscreen will be filtered to display only those ones; if the list is empty, there will be no filter applied to the list of outputs.

This feature can be useful in systems with many outputs, for example, by filtering to only the nearby outputs for a given Touchscreen terminal. When a user accesses the terminal to control an output, they will only see the most relevant outputs for that terminal, rather than having to scroll through every output in the system.
This setting determines which Hardware Modules will be displayed on the Touchscreen terminal.
If there are modules assigned to the list, the Touchscreen will be filtered to display only those ones; if the list is empty, there will be no filter applied to the list of modules.

This feature can be useful in systems with many modules, for example, by filtering to only the nearby modules for a given Touchscreen terminal. When a user accesses the terminal to view a module state, they will only see the most relevant modules for that terminal, rather than having to scroll through every module in the system.

This dialog allows you to download a Contact ID Commissioning Report containing all user and area reporting IDs, as well as all input and module states that can be sent to a monitoring station.
contains a list of everything that can be reported. A
will show a list of all updates and deletions since a previous report was run, then the full report afterwards.
This can be useful for a monitoring station to update their mappings for this site.
Create all of the Areas on your site and give them names. Remember to include an 'Outside' area if you have access control on site and you wish to track user locations.
Select the Inputs that are located on the Entry/Exit Path.

The entry/exit path is required when an LCD Terminal that allows area control is located within an armed area. This lets the terminal be protected, however a user still needs to be able to access the terminal to arm and disarm the area. A primary entry point can be designated which if triggered first, allows a user to move through the armed area to the LCD Terminal, forming the entry/exit path. The path is created using the security inputs, where the inputs that protect the path through the area are programmed differently. This lets a user enter through a specific door and walk a specific path to the terminal to disarm, or arm the area from the terminal and walk along the path to exit the area. Any deviation from this path causes an alarm to be triggered.
Assign the inputs that should be triggered first at the primary entry point as

. These start the entry timer and put the area in entry mode, allowing the user to walk along the path without triggering an alarm. This is generally going to be a door reed or a PIR near an entry door.
All other inputs which cover the entry/exit path should be assigned as

. While the exit delay or entry delay timer is active, Primary and Handover Inputs are ignored, allowing a user to walk along the path. If a Handover input is triggered before a Primary Input on the entry path (that is, the user bypassed the primary entry point), then the area entry time will be ignored and the area will go into alarm instantly.

In entry mode, an alarm will be triggered if the area is not disarmed before the entry time expires or if the user leaves the path and triggers an instant alarm input. Similarly, in exit mode, an alarm will be triggered if the user leaves the path and triggers an instant alarm input.

Generally the first input that will be triggered when a user enters the building via the primary entry point. These inputs will start the entry delay, allowing the user to disarm the system from a keypad or reader without generating an area alarm. Without a primary input being triggered first, the handover inputs would be treated the same as intruder inputs causing the area to go into alarm.
To assign an input to this area as a primary input, click the
button, this will open a window that contains all the available inputs. Once an input is added, it will automatically be assigned the

These are secondary inputs that are triggered as the user moves through the entry/exit path to disarm the area via a keypad or reader. If a primary input is triggered first and starts the entry delay, the activations on these inputs will be ignored for the duration of the entry delay. If the area entry delay is not triggered, the handover inputs will immediately generate an area alarm. These inputs should include most of the inputs that cover the entry/exit path, so if an intruder bypasses the primary entry point the alarm will sound as soon as possible.
To assign an input to this area as a handover input, click the
Select all the other inputs that are monitored by this area that aren't part of the Entry/Exit Path.
When the area is armed, the intruder inputs when triggered will cause the area to go into alarm instantly, the intruder inputs are generally located outside of the primary entry/exit path.
If the area monitors additional inputs that aren't used for intruder detection, such as a panic button or automation switch, you will need to add the input to the

section. Once assigned, you will be able to select a process group to determine how the area will respond to the input state changes, this will determine the activation of sirens and the reporting of alarms. These different inputs may trigger alarms or sirens even if the area is disarmed.

These are the inputs that will immediately generate an area alarm. The intruder inputs should not include inputs that are located on the primary entry/exit path of the building. These inputs will generally be located on doors and in sections of the building that are accessed once the area is disarmed.
To assign an input to this area as an intruder input, click the
button, this will open a window that contains all the available inputs. Once an input is added, it will automatically be assigned the Instant Alarm input type (process group).
Other inputs and devices that are not related to intruder detection but nevertheless require processing or monitoring by an area. To assign an input to this area as an

button, this will open a window that contains all the available inputs. Once an input is selected you will be able to manually select an input type, it is recommended that you do not select primary, handover or instant alarm process groups.

A Walk test can be started and stopped for the selected area using the buttons below. Once started, the inputs will automatically move from the

While a walk test is active, input state processing is disabled, allowing emergency response inputs like user duress buttons, emergency buttons and smoke detectors to be tested without sounding alarms.

However, input processing is only disabled for the area(s) running the walk test, so if inputs are included in multiple areas then the alarm response could still occur. In this case, it may be worth testing multiple areas at once.
A test will automatically complete when all inputs in that area have been successfully tested. If there are some inputs that can't be tested, then the test can be cancelled early with the
button below. Progress of a running test can be seen from multiple devices simultaneously if required.
Activate each of the inputs in the list below to test them. Once tested, they will automatically move to the 'Passed' list.

Areas, Hardware, Intruder Detection and Lifts

This step is used to configure network settings and the method of connection to the Inception web interface. The default network settings are Ethernet using DHCP, if required you can set up the Ethernet connection with a static IP address.

If you have purchased the Inception Wi-Fi Adapter, you can connect the controller to the site's wireless network or connect directly using a Wireless Hotspot. It is recommended to leave this option enabled and set to Wireless Hotspot if you are not leaving a Wi-Fi adapter on site, but will use one in future to aid in servicing this Inception Controller.

You can enable Skytunnel if a remote connection from a device outside of the local network to the Inception Controller is required. Skytunnel provides a hassle free connection that does not need port forwarding. Skytunnel is a cloud-based service and requires internet access for the Inception Controller and the connecting device.
Configure the Ethernet network settings for DHCP or static IP.
Configure the Wi-Fi settings if installing a Wi-Fi Adapter on site.

This step is for the setup of the date and time settings, once completed the controller time displayed should match the time at the panel’s location. It is important to set the date and time accurately to ensure that time periods and calendar events occur at the correct time. Time periods can be used to trigger a number of scheduled actions in the system, for example,

It is recommended to configure the controller to synchronize with an NTP server this will ensure that the date and time are always correct. The NTP server can be located on the local network or the internet.

You will need to set the date and time manually if the network the controller is connected to does not have access to an NTP server. Note: If you manually set the date and time you will also need to manually correct Daylight Savings time changes for your location.
Configure the NTP server and time zone settings, or set date and time manually if no NTP server is available.
This step is used to configure settings such as the user PIN length, End of Line and
. You can select a minimum and a maximum length for user PINs. The default PIN length is two to four digits, however, the site may require longer PINs as they are more secure.
If there is a chance that users onsite will be put in a duress situation, you can enable the
feature. This involves creating a PIN that all users can enter into a terminal or keypad while under duress to silently notify the monitoring station about the duress situation. The

must also be assigned permissions, these should allow the user under duress to be able to access the items they usually access without raising suspicion, but not allow too much access as everyone should know this PIN.

If you are replacing an existing security system, you can avoid changing the End of Line resistors for every detection device by selecting a matching End of Line config. If you are installing a new system, it is recommended to use the default 2k2 + 2k2 or 2k2 + 6k8 as these match the resistors shipped with Inner Range products.

can be chosen in order to meet the local security standards. EN50131 and BS8243 are the two standards that can currently be chosen, which allows the system to show additional options or features specific to those standards.
Configure the user PIN length, Duress User and End of Line settings.
If in Europe, select the Compliance Mode to use if required (e.g EN50131 for European installations).
This step is used to create and name all the areas that will monitor inputs in the system. This involves just creating the areas to allow them to be used in the next
step, the actual configuration of areas is done later on in the

You can program the system with a single area or multiple areas. The benefit of a single area is the entire system is easy to arm and disarm. However, creating multiple areas allows you to divide the site and limit user access to certain areas, not every user will require access to the area like the Server Room.
If you are using the Inception System for access control, it is recommended to have separate areas on the inside and outside of doors to take advantage of features like
You should create additional areas if you want to group inputs that have a common purpose such as all the smoke detectors within the building.
Create and name all the areas for the site.

This step is used to configure the Inception Controller and LAN modules. This will include the programming of inputs, outputs, doors and readers based on the type of module, as well as configuring module behaviour and which module health components have been utilized (for example, cabinet tampers, siren tampers, smart powersupplies, external battery).

Inputs, outputs and readers are only created in the system once the module is configured. When configuring a module, you will be stepped through multiple sections of programming. These steps will depend on the type of module being configured. This step is complete when all the modules appear in the
If you want to configure a module that you have not yet purchased or attached to the system, you can create it by clicking the

If the system is used for intruder detection, this three-part step maps the inputs to the areas for monitoring, configures the areas, then tests the strobes and sirens to ensure their configuration is correct.
The first part is to map the inputs to the areas you created. The
page lets you add inputs to one of four categories. Three of these categories relate to intruder detection and will automatically assign the correct
, these are devices that are not related to intruder detection but still require processing or monitoring by the area.
The second part is the area configuration where you can set the area's
functionality. The options set for each area will vary based on the area's purpose.

The third part allows the testing of area strobes and sirens, which will confirm that the physical sirens and strobes are wired correctly and that the area options that determine which sirens sound are configured correctly.

If the system is used for lift integration, this involves the creation of Lift Floors and Lift Cars, the two main components in the integration.

Essentially, low-level lift integration involves wiring of outputs in the Inception system to lift buttons in a lift car, allowing the individual buttons to be enabled or disabled by the Inception system. Then, a user is able to present their card at a reader located in the lift car and based on their permissions, certain floor selection buttons can be enabled. In addition, the lift buttons can also be wired into Inputs in the Inception system, allowing Inception to know which button a user pressed.

The first step is to create Lift Floors. Lift Floors represent a destination that a button in a lift car would take the user to, and multiple lift cars may have a button that can take the user to the same lift floor. In some cases there may be multiple lift floors created for the same physical floor in a building, for example if the lift car has two doors and there are different buttons for 7A and 7B. In this case, different floors can be created with different permissions for each side of the car. Alternatively, if a particular lift car needs its access to be restricted to only certain users, for example a service elevator, there could be normal floors created for the other cars, along with another set of service floors for the service Lift Car, with only maintenance workers having permission to those floors.

The second step involves the creation of the Lift Cars. These represent a physical lift car or button panel that the user interacts with. These contain one or more readers for the user to present their card or PIN at, as well as a number of lift buttons to be enabled or disabled by the Inception system. Each lift button requires both a Lift Floor for the button destination and an output to enable the button on the panel. Optionally, an input can also be specified that would indicate when the button has been pressed.
No, lift integration is not needed
Is Lift Integration being used on this site?
Create the Lift Floors for the site. Floors can be shared between multiple Lift Cars.
Create the Lift Cars for the site.
You have said that no lift integration is necessary for this site. If this isn't the case, reset the step and answer Yes
This step is used to create and configure time periods that are used in later steps such as

Time periods are based on weekly recurring times, where the time periods are generally valid at the same times each week. Exceptions to these rules can be added to allow temporary changes to be made to the schedule without significant programming changes.

The default time period is called Working Hours; this time period is commonly used to trigger events to occur during a typical working day, such as internal doors unlocking at the start of the work day or turning on an output that controls lighting. This is only an example and can be edited to match the hours of operation for the site or removed.

Consider what items in the system should be scheduled and how they might be grouped together. You may need to create multiple time periods to handle these cases, such as when door free access should be allowed, when areas should automatically arm in the evenings, any scheduled automation required and when user permissions should be allowed or limited.
Create and configure all the time periods for the site.
This step is used to configure

uses time periods and area states to unlock the door and provides a user access to a door without presenting a credential. This can be used to unlock internal doors when the inside area of the door is disarmed during working hours. The door will relock when a user arms the inside area or at the end of the day when the time period goes invalid.
feature with a time period to ensure that the area is armed by a certain time each night, preventing the area from accidentally being left disarmed overnight. The
will not disarm the area when the time period goes valid.

option uses time periods and area states to automatically set the floor into free access, allowing a user to access the floor in a lift car without presenting a credential. This can be used to allow access to a floor during certain times of the day, but only if the area on that floor is disarmed. The floor will re-secure when a user arms the area on that floor or when the time period goes invalid.

This step is used to select which outputs can be controlled by users from the web interface or an LCD Terminal.

allows them to be manually controlled by a user, however they still need to be given permission to control that output. Once assigned to the user's permission they will be able to control the output from the Inception web interface or the LCD Terminal, as well as see it's state. You can also configure an output to allow
, this allows any person to control the output from any LCD Terminal on-site without needing to enter a security PIN. Note: the LCD Terminal must be configured for
It is recommended that only outputs that should be controlled manually by users should be marked as
. These are generally automation related, for example lighting, water sprinklers or airconditioning control.
Go to the User Controllable Outputs Page
Select the outputs that users can control.
Choose Outputs that are User Controllable
This step is used to create the permission groups that will be assigned to one or more users to determine the doors, areas and outputs the users will be able to access or control.

Permission groups are useful as they can include a common set of permissions that can then be assigned to multiple users. Once assigned, the permissions for all of those users can be updated by changing the permission group directly, making permission maintenance easy. When creating permission groups, it is recommended to first identify what is common between a number of users. This will generally be role based such as IT staff, admin staff etc. However on some sites it may simply be different level of access such as basic access or management access. The permission groups would then contain all the permissions required for a worker to access the doors and control the areas and outputs for them to do their job.
It is recommended that you clearly name the permission groups based on the user’s role to allow someone else to assign them to users without needing to look at their contents.
Create permission groups containing the doors, areas and outputs for different user roles.
If the system is used for access control, this step is used to configure the types of credentials used on site.

Credential templates contain information such as how the credentials presented at readers should be interpreted (e.g. as cards, fingerprints, facial scan data) and the card format being used (if applicable). Credentials are then assigned to users by specifying a credential template and the credential number. Credential templates can be configured with a specific card format and include a site code to match cards, or can be configured to use raw card information (direct entry), which uses the full card data as the credential number.
If you intend to use site coded cards, you will need to specify the card format and the site code. To identify the card format and the site code of the cards, open the
page and badge a card at a reader.
If multiple credential types are used on-site, multiple templates can be created, or raw (direct entry) can be used.
Yes, credentials are being used on site

Is this system being used for access control with cards or other credentials?
Create a credential template that matches the format and site code of the credentials being used onsite.

This step is used to review and configure web page profiles. Web page profiles determine which pages a user is able to access via the Inception web interface, as well as what they can do on those pages. The web page profile includes permissions for
sets which areas, doors, outputs and shortcut groups appear on the user's Dashboard.
Some profiles have been created by default to cover the common types of users. If the default profiles are not suitable for the site, you will need to create new ones or modify them.
Review the existing profiles and modify as needed or create new ones if required.
Assign any areas, doors or outputs that should appear on the dashboard pages for users
This step is used to create and configure users. A user is any person that interacts with the Inception System by either badging a card, entering a pin number or logging into the web interface.

Credentials are what identify a user and must be unique between all users. Credentials include the PIN number used to access keypad readers or log into an LCD terminal, cards for access control and RF remotes for remote control. You can also provide the user a username and password to grant them access to the Inception Web Interface; the user will also require a Web Page Profile, which determines which pages they can view and how they can interact with those pages.

You will need to assign the user individual permissions or the permission groups you created in a previous step, this will determine what areas, doors and outputs a user can access or control on the system and when they are allowed to control them.
Users can also be bulk imported via CSV if a number of users need to be added
Create a new user for each person that will interact with the Inception System and assign them the appropriate credentials and permissions.
This step is used to configure the reporting of alarms and events to a monitoring station. The inception controller supports reporting via Skytunnel or with an external T4000 device.

- This uses the Inception controller's network connection with internet access to communicate with the SkyTunnel server. Once the SkyTunnel server receives the event, it is delivered to a monitoring station. This feature works out of the box and requires no other specialized hardware.

- The T4000 is an Inner Range device that connects to the Inception Controller via USB. It provides multiple redundant reporting paths using multiple 3G networks in addition to Ethernet internet reporting. With all paths polled frequently, this ensures reliable alarm monitoring.

Note: These options only enable the reporting paths. Either the Inception controller for SkyTunnel, or the T4000 must also be enrolled onto a monitoring station's Multipath IP system. Contact your monitoring station in order to set up this account.
Multiple paths can be used by marking them as either the primary path or backup path.
No, this system will not be monitored
Is this system being monitored by a monitoring station?
Configure the primary communications path for reporting to a monitoring station.
If using the Contact ID format, download and send the Contact ID report to the monitoring station.
You have said that no alarm monitoring is necessary for this site. If this isn't the case, reset the step and answer Yes

This step is used to configure the system to be able to send notification messages to users when events in the system occur. Notifiers can be configured, for example, to send notifications whenever an alarm occurs in a particular area or access to a particular door is granted or denied. Notifications can also be sent when repeated failed login attempts are made on the web interface or LCD Terminal, or if programming changes are made to particular entities. Maintenance events, such as power and battery issues of modules or RF devices can also be communicated.

Inception supports sending messages to users via email. To use the email notification path, Inception must be configured with an email server to use and the recipient users must have an email address assigned to them.
Multiple notifiers can be created to notify different users about different events at different times. Create as many notifiers as required by the system to send desired events to users.
No, this system will not use user notifications
Is this system sending event notifications to users via email?
Configure the email server settings to allow Inception to send emails.
Successfully send a test email (using the
button in the toolbox) to ensure the path works.
Create and configure the required notifiers for the system.
You have said that no user notifications are necessary for this site. If this isn't the case, reset the step and answer Yes

This step is used to configure any automation logic that is beyond the basic intruder and access control system. Automated actions provide you with the ability to solve automation scenarios for the site. These would most often be simple automation cases such as turning on the lights when the area is disarmed, but have the flexibility to handle very complicated scenarios.

Identify any automated responses that you want the system to perform and when you would want them to happen. They could be things like turning on lighting or air conditioning when the area is disarmed or when there is activity detected. They could also tie in to the security of the site, such as arming some areas when another area arms. Triggers can involve the state of multiple things in the system including time periods, so automation can also be set up to occur only during certain times. Depending on the automation required, multiple actions may need to be configured for the one task.
See the page description on the
page for more information about configuring the actions.
Configure any additional automation for the site
Select the outputs used to indicate pending alerts to level 1 users.

This step is used to change the Security PIN and web interface password for the Installer user. This change is required as the default Installer’s logins are not secure and if used by another person would provide the highest level of permission.
It is extremely important to change the Web Interface password and PIN if you have enabled the
option as the panel will be accessible via the internet. In this case it is also recommended that the username is also changed.
Change the default Installer PIN, password and username.

This step disables service mode that is enabled by default when first configuring the system. Service mode allows you to install and setup the Inception controller without generating sirens and alarm reporting. Once service mode has been disabled the system's sirens and alarm reporting will be enabled.
You can enable service mode at a later date to perform maintenance without generating sirens and alarm reporting.

Note: When disabling service mode, any current alarms will be processed, potentially sounding sirens. Similarly, if any modules are reporting tamper health issues (e.g cabinet tamper or siren tampers), it may also cause the sirens to sound.
Disable service mode to enable sirens and alarm reporting.
This step is used to create a backup to ensure the configuration and programming from all the previous steps is easily recoverable if lost.

Also, this step involves creating a programming report for the system that includes a summary of all of the programming in the system in a human readable form. As database backups are encrypted, this is intended to be a summary of all programming in the system at the end of commissioning and can aid in future maintenance or used as reference material, or verifying any programming issues at a future time.
Download a backup of the database and store in a secure location.
Backup the Database and Download the Programming Report

This step is used to address any remaining warnings in the system. System warnings are designed to highlight issues within the system that are preventing it from running optimally and should be rectified as soon as possible.
page and fix any warnings listed there.
Commissioning is now complete. Test the system to confirm that it behaves as the client requires.
Ensure there are no Warnings in the top bar and if there are, go to

button below will mark the commissioning checklist as complete and remove the checklist shortcut from the title bar.

Review events can be filtered to make it easier to find particular events.
There are 3 ways to filter review events these are time, message or category based. These 3 filters can be used individually or combined for advanced searching.
filter is used to specify a date and time in the past that will filter out any events that occurred before that time. This can be used to display the last 7 days of review. The
filter is used to specify a date and time that will filter out any events that occurred after that time. Specifying a
filter in the past will prevent live review from being added. These options are commonly used together to display only the review events that occurred at a specific time range.

- The search field will filter the currently displayed review events based on any text entered. This text will be searched across the Message, Who, What and Where columns. The text does not have to appear in a particular order, with each word separated by a space being searched for in a row. The results will display all the Review events that contain every entered word in the search field between the fields. This is commonly used to find specific events such as a user called Mary controlling the Foyer area. If the text
was entered into the search field then only the review events that contain both words would be displayed.

- Each review event in the Inception system falls into a category which can be filtered upon to narrow down the types of message shown. These categories are Security, Access, Hardware, System and Audit. Multiple categories can also be included in the filter at once. To set whether a category is included in the filter, click or tap the category to toggle whether it is enabled or not. By default the Review Events page only displays the Security, Access and System message categories.

The above filters can be combined together to offer a flexible way to filter events. When one or more filters are used, any events that don't match the filter will be hidden and information about the number of shown events vs the total number of events will be updated, for example,
options are affected by the filtering of events.

After the command has been sent, the reader will be disconnected and then promptly reconnected to the module under the new address as long as it is free. This can be confirmed either through review messages showing the disconnect and reconnect upon the command being sent, the reader name change, or this same window listing the new address when opened again.
